title: Trailblazing Arab Women
description: This course investigates Arab women through fourteen women pioneers in fields such as literature; economics; and  law and human rights, while building an understanding of the complexities of the various Arab societies’ expectations of women. It provides students with the legal context that govern those expectations and explores gender relations while highlighting their tumultuous journeys.

title: Arab Culture and Civilization
description: This course teaches students about everyday life in the Arabic-speaking MENA region (Middle East and North Africa).  It focuses both on the diversity of the area,  as well as on the common cultural characteristics and points of reference broadly shared by Arabic speakers.  The course also emphasizes comparisons between the cultural phenomena in the MENA region and student's own societies.

title: Business Culture in the Middle East
description: This course introduces the cultural aspects of business in the Middle East, focusing on the economic, institutional, historical, religious, and cultural factors shaping business practices. Topics include Islam's influence on commerce, business etiquette, political and social norms, and communication with men and women in business. The course is taught online in an asynchronous format, with no prerequisites, and is entirely in English.

title: COOPERATIVE EDUCATION WORK EXPERIENCE
description: Course Description not on file

title: Introduction to Financial Accounting
description: This course offers an introduction to financial accounting concepts, placing emphasis on financial statements and how they reflect business transactions. Please note, Accounting Majors must earn at least a "B" in this course to proceed to required 3000 level accounting courses.

title: Introduction to Managerial Accounting
description: This course offers an introduction to managerial accounting concepts. Please note, Accounting majors must earn at least a "B" in this course to proceed to required 3000 level accounting courses.

title: Financial Accounting and Reporting I
description: This course offers an in-depth study of financial-reporting concepts and generally accepted practice, including an overview of the accounting-cycle assets and noncurrent assets. Emphasis is placed on analyzing financial events and the consequences of financial-reporting alternatives.

title: INTERMEDIATE FINANCIAL ACCOUNTING I
description: REVIEW OF THE ACCUNTING CYCLE AND FINANCIAL STATEMENT PREPARATION. ACCOUNTING THEORY AND PRACTICE FOR CURRENT ASSETS;CURR-                 ENT LIABILITIES AND FIXED ASSETS.

title: Financial Accounting and Reporting II
description: This course offers an in-depth study of financial-reporting concepts and generally accepted practice for long-term liabilities, leases, pensions, income taxes, and stockholder equity and earnings per share. Emphasis is placed on analyzing financial events and on the consequences of financial-reporting alternatives on financial statements.

title: INTERMEDIATE FINANCIAL ACCOUNTING II
description: ACCOUNTING THEORY AND PRACTICE FOR LONG-TERM COMMITMENTS;IN- COME TAXES;STOCKHOLDERS EQUITY;EARNING PER SHARE;AND INFLAT-                  ION ADJUSTMENTS. PREPARATION OF THE STATEMENT OF FINAN.CHANGE

title: Analysis of Financial Statement Presentation
description: This course is intended to provide students with the tools needed to evaluate the content of financial statements and accompanying disclosures. This is achieved by developing an understanding of generally accepted accounting principles (GAAP) and their application.

title: COST ACCOUNTING AND ANALYSIS FOR BUSINESS
description: STUDY OF TECHNIQUES OF COST ACCOUNTING AND COST ANALYSIS FOR VARIOUS BUSINESS DECISIONS.  CREDIT NOT ALLOWED FOR ACCOUNTING MAJORS.

title: Cost Accounting and Analysis for Business Decisions
description: This course studies techniques of cost accounting and cost analysis for various business decisions. Credit is not allowed for accounting majors.

title: Cost Accounting
description: This course covers the planning and control of economic entities through cost-volume-profit relationships, job order, as well as process and standard cost accounting. Emphasis is placed on the relationship between accounting systems and decision making.

title: Cost Accounting II
description: This course covers the planning and control of economic entities through direct and relevant costing, inventory management, decentralized operations, capital budgeting, and quantitative techniques. Emphasis is placed on the relationship between accounting systems and decision making.

title: COST ACCOUNTING
description: PLANNING AND CONTROL OF ECONOMIC ENTITIES THROUGH COST-VOLUMEPROFIT RELATIONSHIPS;JOB ORDER;PROCESS AND STANDARD COST ACCOUNTING;RELATIONSHIP OF ACCOUNTING SYSTEMS TO DECISION-MAKING.

title: Experiential Learning
description: This non-credit, experiential learning course offers students an opportunity to gain "real world" on-the-job work experience related to a specific academic field of study. Students must register for this course through the FSU Career Center.

title: Financial Accounting and Reporting III
description: This course offers an in-depth study of financial reporting concepts and generally accepted practice for investments, business combinations, consolidated enterprises, foreign operations, and the statement of cash flows. Emphasis is placed on analyzing financial events and the consequences of financial reporting alternatives.

title: ADVANCED ACCOUNTING
description: ACTG FOR PARTNERSHIPS/GOVERNMENTAL & NOT-FOR-PROFIT ORGANIZA-TIONS/BUSINESS COMBINATIONS/CONSOLIDATED & COMBINED STATE-   MENTS/FOREIGN OPERATIONS AND SEGMENTS.

title: ADVANCED TOPICS IN COST ACCOUNTING
description: STUDY OF ADVANCED TOPICS IN COST/MANAGERIAL ACCOUNTING;      INCLUDING COST ALLOCATION;TRANSFER PRICING INVENTORY CONTROL AND DECISION MAKING UNCERTAINTY.

title: Accounting Information Systems
description: This course is an introduction to manual and computerized accounting information systems. Transaction cycles, internal controls, and flowcharting are emphasized.

title: Accounting for Governmental and Not-for-Profit Entities
description: This course is an introduction to fund accounting procedures and financial reporting requirements for governmental units and not-for-profit entities.

title: Auditing Theory for Non-Majors
description: This course introduces the student to fundamental concepts of auditing and explains the various stages of the audit process. The course familiarizes the student with such concepts as audit standards and auditor responsibilities, audit risk, materiality, and audit evidence. How to audit specific business cycles and accounts are identified and how to prepare audit reports are covered. Internal control concepts are also introduced to the student.

title: Auditing Theory and Application I
description: This course covers legal and professional responsibility of CPAs; generally accepted auditing standards; audit programs, procedures, and evidence; review and evaluation of internal controls.

title: Auditing Theory and Application II
description: This course covers theory of auditing and development of audit programs; sampling; procedures of obtaining audit evidence; auditor responsibility under Securities and Exchange Commission requirements; and auditing computerized systems. Subsequent credit for ACG 5635 is not permitted.

title: Internal Auditing
description: A comprehensive study of basic concepts, philosophy, standards, and practices of internal auditing. The course offers the student an overall understanding and appreciation of the role of internal auditing in an organization.

title: Investigative Accounting
description: This course provides an introduction to issues in forensic accounting. Topics include criminal statutes related to financial crimes, techniques used in solving financial crimes, rules of evidence, interviewing techniques, and forensic accounting procedures.

title: Fraud Examination
description: This course provides an introduction to the field of fraud examination. Topics include the nature of fraud, who commits it and why, deterring fraud, financial statement fraud, and fraud resolution.

title: Directed Individual Study
description: May be repeated to a maximum of five semester hours.

title: Special Topics in Accounting
description: This course content varies to provide an opportunity to study current issues in accounting and topics not offered in other courses. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve semester hours as content changes.

title: Forensic Accounting and Criminology Capstone
description: This course reviews various aspects of forensic accounting including procedural and substantive legal issues, professional ethics, investigation techniques, management of evidence, and special topics in forensic accounting. Students apply concepts and tools previously learned in prior program courses. This course emphasizes the practical application of those concepts and tools.

title: Accounting Internship
description: This accounting internship is designed for College of Business students who desire to gain real-world experience in the accounting field through on-the-job practice. Students work under the direction of an approved industry professional, a faculty advisor, and the internship director.

title: Honors in the Major Research
description: Six credit hours of thesis are required to complete honors in the major. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve credit hours.

title: Accounting Concepts: Introductory
description: "Introductory concepts of accounting and uses of accounting information by management; emphasis on theory, uses, and limitations of accounting data in business decision making. Cannot be taken for credit to apply to the Master of Accounting degree. "

title: Accounting Concepts: Introductory
description: "Introductory concepts of accounting and uses of accounting information by management; emphasis on theory, uses, and limitations of accounting data in business decision making. Cannot be taken for credit to apply to the Master of Accounting degree. "

title: Financial Reporting and Managerial Control
description: This course provides a basic understanding of accounting systems and financial statements as a foundation for analysis. The course also addresses cost systems and controls as they pertain to organizational control. Cannot be taken for credit for the Master of Accounting degree.

title: Fundamentals of Accounting and Finance
description: This course is an introduction to accounting for non-College of Business majors. Course topics include financial accounting, tax accounting, managerial or cost accounting, auditing and corporate finance. Cannot be applied for credit for any graduate business degree.

title: Intermediate Accounting I
description: This course offers an in-depth study of financial-reporting concepts and generally accepted practice, including an overview of the accounting-cycle assets and noncurrent assets. Emphasis is placed on analyzing financial events and the consequences of financial-reporting alternatives.

title: Financial Accounting Theory and Standard Setting
description: This course is an introduction to the development of financial accounting theory, the relationship of accounting theory and research to standard setting, and discussion of the current standard setting environment.

title: SEMINAR IN ACCOUNTING THEORY
description: ACCOUNTING CONCEPTS OF ASSET AND INCOME MEASUREMENT; ACCOUNT-ING THEORY DETERMINATION

title: Financial Statement Analysis
description: This course teaches students to compare profitability and risk of business entities; aid in assessment of the efficiency of a business entity's operations and success of its strategies; and help predict a business entity's future profitability and financial health.

title: Advanced Accounting
description: This course provides a study of accounting principles and problems related to advanced accounting topics.

title: Accounting Concepts for Managerial Control
description: This course explores the controllership function in relation to the responsibilities of management and places emphasis on the measurement and control of unit costs and special decisions of management. Cannot be taken for credit toward the Master of Accounting (MAcc) degree.

title: Accounting Concepts for Managerial Control
description: This course explores the controllership function in relation to the responsibilities of management and places emphasis on the measurement and control of unit costs and special decisions of management. Cannot be taken for credit toward the Master of Accounting (MAcc) degree.

title: Advanced Management Accounting
description: This course is a study of current advanced topics in management accounting.

title: SEMINAR IN MANAGEMENT ACCOUNTING II
description: RESPONSIBILITIES OF THE MODERN CONTROLLER AND HIS RELATION TO MANAGEMENT;USE OF ACCOUNTING AND STATISTICAL INFORMATION IN PLANNING AND CONTROLLING OPERATIONS.                                         0

title: Advanced Accounting Information Systems
description: This course explores the design and operation of accounting systems as well as the relevance of data processing and statistical methods to the system of financial information and control.

title: Emerging Technologies in Accounting and Auditing
description: This course is designed for Master of Accounting students with either an assurance services major or an accounting information systems major. The course furnishes students with knowledge and skills to account for and to audit firms that are using emerging technologies. It provides students with tools to identify and assess the risks of insecure electronic commerce systems and to formulate security-conscious solutions.

title: Enterprise Systems and Accounting
description: This course is designed for Master of Accounting students who are specializing in accounting information systems, assurance services, or corporate accounting. The course furnishes students knowledge and skills to implement, use, and audit enterprise-wide information systems. Students are expected to enter the course with an understanding of databases, as the database is the most crucial component of an enterprise-wide information system.

title: Government and Not-for-Profit Accounting and Auditing
description: This course is an introduction to financial reporting and auditing requirements for government and not-for-profit entities.

title: Forensic Accounting
description: This course provides in-depth exposure to the forensic accounting process and related audit topics, including identification of fraud risk factors and development of skills in detecting fraud.

title: Challenges in Professional Accounting
description: This course examines case studies emphasizing elements of public practice, standards of professional conduct, fraud issues, systematic controls, auditing principles and standards, and communication of findings.

title: Special Studies in Management
description: May be repeated to a maximum of three credit hours.

title: Supervised Research
description: May be repeated to a maximum of five credit hours; may be repeated within the same term.

title: Supervised Teaching
description: A maximum of three credit hours may apply towards the master's degree. May be repeated to a maximum of five credit hours; may be repeated within the same term.

title: Thesis
description: This course may be reapeated to a maximum of six semester hours. S/U grade only.

title: Seminar in Financial and Auditing Research
description: This course offers an introduction to the academic literature in financial accounting and auditing research.

title: Seminar in Behavioral Accounting Research
description: This course is a survey of economic-based and psychology-based experimentalresearch as it relates to accounting and auditing.

title: SEMINAR IN ANALYTICAL RESEARCH
description: A SURVEY OF ANALYTICAL MODELS IN FINANCIAL AND COST/MANAGE-  RIAL ACCOUNTING RESEARCH.

title: Seminar in Analytical Research
description: A survey of analytical models in financial and cost/managerial accounting research.

title: Introduction to Accounting Research
description: This course offers a survey of subject areas studied and research methods used in accounting.

title: SEMINAR IN CAPITAL MARKET BASED ACCOUNTING RESEARCH
description: A SURVEY OF ACCOUNTING RESEARCH USING A MARKET-BASED RESEARCH METHODOLOGY.

title: Seminar in Capital Market-Based Accounting Research
description: This course offers a review and analysis of extant accounting research in the capital markets area.

title: Seminar in Accounting
description: This course covers research methodologies useful in developing and evaluating accounting theories and principles.Behavioral accounting research and empirical financial accounting research are introduced. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve credit hours; may be repeated within the same term.

title: Dissertation
description: A minimum of twenty-four (24) credit hours in required.

title: Doctoral Preliminary Examination
description: This preliminary examination determines if students have mastered the content area of accounting and are prepared to plan and conduct independent and scholarly research. Upon successful completion of the preliminary examination, students are admitted to candidacy and may begin taking dissertation hours.

title: Masters Comprehensive Examination
description: P/F grade only.

description: P/F grade only.

title: Special Topics in Adult and Community Education
description: This course introduces varying topics related to the nature and methods of adult and community education. May be repeated for a maximum of twelve semester hours.

title: Comparative and International Adult Education
description: This course covers the nature of adult education programs in other societies and of international trends in the field of adult education. Emphasis is placed on the comparative analysis of the role and structure of adult education and of the relation of programs to their cultural and socio-political contexts.

title: University Continuing Education
description: This course explores the design and implementation of continuing education.

title: Foundations of Adult and Continuing Education
description: This course explores the social, historical, and philosophical foundations of adult and continuing education as a field of study and professional practice.

title: Human Resource Development
description: This course offers a comprehensive survey of the structure and function of human-resource development in organizations. Focus is on alternative perspectives, professional roles and competencies, as well as on the organizational features affecting Human-Resource Development operations and programs.

title: Analytical Applications in Human Resource Management, Training, and Development
description: This course focuses on case studies and the analytical skills necessary to evaluate the need for and effectiveness of human-resource management, training and development interventions in business and industry, public and community agencies, and not-for-profit organizations. Students should have a basic understanding of statistics to use for calculating efficiency, effectiveness, and return-on-investment.

title: UNIVERSITY CONTINUING EDUCATION
description: EMPHASIS IS ON THE DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION OF CONTINUING EDUCATION.                                                                                                                                  0

title: Program Leadership Development
description: This course covers the principles and theory of program development and appraisal as they apply to selected adult-education enterprises.

title: Staff Training and Development
description: This course covers the theory and practice of training and staff development based on the design and use of experiential instructional interventions meant to enhance individual, group, and organizational efforts. This course introduces students to the key concepts, principles, and processes that drive the staff training and development function and to enable them to improve their related analytical and technical skills.

title: Education and Training in Gerontology
description: This course covers the principles, theory, and practice relative to the development of education and training in gerontology.

title: EDUCATIONAL PROGRAMS FOR THE DISADVANTAGED ADULT
description: EXPLORATION OF EDUCATIONAL NEEDS PROGRAMS AND SERVICES FOR   DISAVANTAGED ADULTS. REPEATABLE 9 HOURS MAXIMUM.                                                                                          0

title: Problems in the Organization and Administration of Adult Education Agencies
description: This course covers general administrative processes and analyses the major purposes of adult education.

title: Processes of Community and Adult Education
description: This course explores the processes of planned change through formal and informal adult and continuing education programs in a variety of community and agency contexts.

title: Adult Learning
description: This course offers a critical examination of the major problems in adult learning. Emphasis is placed on the psychological factors affecting learning ability, achievement, and motivation throughout the adult life-cycle.

description: A STUDY OF THE HISTORY,PROGRAMS,INSTITUTIONAL FORMS,AND CROSS-CULTURAL INFLUENCES OF ADULT EDUCATION IN OTHER COUNTRIES.                                                                               0

description: ASSESSMENT OF PROCESSES THROUGH WHICH EDUCATIONAL PROGRAMS   FOR ADULTS ARE DEVELOPED IN FORMAL/NON-FORMAL EDUCATIONAL                     SETTINGS.

title: E-Learning for Managers
description: This course provides managers in all types of organizations with the essential principles and practices impacting the e-learning revolution in business and government. Students explore the convergence of e-learning with knowledge management and learning systems, learning-content management systems, and performance-support systems.

title: Issues in Adult and Continuing Education
description: This course explores and analyses philosophical, theoretical, and practical issues which shape research and practice in adult and continuing education.

title: Strategies for Participatory Research, Planning, and Evaluation
description: This course explores strategies for participatory research, planning, and evaluartion.

description: This course may be repeated to a maximum of five semster hours. A maximum of three hours may apply to the master's degree. S/U grade only.

title: Special Topics in Adult Education
description: This course covers various current issues and topics otherwise not covered in other courses in the field, such as leadership in adult-education enterprises, participatory planning research, and the foundations and evaluation of human-resource development policies. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve semester hours.

title: Internship in Continuing Education
description: This internship offers a directed practicum to develop administrative and programming competencies by translating theory into practice, testing principles, and evaluating actions. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve semester hours. S/U grade only.

title: Supervised Teaching
description: This supervised-teaching course provides graduate students with the opportunity to engage in experimental teaching situations, under the guidance of afaculty member. A maximum of three hours may apply toward a master's degree. May be repeated to a maximum of five semester hours. S/U grade only.

description: This course offers a critical analysis of the research literature pertaining to the general field of adult education. May be repeated to a maximum of two (2) semester hours. S/U grade only.

title: Adult Education Colloquium
description: This course offers lectures and discussion by distinguished educators, social scientists, graduate faculty, and students. May be repeated to a maximumof three semester hours. S/U grade only.

title: RESEARCH SEMINAR IN ADULT EDUCATION
description: CRITICAL ANALYSIS OF RESEARCH LITERATURE PERTAINING TO THE   GENERAL FIELD OF ADULT EDUCATION.                                             REPEATABLE 2 TIMES MAXIMUM.

title: Advertising Strategy
description: This foundation course in advertising explores creativity in a workshop environment.

title: Advertising Strategy
description: This foundation course in advertising explores creativity in a workshop environment.

title: Principles of Advertising
description: This course explores advertising and promotion as related to level of economic growth, cultural influences, and sociolegal environments.

title: Principles of Advertising
description: This course explores advertising and promotion as related to level of economic growth, cultural influences, and sociolegal environments.

title: Mass Media Law
description: This course offers a comprehensive review of laws, rules, and regulations affecting both the advertising and broadcast industries as well as other forms of mass media. Topics include libel, slander, invasion of privacy, gathering of information, and copyright laws.

title: Hispanic Marketing Communication
description: This course prepares undergraduate students to become educated decision makers and consumers of information regarding U.S. Hispanic marketing communication issues.

title: Advertising Team I
description: This course is application-based and provides students with the opportunity to develop a complete Integrated Marketing Communication campaign plan as part of the National Student Advertising Competition sponsored by the American Advertising Federation. The course is set up as hierarchy-based advertising agency with some students in leadership positions and others working in departments that are managed by student directors. May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Advertising Team II
description: This course is the second of a two course sequence. The course focuses on campaign execution. The advertising team course is an application-based class, which provides students with the opportunity to develop a complete Integrated Marketing Communication campaign plan as part of the National Student Advertising Competition sponsored by the American Advertising Federation. The class is set up as hierarchy based advertising agency with some students in leadership positions and others working in departments that are managed by student directors. May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours.

title: CREATIVE STRATEGY II
description: CREATIVE AND EMPHATIC SKILLS NECESSARY IN COMMUNICATING VIA PRINT AND ELECTRONIC MEDIA; UTILIZING THESE SKILLS IN CREATING                 INTEGRATED ADVERTISING CAMPAIGNS.                           0

title: Advertising Copywriting
description: This writing course is designed for advertising majors. The course explores different types of ad writing and allows students to work on exercises that reinforce styles, grammar, and conciseness.

title: Media Planning
description: This course explores the coordination of advertising and marketing research, planning, creative strategy, and selection of media and production activities leading to the development of advertising campaigns.

title: Multicultural Marketing Communication
description: This course is geared to train students to become effective communicators and marketers when reaching out to multicultural society. Marketers, communicators, and service providers interested in being effective in reaching out to culturally diverse groups need to become adept at designing messages and strategies geared to a culturally diverse society.

title: International Strategic Communication: Multicultural Approaches
description: This course is designed to help students learn the concepts and tools of global marketing, including the various and differing cultural, economic, and political factors that impact international advertising and public relations. In other words, this course focuses on understanding how international advertising continues to be developed under differing economic, cultural, regulatory, and competitive conditions.

title: Advertising Research
description: This course covers survey, observational, and experimental methods and processes. Topics include research design, planning, questionnaire construction, sampling, validity measurements, field work, tabulations, presentation, and interpretation.

title: Account Planning
description: This course teaches students how to become resourceful, creative, and strategic to represent the consumer's voice in planning a marketing communication campaign. Thus, this course emphasizes applied research in consumer insights building, brief creative writing, debriefing, and strategic planning.

title: Advertising Campaigns
description: This course fosters creative and empathetic skills necessary in communicating via print and electronic media and enables students to utilize these skills in creating integrated advertising campaigns.

title: Advertising Campaigns
description: This course fosters creative and empathetic skills necessary in communicating via print and electronic media and enables students to utilize these skills in creating integrated advertising campaigns.

title: Foundations of Integrated Marketing Communcations
description: This course covers the development of Integrated Marketing Communication that has now become part of business models in many corporations and service organizations, as well as universities.

title: Media Consumer Behavior
description: This course explores the research and analysis of consumer behavior.

title: Media Consumer Behavior
description: This course explores the research and analysis of consumer behavior.

title: Media Market Research
description: This course explores the measurement of electronic media audiences, with emphasis on broadcast/cable ratings. The course also offers a review of quantitative and qualitative methods used in conducting research in the field of electronic media and entertainment technologies.

title: Communication Career Futures
description: This course is directed to Communication graduate students who intend to pursue applied, non-academic careers upon completion of their degree. The course assists students in setting up job-search strategies, preparing documentation for seeking employment, developing job-related oral communication field skills, and understanding career opportunities in the communication field.

title: Diversity and Justice
description: This course integrates African authors, pre- and post-Apartheid, to demonstrate the problems of living in a diverse world. It fosters awareness and acceptance of people different from students through the study of the African-American culture, and stimulates an appreciation and respect for people of all cultures.

title: Introduction to the African-American Experience
description: This course is an interdisciplinary examination of African-American culture and socio-economic status. This course also explores elements of the African Diaspora.

title: INTRODUCTION TO THE AFRO-AMERICAN EXPERIENCE
description: INTERDISCIPLINARY EXAMINATION OF FORM/CONTENT AFRO-AMER CULTURE AS EXRESSION OF ITS AFRICAN HERITAGE & RESPONSE TO ECONOMIC;SOCIAL & POLITICAL OPPRESSION IN THE NEW WORLD.

title: Theory of African American Studies
description: This course engages theories of race discrimination and oppression as it relates to African Americans. Students systematically and objectively examine the sources of American oppression and explore how it shapes the life chances of African Americans from prior to the Reconstruction Era to the twenty-first century. The course explores the timing and manner of their entry into U.S. society, conflicts with other groups, encounters with prejudice and discrimination, as well as the extent to which they have secured access to cultural, economic, political, and social assimilation into U.S. society.

title: Black Families in America
description: This course explores the social, economic, and cultural forces that have shaped the development of African-American families. In examining historical and contemporary transitions in the structure and functioning of African-American families, special emphasis is given to the bifurcation in the distribution of wealth and power in American society, as well as the role of racial stratification. The course also seeks to empirically examine contemporary policy and political debates on crucial issues confronting African-American families.

title: Race, Class, and Gender Inequities in the United States
description: This course examines the ways in which race, class, gender and sexuality influence, structure, and shape our public and private lives. Throughout the course, students consider the relationship of these social categories and explore their roots in the historical legacies of power, privilege, and oppression.

title: Psychology of Hip Hop
description: This course uses hip hop music and culture as conceptual lenses for analyzing and interpreting the life experiences of people of African descent throughout the African diaspora. Drawing mainly on psychology as well as other social sciences, this course is intended to provide students with an understanding of the psycho-historical and psycho-social development of African Americans relatively.

title: Cultural Pluralism and Community Service
description: "This course presents students a theoretical framework for evaluating effective community service strategies, especially in response to the needs of African-American clients."

title: Politics of Black Poverty: Issues of Race, Class, and Space
description: This course examines the African American poverty in the U.S. with the goal of understanding how historical conditions and events shaped current circumstances. Students learn about the strengths and weaknesses of antipoverty interventions in addition to some of the unintended consequences of these policies.

title: Politics of Black Poverty: Issues of Race, Class, and Space
description: This course examines the African American poverty in the U.S. with the goal of understanding how historical conditions and events shaped current circumstances. Students learn about the strengths and weaknesses of antipoverty interventions in addition to some of the unintended consequences of these policies.

title: Special Topics
description: This course varies with instructor and semester. May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours.

title: Black Political Thought & Social Movement
description: This course is a critical introduction to Africana political thought and social movements with an emphasis on the African-American experience. The course is a comprehensive exploration of Africana political history, ideology and political practice.

title: African Diaspora
description: This course examines the experiences of Afro-descendant populations around the world in the wake of the trans-Atlantic slave trade. The course focuses on the ways in which Afro-descendant peoples outside of Africa understand and negotiate the lives and the global effects of Blackness.

title: Culture, Land and Ecology: A Seminar in Black Environmental History and Politics
description: This course explores the ways that communities of African descent have understood and related to the earth. Specific emphasis is placed on how this understanding and relationship has changed over time due to the socio-historical forces of westernization, capitalism, slavery, colonialism, industrialization and urbanization.

title: Research Methods and Scholarly Writing in Africana Studies
description: This course introduces students to a general conceptual framework for ordering the social theories and methods to understand Africana life experiences. Students engage in an interdisciplinary examination of areas of critical inquiry pertaining to the diversity and complexity of Africana experiences using social science methods (e.g., interviewing, ethnography, and survey research).

title: African-American Studies Directed Individual Study
description: Course topics vary by each student. May be repeated to a maximum of nine (9) credit hours; repeatable within the same term.

title: African American Studies Internship
description: This course builds on the African American Studies curriculum to provide a solid foundation for subsequent applied work in this interdisciplinary field. As students near the completion of formal course work, an internship in the field affords the opportunity to put what was learned to practical use in applied settings, and to develop professional skills and competencies. May be repeated to a maximum of six (6) credit hours.

title: African History and Civilization
description: This introductory course for African history and civilization covers the broad sweep of African history and culture. The primary emphasis is to understand the background to some of Africa's major problems and possibilities today. The course does not count as credit toward the history major.

title: NORTH AFRICAN HISTORY:A SURVEY
description: THE COURSE WILL CONCENTRATE ON THE MODERN HISTORY OF NORTH   AFRICA INCLUDING THE MAGHRIB;LIBYA;EGYPT;THE SUDAN;ETHIO-                     PIA AND SOMALIA.                                            0

title: Sub-Sahara Africa Since 1800
description: A history of Sub-Sahara Africa since 1800 is intended to provide an understanding of the background and problems of some of the African states today.

title: North African History: A Survey
description: This course concentrates on the modern history of North Africa including: Maghrib, Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia, Libya, Egypt, Sudan, Ethiopia and Somalia. It is intended to provide an understanding of the background and problems of North African states today.

title: Northern African History
description: This course concentrates on the modern history of North Africa including: The Maghreb, Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia, Libya, Egypt, Sudan, Ethiopia and Somalia. It is intended to provide an understanding of the background and challenges facing North African countries today.

title: Foundations of the Air Force I
description: This survey course briefly covers topics relating to the Air Force and defense. It focuses on the structure and missions of Air Force organizations, officership and professionalism.  Additionally, the course is a good introduction into the use of communication skills.

title: Foundations of the Air Force II
description: This course is a continuation of AFR 1101.

title: The Evolution of Aerospace Studies I
description: This course is a survey course concerned with the beginnings of manned flight and the development of aerospace power in the United States, including the employment of air power in WWI, WWII, Korea, Vietnam, the Gulf War and the peaceful employment of U.S. air power in civic actions, scientific missions and support of space exploration.

title: Evolution of Aerospace Studies II
description: This course is a continuation of AFR 2130.

title: AFROTC Leadership Lab
description: This course is a weekly laboratory that touches on the topics of Air Force customs and courtesies, health and physical fitness, drill and ceremonies. It also provides the opportunity to develop fundamental management skills while planning and conducting cadet activities.

title: Basic Aerospace Internship
description: This internship provides AFROTC field training.

title: Leadership Studies I
description: This course is a study in the anatomy of leadership, the need for quality and management leadership, the role of discipline in leadership situations and the variables affecting leadership. Case studies are used to examine Air Force leadership and management situations as a means of demonstrating and exercising practical application of the concepts. Students deal with actual problems and complete projects associated with planning and managing the Leadership Laboratory.

title: Leadership Studies II
description: This course is a continuation of AFR 3201.

title: GROUND FLIGHT TRAINING
description: ASPECTS OF GROUND INSTRUCTION FOR STUDENT TO PERFORM SOLO    FLIGHT IN LIGHT AIRCRAFT.SUBJECTS COVERED:FED AVIATION REGS;                PRINCIPLES OF FLIGHT;RADIO COMM;METEOROLOGY;PRE FLIGHT PLANNING

title: National Security Studies and Preparation for Active Duty I
description: This course examines the role of the professional military leader in a democratic society; societal attitudes toward the armed forces; the requisites for maintaining adequate national defense structure; the impact of technological and international developments on strategic preparedness and the overall policy-making process; and military law. In addition, students study topics that prepare them for their first active-duty assignment as an officer in the Air Force.

title: National Security Studies and Preparation for Active Duty II
description: This course is a continuation of AFR 4211.

title: THE AMERICAN INDIANS AND THE UNITED STATES
description: SURVEY OF AMERICAN INDIAN RELATIONS W/PEOPLE & GOVERNMENT OF HTE U.S., BEGINNING IN THE 1760S TO PRESENT.EXAMINES THE INDIANS DIPLOMATIC & MILITARY STRUGGLES TO RETAIN TERRITORIES, ETC.

title: The History of the United States to 1877
description: In this course, students examine United States history from before European contact to 1877. Topics include but are not limited to indigenous peoples, the European background, the colonial period, the American Revolution, the Articles of Confederation, the Constitution, issues within the new republic, sectionalism, manifest destiny, slavery, the American Civil War, and Reconstruction.

title: A History of the United States Since 1877
description: In this course, students will trace the history of the United States from the end of the Reconstruction era to the contemporary era. Topics will include but are not limited to the rise of industrialization, the United States' emergence as an actor on the world stage, constitutional amendments and their impact, the Progressive Era, World War I, the Great Depression and New Deal, World War II, the Civil Rights era, the Cold War, and the United States since 1989.

title: Civil War in the American Mind
description: "An exploration of myth, image and rhetoric of the Civil War as they have appeared in American culture since 1865. Course will not count as credit toward the history major."

title: American Biography
description: An exploration of issues in American history from the perspectives of the men and women who dealt with aspects of those issues firsthand. The biographical figures vary from semester to semester. May be repeated to a maximum of six (6) semester hours with instructor's permission. Course will not count as credit toward the history major.

title: Survey of African American History in the United States
description: This course introduces students to a broad sweep of the history of African Americans in the United States from the African slave trade to present day. This course contributes to a student's broad foundational knowledge on the historical background and philosophical foundation of the development of the United States of America.

title: Introduction to Native American History
description: This course provides an overview of the major social, cultural, and political trends in the Native American history in North America. This course contributes to a student's broad foundational knowledge on the historical background and philosophical foundation of the development of the United States of America.

title: Black Women in America
description: This course examines (chronologically and thematically) the unique experience of the African American woman in the United States and the role they have played in shaping this nation's history.  Particular attention is paid to the double burden that black women have experienced because of their race and gender. This course does not count as credit toward the history major.

title: The History of Immigration to the United States
description: This course surveys the economic, political, and social issues that shaped immigration to the United States from the seventeenth century to the present. This course contributes to a student's broad foundational knowledge on the historical background and philosophical foundation of the development of the United States of America.

title: The Seminoles and the Southeastern Indians
description: This course explores the history of the Seminoles and other Southeastern Native Americans in the territory that is now known as the American South.  The course covers the pre-contact era to the present with an emphasis on tribal perspectives.

title: HISTORY OF THE UNITED STATES
description: COMPLETE 2 OF THE FOLLOWING:AMH1000;WOH1022;WOH1030;EUH2100  A SURVEY OF UNITED STATES HISTORY WITH EMPHASIS ON SOCIAL;ECONOMIC;AND POLITICAL ISSUES THROUGH THE CIVIL WAR.

title: Evolution of Law, Crime, and Justice in American Society: 1865 to the Present
description: This course serves as an intermediate level survey of the social and political developments in the fields of crime and law from the end of the Civil War to the present. Its focus is on the changing definitions of criminal behavior, regional patterns of crime, the changing nature of punishment and Constitutional issues relating to crime.

title: The United States in the Twenty-First Century
description: This course examines the history of the United States since 2000. It familiarizes students with the historical developments that have shaped their lives.

title: Social History of the United States
description: This course offers an analysis of the day-to-day lives of American people. Topics include morals, manners, religion, family, social class, health, and occupations.

title: The Evolution of Law, Crime, and Justice in American Society, to 1876
description: This course offers an intermediate level survey of social and political developments in the fields of law and criminology, from the colonial era through Reconstruction. The course focuses upon social problems, criminal behavior, punishments, changing definitions of citizenship, and the Constitution.

title: Mass Incarceration and the Politics of Exclusion
description: This course examines the phenomena of policing and punishment in the United States, focusing specifically on the emergence of mass incarceration since the 1960s and 1970s.

title: US Political History to 1877
description: This course covers the colonial and revolutionary background of U.S. politics. Topics cover U.S. political parties and elections from the 1790s to 1877, emphasizing the presidency and the groups and issues that have influenced political parties.

title: US Political History from 1877 to the Present
description: This course studies U.S. political parties and elections from the end of Reconstruction to the present. Special emphasis is placed on the presidency and on the groups and issues that have influenced political parties. AMH 3351 is not a prerequisite for 3352.

title: Economic History of the United States
description: A survey of American economic history from the early colonial beginnings to the present. The course analyzes the role of economic issues and events in American history. Major attention is directed to the 20th century. The course proceeds chronologically and is broken into 12 time periods.

title: Economic History of the United States
description: A survey of American economic history from the early colonial beginnings to the present. The course analyzes the role of economic issues and events in American history. Major attention is directed to the 20th century. The course proceeds chronologically and is broken into 12 time periods.

title: US Business History
description: "This historical survey of American business from colonial times to the present includes manufacturing, industrial development, agribusiness, organized labor, finance, management approaches, and the evolution of business ethics."

title: Energy: A History
description: This course offers a historical perspective on the role that technology has played in modern history. It focuses on the American experience from the Colonial period to the present.

title: History of the Trans-Mississippi American West
description: This course covers the history of the Trans-Mississippi West during the 19th century. Students are expected to develop an understanding of this area as a geographical region and its role in American history beginning with the early 19th century explorations and culminating with the symbolic "closing of the frontier" of the 1890s.

title: The Evolution of Organized Crime
description: This course discusses the evolution of organized crime in the United States, the social and legal factors that contributed to its development, and the ethnic groups involved.

title: Labor History of the United States
description: "The social history of American workers, the labor movement, labor strikes and violence, and working class politics from colonial America to the present."

title: Military History of the United States
description: This course is a survey of both the military experiences and issues in American history. The course analyzes war, its economic issues, technological developments, politics, and other factors that have influenced the military aspects of American history.

title: The United States and Vietnam, 1941-1975
description: This course examines the involvement of the United States in Vietnam from World War II through the fall of Saigon in 1975 and considers the legacy of this experience for American foreign relations and society.

title: AFRO-AMERICAN HISTORY
description: INTRODUCES STUDENTS TO THE METHODS; MATERIALS; AND HIGHLIGHTSOF THE STUDY OF AFRICAN PEOPLES IN WORLD SOCIETY.                                                                                         0

title: Environmental Policy: Twentieth Century and Beyond
description: This course provides an overview of key issues in environmental policy, primarily in the United States but placed with transnational and global contexts, from the start of the twentieth century to the present.

title: Studies in U.S. History
description: This course includes examination of a special topic related to U.S. history. Topics vary. The course may be repeated as topics vary to a maximum of twelve semester hours.

title: Colonial America to 1763
description: This course studies and compares the founding and development of the English colonies in North America.

title: Revolutionary America, 1760-1788
description: This course examines the political, social and economic history of British  America from the end of the Seven Years War to the ratification of the U.S. Constitution. Emphasis is placed on the origins, course, and aftermath of the colonial rebellion that became the American Revolution and led to the founding of the U.S. The course considers the fundamental causes of the Revolution and the ways in which the former colonies were transformed by the experience.

title: THE AGE OF WASHINGTON: 1763-1801                    .
description: TRACES THE COMING/PROGRESS/ AND EFFECTS OF THE AMERICAN       REVOLUTION.                                                                                                                              .

title: Thomas Jefferson's America
description: This course examines the political and cultural history of the United States from the first presidential election through the "Era of Good Feelings." In 1789, the leaders of the new government faced a difficult and confusing task: building working political institutions out of the Constitution's vague instructions and, at the same time, creating a stable, unified nation out of a divided and scattered collection of societies and peoples. The events of this period determined what type of nation the U.S. would become. Emphasis is placed on Thomas Jefferson as a figure who both shaped and represented and shaped this era.

title: The Civil War Era
description: This course offers an in-depth study of the twenty years from 1845 to 1865. Emphasis is placed on the coming of the Civil War, the secession crisis, and on both the military and nonmilitary events of the war years.

title: Post-Civil War America, 1865-1890
description: This course analyses post-Civil War America with emphasis on the Black role in American society and the attempt to heal the wounds of the Civil War. Topics include the rise of big business, labor unions, and the last frontier.

title: US Progressive Era, 1890-1920
description: This course includes a study of the development of domestic and foreign policy, the revolution of social thought, and the paradoxical path of reform in urbanized, industrial America. Emphasis is placed on the nation's effort to accommodate old values with the new realities.

title: The United States, 1920-1945: Prosperity, Depression, and World War II
description: A general course in United States history from 1920 through 1945, i.e., a study of political, economic, diplomatic, military, social, and cultural/intellectual developments during that period.

title: The United States, 1920-1945: Prosperity, Depression, and World War II
description: A general course in United States history from 1920 through 1945, i.e., a study of political, economic, diplomatic, military, social, and cultural/intellectual developments during that period.

title: The United States Since 1945
description: This course focuses on the political and cultural issues faced by the United States during the period of the Cold War (1945 to 1988). Special attention is given to postwar affluence, suburban America, the mass society, the movement from isolationism to interventionism, McCarthyism, the civil rights movement, social conflict in the 1960s, and the rise of postwar conservatism.

title: America in the 1960s
description: This course examines selective aspects of the era known as "the sixties." Spanning two decades, it starts in 1954 with the decision to integrate America's schools as a flash point for the civil rights struggle, and it concludes in 1974 with Richard Nixon's resignation, the final statement in the Watergate affair. During those years of intense and accelerated change, civil rights, black power, the war in Vietnam, radical politics, and the counter culture divided the country so passionately that at times it appeared as though the nation might come apart.

title: US Intellectual History I: Beginning to 1880
description: This course offers an interdisciplinary study of American thought from the Puritans to the late 19th century, asking, among other questions, what mission America assigned to itself. Topics include Puritanism, the Revolutionary ideology, federalism, the American Enlightenment, romanticism, individualism, and manifest destiny.

title: US Intellectual History II: 1880 to the Present
description: This course offers an interdisciplinary study of the impact on American thought of social Darwinism, industrialism, naturalism, the culture of consumption, radicalism, anticommunism, post-industrialism, and affluence. Examines the growth of cultural criticism as a task required of the 20th-century intellectual.

title: The Political History of the South, 1607-1965
description: This course explores developments in Southern political history from 1607 to 1965, focusing on the role of the region in shaping national debates. It examines the South as a place inhabited by diverse groups of people, as a laboratory for ideas about political theories and institutions, and as a set of ideologies and images that still impact American life.

title: The South Since 1865
description: This course views the South both as a distinct region and as an area gradually coming back into "regular" American life after the Civil War. The unique problems of adjusting to defeat, the revolution in the labor system, and troubled race relations are considered.

title: The History of Florida
description: This course explores the history of Florida from its pre-Columbian origins to the present.

title: SPANISH FLORIDA AND THE BORDERLANDS TO 1821
description: DEALS WITH THE GREATER SPANISH LA FLORIDA FROM THE 16TH TO   19TH CENTURIES. TOPICS EMPHASIZED ARE INDIANS; FRONTIER SITUATION, RIVALRIES WITH BRITAIN AND FRANCE, AND INVASION BY JACKSON.

title: History of Florida from 1821 to the Present
description: This course covers the history of Florida from the period of its acquisition from Spain in 1821 until the present. The various "periods" in the state's past are discussed with special attention given to the period 1920 to the present.

title: History of the Frontier to 1865
description: "This course examines the Westward Movement from 1763 until 1865. Social, political, economic, and military aspects of the frontier experience, and the significance of the frontier in American history, are examined."

title: HISTORY OF THE AMERICAN WEST FROM 1865 TO PRESENT
description: THIS COURSE EXAMINES THE AMERICAN WEST IN THE CLOSING YEARS  OF THE FRONTIER;ITS COLONIAL STATUS IN THE EARLY 20TH CENTURY.

title: URBAN AMERICA TO 1879
description: DEVELOPMENT OF AMERICAN CITIES AND THE ATTEMPTS TO DEAL WITH CHANGING URBAN PROBLEMS FROM COLONIAL AMERICA TO APPRX 1879                                                                               .

title: Urban America Since 1879
description: This course explores the development of American cities and the attempts to deal with changing urban problems from 1879 to the present.

title: United States Foreign Relations to 1900
description: "This course examines the ideas, forces, and institutions which shaped American foreign policy in the era when America evolved from an agrarian republic to an industrial empire."

title: Twentieth-Century United States Foreign Relations
description: This course covers the responsibilities of global power and how American foreign policy changed to meet rapidly altering circumstances.

title: U.S. Immigration History
description: This course explores the histories of different immigrant and migrant groups and how they have shaped and been shaped by the United States.

title: War and American Society
description: "This course will explore the impact of war on American diplomatic, economic, political, social, and legal developments and institutions from the American Revolution to the present."

title: American Legal History I
description: This course surveys the history of the U.S. Constitution up to 1800, including the British background, the first state constitutions, the Articles of Confederation, the Constitutional Convention, ratification debates, and first use of the Constitution in the 1790s. It concludes with the first major controversies faced by the founders, issues that the Constitution did not resolve for them easily. The course is not about constitutional interpretation or theories applied by the current Supreme Court.

title: American Legal History II
description: This course surveys the history of both the U.S. Constitution and American law in the 19th-century. Topics include the Marshall Court, slave law and the Dred Scott decision, the impact of the Civil War and Reconstruction on the law, and the effects of industrialization on American law. The course is not about constitutional interpretation or theories applied by the current Supreme Court.

title: Women in 19th-Century America
description: This course examines the experiences of women in 19th century America, focusing upon the ways gender, race, ethnicity, class, religion, and region interacted to shape women's lives. Examines women's family, work, social, and political roles. Also examines women's contributions and quest for equality.

title: Women in Modern America
description: This course examines the experiences and contributions of women in 20th-century America, with particular attention to the forces that served to differentiate the opportunities and roles of women from those of their male peers.

title: Colonial and Revolutionary Era American Women's History
description: This course explores the lives of Native American, African, and European women before they came into contact in America, how that contact altered their patterns of behavior, and how major events in America affected women's lives.

title: Black America to 1877
description: This course begins with the African background of Black Americans and ends with the final curtailment of Reconstruction in 1877. Although some portions of the course are topical, cutting across chronological divisions, there is a general chronological progression from colonial times to the end of Reconstruction.

title: Black America Since 1877
description: This course traces the social, economic, cultural, and political activities of African-Americans from Reconstruction through the Civil Rights Movement.

title: History of the Seminole Indians
description: This course offers an ethnohistory of the Seminole Indians in Florida from prior to their formation, in the eighteenth century, to present. The course focuses on the Indians themselves and their experiences, exposing students to the history of the Seminole's culture, lifestyles, religions, economy, and tribal community.

title: North American Environmental History
description: This course introduces the changing relationships between human beings and the natural world in America through time.

title: The Nature of Florida
description: This course is an online course that applies the methods and approaches of environmental history to Florida and the southeastern United States.

title: The Nature of Florida
description: This course is an online course that applies the methods and approaches of environmental history to Florida and the southeastern United States.

title: Florida Environmental History
description: This course applies the methods and approaches of environmental history to Florida, considering the changing relationships between human beings and the natural world through time. The field explores how nature has helped to shape culture as well as how humans have modified the natural world and transformed the land.

title: Humor and the American Mind
description: This course discusses American intellectual and cultural history from the eighteenth century to the present, through the lens of humor. It investigates the relationship between American ideas and historical transformations. It uses humor to explore the connections and tensions between the various parts of the American mind.

title: Women and Children in the Civil Rights Movement
description: This course examines the role of women and children in the modern day Civil Rights Movement in the United States with the underlying themes of race, class and gender.

title: Colonial American History to 1763
description: This course is a study and comparison of the founding and development of the English colonies in North America.

title: Post- Civil War, 1865-1890
description: This course analyses post-Civil War America with emphasis on the Black role in the American society and on the attempt to heal the wounds of the Civil War. Topics include the rise of big business, labor unions, and the last frontier.

title: The United States Since 1945
description: This course focuses on the political and cultural issues faced by the United States during the period of the Cold War (1945 to 1988). Special attention is given to postwar affluence, suburban America, the mass society, the movement from isolationism to interventionism, McCarthyism, the civil rights movement, social conflict in the 1960s and the rise of postwar conservatism.

title: The Old South
description: This course is a study of the social and economic development of the Southern states from settlement by Europeans to the end of the Civil War, with emphasis placed on the rise of the Cotton Kingdom and the causes of secession.

title: History of Florida From 1821 to the Present
description: This course includes a history of Florida from the period of its acquisition from Spain in 1821 until the present. The various "periods" in the state's past are discussed and major attention is given to the period from 1920 to the present, the period of greatest growth.

title: HISTORY OF THE AMERICAN WEST: 1865 TO THE PRESENT
description: THIS COURSE EXAMINES THE AMERICAN WEST IN THE CLOSING YEARS  OF THE FRONTIER.                                             .

title: American Historiography
description: A study of American historians from Parkman to the present. Treats historians as thinkers who contributed to the larger themes and debates of American intellectual history. An examination of the progression of historical "schools" and their arguments with each other over historical and political assumptions.

title: Technology in America
description: This course examines a historical perspective on the role technology has played in United States history since the arrival of the first European settlers. Students examine the role of technologies and technological systems, how they affected society and culture, and how society and culture affected technological implementation and advancement.

title: American Authors to 1875
description: This course covers important writings by representative American authors from the colonial period through the post-Civil War era. Typically included are Franklin, Irving, Emerson, Thoreau, Poe, Hawthorne, Melville, Whitman, Douglass, and Emily Dickinson.

title: INTRODUCTION TO AFRICAN-AMERICAN LITERATURE
description: SURVEY OF AFRICAN-AMERICAN POETRY, FICTION, AND PROSE FROM THE MID-EIGHTEENTH CENTURY TO THE PRESENT.

title: Introduction to African-American Literature
description: This course offers a survey of the canonical works of African-Americans, typically including Douglass, Chesnutt, Hurston, Wright, Ellison, Baldwin, Morrison, and Walker.

title: AMERICAN AUTHORS SINCE 1875
description: SIGNIFICANT WORKS BY REPRESENTATIVE REALISTS, LITERARY NATURALISTS, MODERNISTS, AND CONTEMPORARY WRITERS.

title: American Authors Since 1875
description: Significant works by representative Realists, Literary Naturalists, Modernists, and contemporary writers. Authors typically covered include Twain, James, Crane, Chopin, Eliot, Hemingway, Frost, Fitzgerald, Faulkner, Wright, Baldwin, Morrison, and O'Connor.

title: THE NINETEENTH CENTURY AMERICAN NOVEL
description: FROM BROWN AND COOPER TO HAWTHORNE;MELVILLE;TWAIN;AND CRANE.

title: THE TWENTIETH CENTURY AMERICAN NOVEL
description: TYPICALLY DREISSER; DOS PASSOS; FITZGERALD; HEMINGWAY; WRIGHTFAULKNER; AND BELLOW

title: AMER MULTI-ETHNIC LITERATURE
description: INTROD. TO BACKGROUNDS, TRADITIONS, THEMES AND TECHNIQUES.   INCL. DUBOIS, WRIGHT, ELLISON, BALDWIN,BAKA,MAMADAY,CASTANEDA

title: LATINO/A LITERATURE IN ENGLISH
description: INTRODUCTION TO LANDMARK LATINO/A WORKS WRITTEN IN ENGLISH.

title: Major Figures in American Literature
description: This course examines selected works of major American writers.

title: Latino/a Literature in English
description: This course offers an introduction to landmark Latino/a works written in English.

title: Asian American Literature
description: This course introduces students to selected works of Asian American literature, focusing on Asian Indian, Pacific Islander, Filipino, Chinese, Japanese, Cambodian, and Vietnamese American writers. Common topics include issues of diaspora, dislocation, and cross-culturality.

title: American Multi-Ethnic Literature
description: "Introduction to cross-cultural literary traditions, looking at historical rationales and interconnections among communities as well as vital differences. "

title: The 19th-Century American Novel
description: This course covers from Brown and Cooper to Hawthorne, Melville, Twain, and Crane.

title: The 20th-Century American Novel
description: This course typically covers Dreiser, Dos Passos, Fitzgerald, Hemingway, Faulkner, Bellow, and Wright.

title: Early American Literature and Culture before 1800
description: This course focuses on varying topics in pre-1800 American literature and  culture, such as exploration and captivity narratives, Native American literature, the Puritan tradition, the enlightenment and revolutionary eras in America, the trans- and circum-Atlantic world, the slave trade, early-American print culture (including the novel), gender studies, and/or selected authors.

title: Literature of the South
description: This course offers a survey from Colonial times to the present, including Byrd, Poe, Simms, Cale, Faulkner, Warren, O'Conner, and others.

title: THE AFRICAN-AMERICAN LITERARY TRADITION
description: AN EXAMINATION OF THE CANONICAL WORKS OF BLACK AMERICANS;    INCLUDING DOUGLASS;CHESNUTT;HURSTON;WRIGHT;ELLISON;                        MORRISON AND WALKER.

title: THE AFRICAN AMERICAN LITERARY TRADITION
description: AN EXAMINATION OF THE CANONICAL WORKS OF BLACK AMERICANS; INCLUDING DOUGLASS; CHESTNUTT; HURSTON; WRIGHT; ELLISON; MORRISON; AND WALKER.

title: The African-American Literary Tradition
description: This course examines selected works by major African-American writers in their social, historical, and cultural contexts.

title: Studies in Ethnic Literature
description: This course is an advanced study offering a survey of a particular ethnic literary tradition and adopting a cultural studies model. May be repeated up to a maximum of twenty-four semester hours.

title: Studies in U.S. Literature to 1875
description: This course takes various approaches to the study of U.S. literature from the colonial period to 1875. May be repeated within the same term to a maximum of twelve semester hours as topics vary.

title: Studies in U.S. Literature Since 1875
description: This course takes various approaches to the study of U.S. literature from 1875 to the present. May be repeated within the same term to a maximum of twelve semester hours as topics vary.

title: Studies in Literature of the American South
description: This course takes various approaches to the study of American southern literature from the colonial period to the present. May be repeated within the same term to a maximum of twelve semester hours as topics vary.

title: Studies in Multi-Ethnic Literature
description: This course offers an intensive study of a particular ethnicity, period, or topic in ethnic literature of the U.S. May be repeated within the same term to a maximum of twelve semester hours as topics vary.

title: Studies in the African-American Literary Tradition
description: This course offers a study of the literary works of African-American writers. May be repeated within the same term to a maximum of twelve semester hours as topics vary.

title: Studies in Latino/a Literature in English
description: This course covers various approaches to the study of Latino/a literature, including the work of Mexican-Americans (Chicano/a), Puerto Rican-Americans, and Cuban-Americans. May be repeated within the same term to a maximum of twelve semester hours as topics vary.

title: Issues in American Civilization: The University
description: This course covers issues in the American civilization and places emphasis on the University. May be repeated to a maximum of two semester hours.

title: Issues in American Civilization: The University
description: This course covers issues in the American civilization and places emphasis on the University. May be repeated to a maximum of two semester hours.

title: President's Seminar in American and Florida Studies
description: Course Description not on file

title: Changing Concepts of the American Character
description: This course is a study of American culture and values. May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours. May be repeated within the same semester.

title: The Liberal Arts Tradition
description: "A survey of the origins, values, and rationale for the liberal arts. Students who receive credit for AMS 1363 are ineligible for enrollment."

title: The Life of the Mind in America
description: This course addresses topics in American intellectual history. May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours. May be repeated within the same semester.

title: Lecture Series in American Problems
description: This course explores American problems. May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours. May be repeated within the same semester.

title: Honors Work
description: May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours.

title: Senior Seminar
description: May be repeated to a maximum of twelve semester hours. May be repeated within the same semester.

title: Internship in an Approved American Studies Field
description: May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours.

title: Seminar in American Culture
description: May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Seminar in American Thought
description: May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours within the same term.

title: Thesis
description: A minimum of six semester hours is required.

title: Proseminar
description: This seminar is designed to acquaint beginning graduate students with the organization of anthropology as a profession and to introduce them to basic bibliographic tools and related skills in anthropology. S/U grade only.

title: Seminar in Geospatial Archaeology
description: This course focuses on the relationships between humans and their environments, between rivers and cities, and between the natural world and the built environment. Using Geographical Information Systems (GIS) as a set of methods, this course provides the theoretical and methodological tools necessary for engaging in 21st-century archaeological research. Emphasis is on geospatial technology for fieldwork and analysis.

title: Looking Below the Surface: Archaeological Prospection and Remote Sensing
description: This course examines remote sensing techniques, including lidar, multispectral imaging, GPR, satellite data, and magnetic gradiometry. Students develop skills in theory, data acquisition, and applications in archaeology through lectures, hands-on labs, and field exercises. Discussions and case studies enhance problem-solving skills. The course highlights applied uses of remote sensing and career opportunities in government, NGOs, private industry, and academia.

title: Seminar in Research Methods
description: This course acquaints students with the elements of scientific research designs as used in anthropology including research designs, consideration of variations for field work and for laboratory/library projects. It also considers the format for the  publication of results. Each of the elements of research design are considered and a variety of readings are utilized to understand the basic elements.

title: Seminar in Archaeological Method and Theory
description: This seminar is an in-depth exploration of current theoretical and methodological topics in American archaeology. The aim is to develop a critical assessment and understanding of underlying principles and assumptions in the field of archaeology.

title: Forager Societies
description: This course focuses on human societies throughout the world that have lived by hunting and gathering wild resources. The course examines specific subsistence strategies of a wide range of hunter-gatherer groups, relative to their technology, social structure, territory, demography and interaction with food producers in both the archaeological record and through ethnography.

title: Seminar in Archaeology
description: In this course, seminar topics vary from semester to semester. Past topics have included paleodemography, quantitative methods, research design, and others. May be repeated within the same semester. Fifteen credit hours maximum.

title: Regional Analysis in Archaeology
description: This is an advanced graduate-level seminar designed to explore archaeological approaches to modeling regional social processes. The course is restricted to graduate students who have had some training in archaeological methods and theory at the graduate level. The course considers theoretical frameworks and methodological approaches to understanding anthropological processes that are best studied with the region as the primary unit of analysis. The course also introduces students to classic and contemporary literature related to regional models in geography, anthropology, and sociology, and assesses how those models have been applied to specific archaeological contexts. Students are required to gain hands-on experience conducting their own analysis of archaeological data at the regional level. May be repeated within the same semester. Fifteen credit hours maximum.

title: Core Seminar in Archaeology
description: This course is designed to guide students to the essential works in archaeology of different parts of the world, whether they are classic readings or cutting-edge research.

title: Archaeobotany
description: This course is an introduction to the study of humans' use of plants during prehistoric and historic times, focusing on techniques to recover, analyze, and interpret plant remains from archaeological sites.

title: Geoarchaeology
description: Designed in a seminar style, this course presents a wide range of pertinent archaeological issues including sedimentology, stratigraphy and soil development and description. Alluvial aeolian, karstic, cave, coastal, and other geological environments are reviewed.

title: Zooarchaeology
description: This course is a practical introduction to the analysis of animal (and some plant) remains from archaeological sites. This course uses lecture, laboratory experience, and readings to introduce the student to the interpretation of subsistence remains and their implications for the reconstruction of prehistoric environments, ritual or ceremonial usage, technological requirements for capture and processing, chronological affiliation, human economics (both prehistoric and historic), and dietary choice.

title: Material Culture
description: "Provides an overview of the methods and techniques of material culture (artifact) analysis. The diversity of material remains, manufacturing techniques, and analytical strategies and results are the primary focus of the class."

title: Advanced Zooarchaeology
description: This seminar is dedicated to learning and understanding data, analysis methods, and common software to assist with analysis and interpretations. This class covers in-depth all aspects of data collection, analysis, reporting and long-term preservation, including: structuring datasets

title: Seminar in Lithic Analysis
description: This seminar-style course introduces students to the technology of flint knapping, analysis of the chipping debris, projectile point and tool typology, and use/wear characteristics.

title: Wetlands Archaeology
description: This course provides an introduction to wet site archaeology, incorporating an overview of wet sites, their geographic distribution, methods of excavation, conservation requirements, and the field's contribution to our understanding of the past.

title: Fundamentals of Underwater Archaeology
description: This course surveys the history, theory, methods, and problems of underwater archaeology, with attention given to the types of investigations and environments in which underwater archaeology is conducted and to the field's particular

title: Underwater Archaeology
