title: THEORY OF COMPUTATION
description: REGULAR EXPRESSIONS/REGULAR,CONTEXT-FREE, CONTEXT SENSITIVE, AND UNRESTRICTED GRAMMARS/FOUNDATIONS OF LANGUAGE THEORY/FINITE AUTOMATA AND LINEAR GRAMMARS/PUSHDOWN AUTOMATA/TURING MACHINES AND UNSOLVBL

title: Top 10 Algorithms
description: This course focuses on a wide-ranging selection of ten of the most influential algorithms in use today: what they are, how they work, and their impact on modern life.

title: Theory of Computation
description: This course is an introduction to the theory of computation, including models of computation such as Turing machines; theory of programming languages, including grammars, parsing, syntax, and semantics.

title: Formal Methods in Software Engineering
description: This course examines formal methods in software analysis and design, including formal specification and verification.

title: THEORY OF AUTOMATA AND FORMAL LANGUAGE
description: FORMAL MODELS OF COMPUTATION; AUTOMATA; FORMAL LANGUAGES; THEINTERRELATIONSHIPS; DECIDABLE AND UNDECIDABLE PROBLEMS.                       *CNS CHANGE TO COT5210*                                     0

title: Theory of Parsing, Translation, and Compiling
description: Introduction to compiling, elements of language theory, syntax-directed translation, lexical analysis, symbol tables, backtrack parsing, precedence parsing, LR(k) parsing, LL(k) parsing, intermediate code generation, code optimization, code generation, error detection and recovery.

title: Theory of Automata and Formal Languages
description: This course examines formal models of computation; automata; formal languages, their relationships, decidable and undecidable problems.

title: COMPLEXITY OF ALGORITHMS                                                      *CNS CHANGE TO COT5410
description: FORMAL METHODS FOR ANALYSIS OF ALGORITHM COMPLEXITY; APPLICA-TION TO SPECIFIC ALGORITHMS; LOWER BOUNDS; ASYMPTOTICALLY    OPTIMAL ALGORITHMS; REDUCIBILITIES; NP COMPLETENESS;OTHER HARD PROBLEM CLASSIF

title: THEORY OF COMPUTABILITY                                                       *CNS CHANGE TO COT5421
description: PRIMITIVE RECURSIVE AND PARTIAL RECURSIVE FUNCTIONS; EQUIVA- LENCE OF MODELS OF COMPUTATION; LIMITS OF COMPUTABILITY; AND                  COMPUTATIONAL COMPLEXITY ISSUES.                            0

title: LOGIC FOR COMPUTER SCIENCE
description: SYNTAX;SEMANTICS;PROOF THEORY PROPOSITIONAL LOGIC/FIRST ORDERLANGUAGES;PRENEX NORMAL FORM;GENTZEN SYSTEMS;RESOLUTION FOR PROPOSITIONAL LOGIC;ELEMENTS OF PROLOG AND PROGRAM VERIFICATION.              0

title: Advanced Algorithms
description: This course covers algorithms, formal proofs of correctness, and time complexity analysis for network flow problems, approximation of NP hard combinatorial optimization problems, parallel algorithms, cache-aware algorithms, randomized algorithms, computational geography, string algorithms, and other topics requiring advanced techniques for proof of correctness or time/space complexity analysis.

title: Complexity of Algorithms
description: This course examines formal methods for the analysis of algorithm complexity, application to specific algorithms, lower bounds, asymptotically optimalalgorithms, Reducibilitys, NP completeness, and other classifications of hard problems.

title: Theory of Computability
description: "Primitive recursive and partial recursive functions; equivalence of models of computation, limitations of computability, computational complexity issues. "

title: Analytic Methods in Computer Science
description: This course teaches computer science students the fundamental discrete mathematics required for serious graduate work in algorithms and theoretical computer science.  It specifically covers topics in recurrent problems, sums, integer functions, elementary number theory, binomial coefficients, special numbers, and generating functions.

title: Logic for Computer Science
description: This course examines syntax, semantics, and proof theory of propositional  logic and first order languages; prenex normal form; Gentzen systems; resolution for propositional logic; elements of PROLOG and program verification.

title: Random Number Generation
description: This course provides a graduate-level examination of all aspects of random number generation as used in simulation; specifically, the course concentrates on pseudorandom number generation and quasi-random number generation theory and practice.

title: Introduction to Comparative Government and Politics
description: This course uses basic social and political science concepts and principles to provide an introductory survey of government institutions and current political parties throughout the world, as well as theories that explain similarities and differences among countries. Students will be equipped to understand alternative electoral systems, parliamentary systems, the causes of political change, democratization, political culture, ideologies, and the development of economic and social policy. The course introduces examples from western democracies and developing countries.

title: INTRODUCTION TO COMPARATIVE GOVERNMENT AND POLITICS
description: BASIC CONCEPTS AND THEORIES ASSOCIATED WITH THE STUDY OF     COMPARATIVE POLITICS  AND POLITICAL SYSTEMS                                                                                               0

title: Politics of Developing Areas
description: This course examines how economic and social conditions affect politics andgovernment in Africa, Asia, Latin America, and/or the Middle East. Typical topics include theories of economic development, cultural influences on politics, religious and ethnic conflict, changing roles of women in the developing world, foreign aid, causes and consequences of poverty, causes of revolution, environmental policies, military regimes, and corruption.

title: COMPARATIVE PARTIES AND VOTING BEHAVIOR
description: POLITICAL PARTIES/ PARTY SYSTEMS/ ELECTIONS/ AND VOTING      BEHAVIOR IN ADVANCED INDUSTRIAL SOCIETIES                                                                                                 0

title: Authoritarian Regimes
description: This course examines various aspects of the politics of authoritarian regimes: political institutions in dictatorships, the relationship between dictatorship and economic development, the role of elections and electoral fraud, the impact of international election monitoring, public support for dictatorship, the impact of traditional and social media, censorship, the extent to which authoritarian regimes are accountable to the public, and power struggles among authoritarian elites.

title: INTRODUCTION TO COMMUNIST POLITICAL SYSTEMS
description: MARXIST THEORY AND POLITICAL STRUCTURES; INSTITUTIONS; AND   PROCESSES  IN THE SOVIET UNION AND OTHER SYSTEMS                                                                                          0

title: EAST EUROPEAN POLITICS
description: POLITICAL INSTITUTIONS;PROCESSES AND POLICIES UNDER THE COM- MUNIST REGIMES IN ALBANIA;BULGARIA;CZECHOSLOVAKIA;E. GERMANY HUNGARY;POLAND;ROMANIA;AND YUGOSLAVIA SINCE WORLD WAR II;IMPACT OF CHANGES.

title: European Union
description: This course covers the historical development, political institutions, and philosophical underpinnings of the European Union. Topics include federalism, different notions of sovereignity, contemporary decision-making in the EU, assessments of democratic institutions in Europe, and prominent points of debate, such as monetary union, trade policies, environmental policies, and enlargement policies.

title: Comparative Government and Politics: Western Europe
description: This course focuses on political behavior and institutions in Britain, Germany, France, and other European countries and transnational developments in Europe, such as the postindustrial society phenomenon, terrorism, Eurocommunism, and European federation.

title: Comparative Government and Politics: Great Britain
description: This course examines the political and governmental system of Great Britain within a comparative framework. Comparison and contrast with the United States emphasized.

title: Politics of Latin America
description: This course examines Latin American politics after the mid-twentieth century. The course also examines the historical, economic, and international contexts in which Latin American political systems function, and identifies challenges to democracy and development. The specific Latin American countries covered will vary.

title: Comparative Government and Politics: The Middle East
description: This course discusses the political systems of the Middle East and their social, economic, and cultural foundations.

title: Political Development in East Asia
description: This course provides a comprehensive look at the events and people shaping Asian-Pacific history since the mid-18th century, and relates them to the shape of current Pacific Basin relations. The western colonial impact on and the subsequent political development of eleven northeast and southeast Asian countries is covered including: China, Hong Kong, Japan, Korea, Taiwan, Singapore, the Philippines, Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand and Vietnam.

title: Emerging Democracies in Northeast Asia: Korea, Taiwan, Japan
description: This course is an introduction to politics in Korea, Taiwan, and Japan. Looks at twentieth century political developments to better understand contemporary events in these countries. Deals with political issues such as electoral systems, party systems, "economic miracles", the process of democratization, the potential future role of these countries in world affairs, North Korean nuclear development, and unification of the Korean Peninsula.

title: Politics of China
description: This course is an introduction to the politics of the People's Republic of China, its political history and contemporary organization. Covers such topics as Chinese communism, the Cultural Revolution, the post-Mao era, the two Chinas, and popular movements and reform. Also examines current issues.

title: Politics of Japan
description: This course examines Japanese society and culture, political behavior, and political institutions since World War II. Emphasis is placed on political transformation since the early 1990s.

title: East European Politics
description: Examines the past and future of East Europe, focusing on the political, social, and economic legacies of the pre-World War I and inter-war periods, and the Stalin and post-Stalin periods. The collapse of communism and the democratization process are also examined.

title: Post-Soviet Politics
description: This course examines developments in the so-called 'transition countries' of Eastern Europe and Eurasia, drawing on readings to introduce students to the major debates on economic and political reform in the region.

title: Comparative Democratic Institutions
description: This course examines political institutions (including executive, legislative, judicial, as well as electoral systems) and evaluates their importance and role in democratic societies.

title: Ermerging Democracies of Central Europe
description: This course examines the new democracies of Central Europe after the collapse of communism. Emphasis is placed on democratic development in Poland, Czechoslovakia [sic], Romania, Yugoslavia, and Hungary. Discussion covers the historical process of political institutions of each state, especially the transition to democracy and the obstacles faced by all emerging democracies.

title: States and Markets
description: This course analyzes the multifaceted ways in which political and economic spheres interrelate. Students will be exposed to relevant debates on democracy and growth, the state's role in the economy, corruption, natural resources, and redistribution.

title: Special Topics in Comparative Government and Politics
description: Topics vary with the instructor and semester. May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours.

title: Political Violence
description: This course introduces students to scholarly writing on violent political conflict. Reviews theories of guerilla struggle and counter-insurgency, as well as the philosophy of non-violent direct action and several theories of conflict resolution. Course also explores the human costs of political violence.

title: Political Psychology
description: Course Description not on file

title: Institutional Approaches to Democracies and Dictatorships
description: This course examines questions about democracy and dictatorship from an institutional perspective. What is democracy and how is it measured, and how does regime affect the welfare of citizens? An emphasis is on the variety of institutional arrangements found in dictatorships.

title: Comparative Welfare States
description: This course explores three questions related to welfare states: the evolution of welfare states in different societies, how the welfare state has developed in the modern era, and how welfare states in other countries can lead to better understanding of American welfare politics and policies.

title: SEMINARS IN COMPARATIVE GOVERNMENT AND POLITICS:CORE SEMINAR IN COMPARATIVE GOVERNMENT
SEMINARS IN COMPARATIVE GOVERNMENT AND POLITICS:                                  SELECTED TOPICS

title: SEMINAR IN COMPARATIVE GOVERNMENT AND POLITICS:                                    COMMUNIST AND AUT
SOVIET POLITICS
description: AN INTENSIVE SURVEY OF POLITICAL INSTITUTIONS; PROCESSES;    AND SELECTED POLICIES IN THE SOVIET UNION; WITH PRINCIPAL                     EMPHASIS ON THE POST WORLD WAR II PERIOD.                   0

title: Core Seminar in Comparative Government and Politics
description: This core seminar offers a broad survey of the comparative field to familiarize the student with the scope and variety of approaches, theories, methods, and findings associated with comparative politics, including both the classics in the field and the most recent new research directions.

title: WEST EUROPEAN POLITICS
description: EXAMINES MAJOR ASPECTS OF EUROPEAN POLITICS IN A COMPARATIVE FRAMEWORK. FOCUSES ON MASS POLITICAL BEHAVIOR; PARTY CLEAVAGES; LEGISLATIVE BEHAVIOR AND EUROPEAN INTEGRATION. Ã¿ÂPURGED SUMMER 1997Ã¿Â¨

title: Seminar in Comparative Government and Politics: Great Britain
description: This course investigates and analyzes the major institutions and processes of British government and politics. Comparison and contrast with the political and governmental system of the United States is emphasized.

title: Seminar in Comparative Government and Politics: The Middle East
description: This course covers the political systems of the Middle East and their social, economic, and cultural foundations.

title: Political Islam: Ideology or Religion
description: This course focuses on seeking to understand the emergence, evolution and possible futures of what is commonly called political Islam. Students examine the crucial interplay between local and global forces shaping Muslim politics as a lived reality in today’s world.

title: Seminar in Comparative Government and Politics: Japan

title: Hydraulic Engineering II
description: This course covers methods for analyzing a broad range of unsteady flow conditions and for designing facilities to cope with resulting problems. Based on these methods, students learn to apply computer programs to practical water distribution and open-channel systems.

title: Urban Stormwater Runoff
description: The course is intended to provide an understanding of (1) storm events, stormwater runoff, and effects of urbanization on stormwater quantity and quality; (2) methods of analysis; and (3) planning and design procedures for stormwater facilities.

title: WATER RESOURCES PLANNING AND MANAGEMENT
description: ANALYSIS OF THE WATER CYCLE AND WATER BUDGET TO WATER RESOUR CE SYSTEMS. WATER QUALITY ISSUES IN WATER RESOURCES PLANNING & MANAGEMENT. MODELING TECHNIQUES FOR WATER SYSTEMS. LEGAL,SOCIAL,ECON ISSUE

title: Coastal and Estuarine Hydraulics
description: This course covers coastal hydraulic principles and waves in estuaries and coastal oceans, wave properties and wave forces on coastal structures, tidal motions, mixing and transport in estuaries, and coastal -engineering analysis.

title: Physical Models of Hydraulic Systems
description: Classical techniques of dimensional analysis and similitude are presented for hydraulic problems; operational physical model will be constructed and used to solve a practical engineering problem.

title: Numerical Models in Hydraulics
description: Numerical approaches including finite element techniques used in hydrology and hydraulics are presented and applied to simple engineering case studies.

title: Water Resources Planning and Management
description: This course examines the quantity and quality planning of water resources  systems. Economic considerations.

Finally, the course investigates the historical context of the variations through mini-lectures, discussions, and video viewings.

description: This studio course is the first in a series of graduate level courses in choreography. The course provides a supportive environment in which students explore the creative process to define and refine their unique creative voice and vision. Guided by critical inquiry, the course is dedicated to exploring choreographic practice, method, and process through a variety of creative strategies including readings, assignments, and lab time.

description: This course investigates, experientially and academically, New York City's resources. Using performances and exhibitions as the center point, the relationships among the various elements that compose an urban art event are explored. May be repeated concurrently within the same semester to a maximum of six semester hours.

title: METHODS AND MATERIALS IN DANCE EDUCATION
description: APPROVED FIELD WORK TEACHING EXPERIENCE REQUIRED PRIOR OR    CONCURRENT WITH ENROLLMENT..PROVIDE PROSPECTIVE TEACHER WITH                  OPPORTUNITIES TO DEVELOP CONCEPTS OF PEDAGOGY.              0

title: Methods and Materials in Dance Education
description: This course studies the principles of learning and how they inform the processes of designing lessons and teaching dance. Includes a five-week teaching practicum in local schools in addition to coursework on campus.

title: Educational Dance
description: This course follows the movement framework for educational dance with a focus on the exploration of themes and the refinement of movement sequences and creative dances.

title: Science of Dance Training
description: This course applies the movement sciences to the challenges of training dancers.

title: Dance History Pedagogy
description: This course introduces students to basic skills necessary to teach dance history and dance appreciation at the undergraduate level.

title: Supervised Teaching
description: A maximum of two hours may apply to a master's degree.

title: Dance Appreciation
description: This course surveys the development of dance as an art form within human culture. The course focuses on seminal dance works, including the Western canon, while cultivating an understanding of dance's historical, artistic, and cultural significance. Students explore major periods of dance history, choreographic masterworks, current dance trends, and influential choreographers and performers, all through readings, discussions, media presentations, live performances, and accessible movement laboratories. No prior dance experience is required.

title: Introduction to the Dance Profession
description: This course orients new BFA dance majors to professions in dance, exploring the complexities of careers in performance and related areas. Students develop a working knowledge of ballet and contemporary dance traditions and history, using a variety of experiences within the classes, including lectures, videos, studio sessions, guest presentations and panels, and live performance.

title: Classical Perspectives on Dance
description: This course introduces students to the history of dance by comparing ballet with other classical forms, including key works from the Western canon. It explores what makes a dance form "classical," how these traditions continue to influence culture, and examines important performances across different styles. The course highlights the balance between tradition and innovation, and how these dance forms both reflect and shape society. Through readings, discussions, and viewing performances, students will explore how classical dance has evolved over time and how contemporary artists reimagine these traditions today.

title: Introduction to Design
description: This course examines the basic vocabulary, understanding, and appreciation of the design process in dance production. May be repeated to a maximum of two semester hours.

title: MUSIC AND CHOREOGRAPHY
description: ACQUAINT STUDENTS MAJORING IN DANCE WITH ELEMENTS OF MUSIC   WHICH MAY AID THEM IN THE PERFORMANCE AND COMPOSITION OF                      DANCE.                                                      0

title: Rhythmic Analysis
description: This course is an analysis of rhythmic structures and their relationship to dance form and composition.

title: Music and Choreography
description: This course is an analysis of various elements of music with relationship to dance performance and/or choreography.

title: HISTORY AND PHILOSOPHY OF DANCE
description: STUDY OF ORIGINS/DEVELOPMENT OF DANCE FROM EARLY MAN'S       PRIMITIVE CULTURES THROUGH LATE MIDDLE AGES;SPECIFIC EMPHASIS                 RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN DANCE/ITS SOCIETAL INFLUENCES.

title: MANCC Experience
description: This course explores the Maggie Allesee National Center for Choreography (MANCC) experience with focus on ideas surrounding dance collaborations and process-oriented work, especially contextualization of and interaction with visiting MANCC artists and their current projects. The course also engages students in discussions related to dance research, aesthetics, and history in order for students to become more familiar with MANCC and to support how students envision contributing to the current professional dance field once they graduate.

title: Cultural Perspectives on Dance
description: This course surveys approaches to the study of global dance perspectives and practices through emphasis on dance as expression of cultural, historical, social and political forces. Issues of tradition and innovation in select dance phenomena are especially explored through readings, discussion, media presentation, embodied experiences, and movement laboratories. While movement is a key component of this course, no prior dance experience is required.

title: Contemporary Perspectives on Dance
description: This course introduces students to a comparative study of contemporary dance forms, predominantly in Western culture. The course traces the development of modern and contemporary dance as reflective of larger cultural and historical movements, focusing on the codification of dance technique, gender theories of performance, and the role of dance in society.

title: African-American Perspectives on Dance
description: This course examines how cultural and artistic expression can both integrate and divide different groups of people along lines of race, gender, and class using African-American dance as the central focus.

title: African-American Dance in American Culture
description: Using African American dance as a central focus, this course examines how cultural and artistic expression can both integrate and divide different groups of people along lines of race and class.

title: Movement Analysis
description: This course introduces and develops a basic understanding of movement-analysis concepts and systems of documentation and reconstruction. An historical survey of dance notation systems and movement analysis theories is conducted. Theoretical materials are examined through emphasis on movement observation, writing and reading skills, and creative use of concepts.

title: LABANOTATION
description: BEGINNING THROUGH INTERMEDIATE LEVEL LABANOTATION SKILLS ARE INTRODUCED AND DEVELOPED. THEORETICAL MATERIALS IN LABANOTATION ARE EXAMINED  WITH EMPHASIS ON WRITING SKILLS & DEVELOPED READING SKILLS.

title: INTERMEDIATE LABANOTATION
description: THEORETICAL MATERIAL IN LABANOTATION WITH FURTHER DEVELOPMENTOF WRITING SKILLS AND READING SKILLS.                                                                                                     0

title: Dance Production
description: This course studies the technical aspects of production.

title: Dance Theatre Laboratory
description: This lab provides hands-on training in various areas of production, publicity, and service through support of the School of Dance production, publicity, or service activities.

title: DANCE THEATRE LABORATORY
description: ASSIGNED PROBLEMS IN CONNECTION WITH CURRENT DANCE THEATRE   PRODUCTION.REPEAT TO A MAXIMUM OF 4 HOURS.                                                                                                0

title: Dance Kinesiology
description: This course covers the study of movement theories and body alignment for the technical aspects of dance performance.

title: Dance Conditioning
description: This course is a studio laboratory for concepts in movement theory and body alignment. May be repeated to a maximum of four semester hours.

title: Reformer Training for Dancers
description: This course teaches students to use the Pilates Reformer and other conditioning equipment to augment their dance technique training. Students learn a progressive program of exercises designed for dancers, refine their performance of the exercises, and adapt the program to their individual needs. Students also learn to see optimal alignment and efficient movement in other dancers and learn to provide targeted cueing and feedback for their partner. May be repeated to a maximum of six (6) credit hours.

title: Special Topics in Dance
description: Topics may vary from term to term.

title: Dancing in the Movies
description: This course traces the evolution of dance in the American popular film industry. Emphasis will be placed on how movies encapusulate popular stereotypes and icons, revealing the roles of gender, race, fashion, economic and political forces.

title: History of African American Social Dance of the Twentieth Century
description: This course traces the major African-American social dance styles of the twentieth century. These dance styles are examined in a context that facilitates understanding their relationship to the culture(s) and events that produced and influenced them.

title: Survey of Dance Technologies
description: This course provides training and aesthetic guidance for dance artists through the generation of computer-assisted imagery. It sets a foundation for future work in the areas of dance documentation, preservation, creation, promotion, and multimedia performance. May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours; may be repeated within the same term.

title: Dance and Video
description: This course includes the study of camera techniques for the screen and projection design for stage. The course is conducted in two units. The first unit explores concert dance documentation and videodance production. The second unit explores visual media design for the theater. These units may be taken concurrently in the same semester or sequentially for two credits each to a maximum of eight credit hours.

title: Photography for Dance
description: This course addresses the representation of dance and dancers in two dimensional non-time based photographic media. It involves hands-on camera work and post-production editing.

title: Documentation Techniques
description: This course instructs students in capturing the art of motion, combining hands-on experience with reading, discussion, and critique to develop technical skills and aesthetic awareness related to the documentation of concert dance.

title: Production and Stage Management for Dance
description: This course introduces students to the foundations of stage and production management for dance. Students cover basic coursework in resource and project management, as well as theoretical and practical experience in stage management.

title: Targeted Cross-Training for Dancers
description: This course provides a structure to help dancers (re)build the capacities they need to participate fully in dance technique classes, rehearsals, and performances following injury.

title: Gyrotonic Methodology
description: This course introduces students to the GYROTONIC® specialized exercise system. The course demonstrates how Gyrotonic offers enhanced freedom of movement with exercises executed on the professional Pulley Tower machine. Students learn how this approach systematically works joints and muscles while stimulating the body's internal organs with corresponding breathing patterns. Students' personal cross training interests are included in the course. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve (12) credits.

title: Honors Study in Dance
description: Written thesis for creative or academic research done as part of the honors in dance program. May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours.

title: Directed Individual Study
description: May be repeated to a maximum of twelve semester hours.

title: Dance Internship
description: This internship experience provides students with professional experience related to their field of study. This internship offers placements with a variety of dance- and arts-related organizations. The internship enables students to receive valuable networking connections, hands-on training, and the opportunity to work closely with professionals on meaningful projects. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve (12) credit hours.

title: New York City: Arts and Resources
description: This course investigates, experientially and academically, New York City's resources. Using performances and exhibitions as the center point, the relationships among the various elements that compose an urban art event are explored. May be repeated within the same semester. May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Senior Capstone Experience
description: This course will provide senior dance majors with opportunities to develop a significant, original project through the cultivation of an individual studio or related studies practice and semester portfolio. The course aims to prepare students for entering the field after graduation from the BFA program. Emphasis is placed on verbal articulations of creative process as well as thoughtful critiques of dance works.

title: Current Issues in Dance History, Theory, and Research
description: This course introduces students to current state-of-the-art trends in dance history, theory and research methodology. As the field of dance scholarship is currently undergoing dramatic, paradigm-shifting changes, the content of the course changes each semester to include the most current information. May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours.

title: Theory of Dance
description: This course focuses on the study of theoretical approaches to dance as evidenced by the work of influential scholars in the field of dance theory.

title: Dance Practices of the Global Gulf
description: This course examines social, religious, and concert dance practices in the Gulf South and surrounding areas—including Mexico and Cuba—from a global perspective, tracing the inter-textual, cultural forces that shape American identities (broadly defined) with attention to questions grounded in diasporic, transatlantic, and transnational issues. A maximum of three semester hours may apply toward the master’s degree.

title: Dance Migrations and Mobilities
description: This course examines social, religious, and concert dance practices in the Americas, tracing the inter-textual, cultural forces that shape American (broadly defined) identities with special attention to questions surrounding issues of migration and mobility. A maximum of three semester hours may apply toward the master’s degree.

title: Contemporary Stage and Social Movements
description: This course looks at a range of dance forms that flourished in the United States in the second half of the 20th Century and through to the present day, seeking novel connections between street, social, and concert dance forms and their entanglements in public discourse. A maximum of three semester hours may apply toward the master’s degree.

title: Theory of Dance Performance and Directing
description: This course is a study of historical development and theoretical bases of performance and directing.

title: Theory and Practice in Dance Technique
description: This course focuses on the study and studio exploration of principles of selected dance technique systems, with specific reference to their historic, kinesthetic, and aesthetic parameters.

title: Seminar in Dance Research
description: This course examines the development of advanced research skills in the area of dance history. Dance majors only. May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Production and Stage Management For Dance
description: This course introduces students to the foundations of stage and production management for dance. Students cover basic coursework in resource and project management, as well as theoretical and practical experience in stage management.

title: Visual Design for Choreography
description: This course is a critical analysis of the relationship between visual design and dance choreography. Study of current status of theatre design and technology.

title: Studies in Dance Technology
description: This course contextualizes art practices and professional career options within dance technology. Sub-genres are investigated as students develop an appreciation for the processes employed when working toward the merger of dance and technologies. Course topics will be supported by viewings, readings, critiques, and group discussions. Value is placed on developing one’s ability to articulate the creative process utilizing technology.

title: Screendance Composition
description: This course combines theoretical inquiry and practical application to establish a working knowledge for creating art within the genre, screendance. Dance-specific concepts are reinforced as choreographic principles are transposed from stage to screen. The course follows the chronological progression of production from conception to dissemination, while addressing artistry in the creative process using industry-standard equipment and software.

title: THEORY OF DANCE PERFORMANCE AND DIRECTING
description: THE STUDY OF THEORETICAL BASES OF PERFORMANCE AND DIRECTING.

title: Music Praxes in Dance
description: This course examines a diverse range of histories and fundamentals of music through the lens of past and current dance practices while also exploring music-dance collaborations. Students develop skills in communicating with musicians as well as creating and editing sound scores.

title: MFA Creative Project Defense
description: This zero-credit hour course is intended for MFA candidates in the last semester of the program. Students meet with their creative project committee to deliver an oral defense of their Creative Project and present an analysis of the creative process and a self-examination of the culminating project. May be repeated two times. May be repeated withing the same semester.

title: MA Capstone
description: This course facilitates MA student progress in their final semester as they work toward the completion of a culminating capstone project as outlined in their research proposals defended in the previous semester. Students work closely with the Committee Chair to fulfill independent research objectives. Capstone projects in both MA American Dance Studies and Studio and Related Studies may entail a wide variety of creative and scholarly activities tailored toward student interests, but must include a public sharing of the project, a reflection and synthesis paper, and defend the findings of the research in a final meeting with the committee.

title: Creative Thesis
description: This course is for MFA degree candidates in dance only and oversees the research, development, and production of the graduate thesis project. A minimum of six semester hours is required.

title: Master's Thesis in American Dance Studies
description: This is an individualized course of study leading to completion of a formal master's thesis in American dance history. May be repeated within the same term to a maximum of six semester hours. May be repeated during the same semester.

title: MFA Creative Project
description: This course is designed to facilitate MFA student progress in their final two or three semesters as they work toward the completion of a culminating MFA Creative Project as outlined in their research proposals defended in the second or third semester. Students enrolled in this course work closely with the MFA Creative Project Committee Chair to fulfill independent research objectives.

title: Master's Thesis Defense
description: Thesis topic to be arranged with advisor.

title: Directed Individual Study
description: In this course, students do readings in an area of demography with subject tailored to the student.

title: Directed Individual Study
