description: Course Description not on file

title: President's Seminar in Economics
description: Course Description not on file

title: Introduction to Economics
description: This course is a survey of the discipline for people taking only one economics course. Historical perspective and major principles of theory are presented. Not to be taken by students who have had or who must take ECO 2013 and 2023. Not applicable to the economics major nor the economics minor.

title: INTRODUCTION TO ECONOMIC THINKING
description: SURVEY OF DISCIPLINE FOR PEOPLE TAKING ONLY ONE ECONOMICS    COURSE.HISTORICAL PERSPECTIVE;MAJOR PRINCIPLES AND THEORY.   .                THEORY ARE PRESENTED.

title: Principles of Macroeconomics
description: In this course, students will learn the foundations of macroeconomics as the branch of economics concerned with how decision-making, in an environment of scarcity, maps onto the aggregate economy. Students will examine theories and evidence related to the following core set of topics: national income determination, money, monetary and fiscal policy, macroeconomic conditions, international trade and the balance of payments, and economic growth and development.

title: ECONOMICS OF THE PRICE SYSTEM
description: CONSUPTION; PRODUCTION/RESOURCE ALLOCATION ARE CONSIDERED    FROM A PRIVATE/SOCIAL POINT OF VIEW;MICROECONOMIC PROBLEMS                    AND POLICY ALTERNATIVES.

title: Principles of Microeconomics
description: This course covers consumption, production, and resource allocations considered from a private and social point of view; microeconomic problems and policy alternatives; economics of inequality and poverty; and comparative economic systems.

title: ECONOMICS FOR ENGINEERS
description: THIS COURSE WILL EMPHASIZE MICRO-ECONOMICS BUT WILL ADD      APPLICATIONS & SUBJECT MATTER PERTINENT TO ENGINEERING STU-  DENTS. QUANTITATIVE EXAMPLES WILL BE USED TO ALLOW STUDENTS TO USE MATH. TOOLS

title: Debating Economic Issues
description: This course applies economic analysis to current economic policy issues. Topics may include financial markets, Social Security, debt finance, health care, immigration, global climate change and environmental policy, regulation, welfare reform, labor market discrimination, drug policy, and topics selected by students.

title: Personal Finance: Foundations for Entrepreneurship
description: This course is designed to help students better understand personal finance and provide them with the tools to make better choices and live a more fulfilling life.

title: Personal Finance: Foundations for Entrepreneurship
description: This course is designed to help students better understand personal finance and provide them with the tools to make better choices and live a more fulfilling life.

title: Family Consumer Economics for Financial Planning
description: This course covers the fundamentals of family and consumer economics as a branch of economics to help with financial planning strategies. Students receive instruction in modeling, economic forecasting, indexing, price theory, and analyzing individual/group commodities and services to provide an important foundation for financial planning.

title: Decision Making Under Risk and Uncertainty
description: This course is an introduction to the theory of economic decision-making under risk and uncertainty. Emphasis is placed on developing and applying alternative theories of decision making to insurance markets, financial markets, and the negotiation of contracts.

title: Intermediate Microeconomic Theory
description: This course covers various topics such as supply, demand, cost of production, theory of the firm, factor price determination, and other microeconomic resource allocation questions.

title: Theory of the Firm, Hierarchies and Entrepreneurship
description: This course introduces students to the theory of the firm. Particular emphasis is placed on understanding how firms are organized and how they manage their employees using incentives and other economic mechanisms.

title: Applied Microeconomic Analysis
description: This course examines the applications of microeconomic theory for business and policy analysis. Topics include the theory of the firm, valuation techniques in the absence of market prices, empirical research with accounting, financial and administrative data, theory of supply and business strategy, cost-benefit methods.

title: APPLIED MICROECONOMIC ANALYSIS
description: APPLICATIONS OF MICROECONOMIC THEORY FOR BUSINESS/POLICY ANALYSIS. THEORY OF THE FIRM/VALUATION TECHNIQUES IN ABSENSE OF MARKET PRICES/EMPIRICAL RESEARCH WITH ACCOUNTING, THEORY OF SUPPLY, ETC. .  . 0

title: Free to Choose
description: This course, dealing with liberty and economic freedom, addresses many present and past social issues and public policy decisions.

title: Market Ethics
description: This course examines the vices, virtues, and values of capitalism to evaluate its ethical properties. It is designed to raise questions and clearly-structured issues so that the student can make informed and thoughtful decisions.

title: Intermediate Macroeconomic Theory
description: This course covers the basic model of income determination, emphasizing the roles of real and monetary sector of the economy. Results of empirical work are surveyed.

title: MONEY,BANKING,AND MONETARY POLICY
description: FUNCTIONS OF MONEY;BANK CREATION OF DEPOSITS;CREDIT;FEDERAL  RESERVE CONTROL OF MONEY SUPPLY/MONETARY THEORY AND POLICY                    QUESTIONS.                                                  0

title: Financial Markets, the Banking System, and Monetary Policy
description: This course explores the functions of money, bank creation of deposits, and credit; Federal Reserve control of money supply; and monetary theory and policy questions.

title: DEBATING ECONOMIC ISSUES
description: ECONOMIC ANALYSIS USED TO UNDERSTAND CURRENT ECONOMIC AND PUBLIC ISSUES.COURSE RELIES HEAVILY ON DEBATE AND OTHER FORMS OF ORAL DISCOURSE.TOPICS VARY.TOPICS INCLUDE S.S., HEALTH CARE, IMMIGRATION, ETC

title: History of Economic Ideas
description: This course discusses the evolution of economic ideas from ancient Greece to the modern period emphasizing the relationship between developments in economic analysis and cultural/technological changes. Critique of modern economic theory in terms of its sources and logical content.

title: INTRODUCTION TO RADICAL AND UTOPIAN ECONOMICS
description: DISSENTING ECONOMIC DOCTRINES FROM ADAM SMITH TO PRESENT;    INCLUDING KARL MARX/CONTEMPORARY DISSENTERS.

title: Analysis of Economic Data
description: This course provides basic skills in graphing and analyzing economics data. The first two blocks of the course are composed of an extensive coverage of probability and statistics that is necessary to understand the theory and practice of regression analysis. The third block of the course is devoted entirely to regression analysis. Some of the concepts discussed in the second and third block of the course are illustrated with widely-used statistics and econometrics software giving the student the opportunity to learn the application of some of the concepts discussed in class to economics data.

title: GROWTH OF AMERICAN ECONOMY
description: DEVELOPMENT OF THE ECONOMIC FORCES,RESOURCES,INSTITUTIONS,ANDIDEAS RELATING TO AMERICAN ECONOMIC GROWTH ANALYZED THROUGH              GROWTH THEORIES AND ISSUES DEBATES ON ECONOMIC HISTORY.

title: Growth of the American Economy
description: This course examines the factors in the development of economic forces, resources, institutions, and ideas relating to American economic growth analyzed through growth theories and issue debates on economic history.

title: Special Topics in Economics
description: This course explores special topics of current interest or of benefit from specialties of visiting faculty. May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours. May be repeated within the same semester.

title: Cooperative Education Work Experience
description: Course Description not on file

title: Behavioral Economics
description: This course examines the consequences for economic analysis when individuals (and groups) deviate from rational behavior in identifiable and predictable ways, and incorporates these systematic biases into more accurate models of economic decision making.

title: Economics of Compassion
description: This course addresses international and domestic issues of compassionate, charitable, and philanthropic activities. It offers an economic framework from which students can critically evaluate public and private actions whose purpose is to eliminate hunger, disease, poverty or other human burdens.

title: Economics of Information
description: This course covers the theory of Uncertainty and Information. The course first defines uncertainty, information, and describes how the economic agent reacts to it. The course is also devoted to cases where information information is endogenous, and can be generated or revealed by agents.

title: Issues in Money and Banking
description: This course emphasizes the role of monetary policy in various macroeconomic theories. Focus on controversy over the effects monetary policy has on employment, inflation, and interest rates.

title: ISSUES IN MONEY AND BANKING
description: ROLE OF MONETARY POLICY IN VARIOUS MACROECONOMIC THEORIES IS EMPHASIZED. CONTROVERSY OVER EFFECTS MONETARY POLICY HAS ON                   EMPLOYMENT;INFLATION;INTEREST RATES IS FOCUSED ON.          0

title: Introduction to Economic Forecasting
description: Provides a hands-on survey of forecasting methods used in business and economics. Students work extensively with computer-based statistical software to solve actual cases encountered in the business world.

title: Economic Growth
description: This course covers the differences in income per capita in/between countries. Topics cover what drives the sustained growth in the standard of living in the United States and other developed countries, how less developed countries might catch up with the developed countries, and what has impeded this process.

title: Monetary Thought and History
description: This course deals with issues in money, banking, and monetary policy in the United States and other countries. A major theme is how the monetary and financial organizations can be improved.

title: Games and Decisions
description: This course is a non-technical introduction to strategic decision-making. Focus on situations involving conflict and cooperation and on decision-making under conditions of uncertainty and ignorance. Applies game theory and decision theory to such topics as bargaining and negotiations, contracting, auctions, and voting.

title: Into to Math Economics
description: This course uses mathematical techniques such as probability, matrix algebra, and calculus to better understand fundamental principles of economics and applies these techniques to policy analysis.

title: Introduction to Econometrics
description: This course introduces statistical inference, estimation theory, model building, and forecasting methods. Emphasis is on model building and policy analysis. Extensive use is made of PC econometric software.

title: Introduction to Research in Economics
description: This course is research based, and provides an introduction into the world of scholarly research in economics.

title: Experimental Economics
description: This course is an introduction to the use of laboratory experimental economics, a relatively new method of economics research in which the classic model of laboratory experimentation is applied to microeconomics. The course is presented using both traditional lecture format and hands-on participation in different experimental economic formats.

title: Public Sector Economics
description: This course examines the logic of collective actions, principles of government expenditures, theory and practice in taxation, shifting and incidence of taxes.

title: Economic Analysis of Politics
description: This course uses economic models to analyze political decision making. A theory of constitutions is developed and applied to the U.S. Constitution. Models of majority rule decision making and bureauractic supply are used to develop an understanding of supply and demand in the public sector.

title: Economics of State and Local Government
description: This course covers state and local revenues, expenditures, and borrowing; intergovernmental relationships.

title: International Trade
description: This course discusses the theory of international trade, the gains from trade, tariffs and other trade restrictions, cartels.

title: International Finance
description: This course focuses on the balance of payments; disequilibrium and adjustments; birth, evolution, and demise of the Bretton Woods System; the managed float; international monetary reform; multinational corporations.

title: Directed Individual Study
description: May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours.

title: SENIOR TUTORIAL IN ECONOMICS
description: SELECTED TOPICS IN ECONOMICS. MAXIMUM ENROLLMENT OF FIVE STUDENTS IN EACH TUTORIAL. REPEATABLE TO A MAXIMUM OF SIX (6)           SEMESTER HOURS.                                                       0

title: Professional Development in Economics
description: This course covers issues that economics students encounter in the workplace: ethics, presentation skills, data gathering and analysis. These topics, as well as problems encountered in students' field study course work, are  discussed. Limited to Economics and Applied Economics majors in the Economics Internship Program.

title: Senior Tutorial in Economics
description: This course covers selected topics in economics. Maximum enrollment of five students in each tutorial. Repeatable one tome to a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Honors in the Major Research
description: In this course, students accepted into the Honors in the Major program complete an original research or creative project in their major area of study. This course must be repeated at least twice to complete a minimum of six (6) credit hours total, but may be repeated up to a maximum of twelve credit hours in total.

title: Economics Internship
description: This course is an academic course related to the internship experience. Students are required to submit a weekly description of their internship activities, duties, and responsibilities; to complete a set of assignments; and at the end of the semester, to submit a paper that describes in detail the tasks they performed during the internship and discusses the skills and information required to accomplish each task. Students enrolled for six credit hours must also complete a research paper that integrates their classroom knowledge and work experience.

title: Field Study in Economics
description: This course enables students to receive credit toward the economics major for applied and practical experience working in a variety of organizations such as state agencies, banks, and finance departments. Limited to Economics and Applied Economics majors in the Economics Internship Program.

title: PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN ECONOMICS
description: COVERS ISSUES THAT ECONOMICS STUDENTS ENCOUNTER IN THE WORKPLACE;ETHICS/PRESENTATION SKILLS/DATA GATHERING & ANALYSIS,ALSOCOVERS STUDENTS' PROBS IN FIELD STUDY COURSEWORK.MAY BE REPEATED TO 3 HOURS.

title: Economic Principles for International Affairs
description: This course serves as an introduction to economics for graduate students inmajors other than economics. Covers material in ECO 2013 and ECO 2023 in one semester. Intended for international affairs graduate students and similar.

title: Applied Microeconomics I
description: This is a beginning graduate-level course in microeconomic analysis. The course is designed to prepare students for subsequent work in microeconomic analysis and in applied microeconomics' courses such as public finance, industrial organization, and labor economics.

title: Product Markets and the Theory of the Firm
description: This course examines consumer choice, demand theory, production theory, costs, market supply, theory of the business firm, and allocation under the competitive market structure. Undergraduate price theory is a prerequisite.

title: Imperfect Competition, Factor Markets, and Income Distribution
description: This course covers topics such as monopoly, oligopoly monopolistic competition, derived demand and theory of factor markets, general equilibrium analysis, welfare economics, interdependencies, income distribution, and public choice theory.

title: Applied Microeconomics II
description: This course explores such topics in applied microeconomics as demand estimation, hedonic models, cost functions, cost-benefit analysis, tax incidence, event studies, selection bias, and earnings equations. A student project is required.

title: Markets and Auctions
description: This course familiarizes students with relevant topics, literature, and research techniques in the field of experimental economics.

title: Applied Market Design
description: This course explores the theory and practice of market design. Particular emphasis is placed on understanding how the rules and institutional features of different market-based mechanisms can be engineered to solve resource allocation problems. The course focuses on applications of two-sided matching markets, single-unit and multi-unit auctions, and markets for transferable permits.

title: Macroeconomic Theory I
description: This course introduces the basic tools in macroeconomic theory.

title: Money and National Income Determination
description:
This course is based on the premise that the basic model of income determination is built emphasizing the roles of real and monetary sectors of the economy. Results of empirical work are surveyed. A project is required of each graduate student.

Restricted to non-Economics majors.

title: Macroeconomic Theory, Practice, and Policy
description: This course investigates aggregate production functions and productivity, and provides an introduction to dynamic macro systems.

title: Macroeconomic Theory II
description: This course explores further macro dynamics, the quantity theory, determinants of the demand for and supply of money, and money models.

title: Global Macroeconomics
description: This course is a continuation of the first macroeconomics course for master's students. Though the focus is on macroeconomic applications, macroeconomic theory is expanded to consider the implications of income distributions, wealth distributions, financial market innovations, price markup practices, and global integration for macroeconomics.

title: MONEY AND THE MACRO ECONOMIC SYSTEM
description: FURTHER MACRO DYNAMICS;QUANTITY THEORY;DETERMINANTS OF THE   DEMAND FOR AND SUPPLY OF MONEY;MONEY MODELS.

title: Financial Economics I
description: This course is intended to provide a comprehensive introduction to the field of financial economics. The class focuses on static and dynamic consumption based on asset pricing models and a few elementary applications. The class is designed to set up the framework for models with production, financial institutions and monetary policy issues, which is the basis for more advanced work.

title: Financial Economics II
description: This course focuses on three broad areas: production-based asset pricing theory and corporate finance; financial intermediation; and monetary theory and policy. Particular emphasis is placed on the economic role played by commercial banks in private information economies, and on the effect of Federal Reserve policy on financial markets.

title: Macroeconomic Theory III
description: This course Introduces advanced graduate students to the core concepts and techniques used in cutting-edge academic research. The course covers essential techniques for analyzing macroeconomic data and for mapping data to theoretical models; and covers the core models commonly used in macroeconomic research.

title: History of Economic Thought
description: This course covers the analysis and critique of economic ideas, beginning with the Greeks. Concentration is upon classical ecnomists.

title: CONTEMPORARY ECONOMIC THOUGHT AND METHODOLOGY
description: LATE 19TH/20TH CENTURY ECONOMIC THOUGHT BEGINNING WITH       MARSHALL.PARTIAL/GENERAL EQUILIBRIUM MODELS;AUSTRIAN THRORY                   OF CAPITAL;INSTITUTIONAL ECONOMICS;KEYNES/MACROECONOMICS.   0

title: Static Optimization in Economics
description: This course examines mathematical methods used for the solution of static optimization problems in economic theory.

title: Introduction to Mathematical Economics
description: This course focuses on the use of mathematical economic models, equilibrium analysis, linear algebra, comparative static analysis, optimization problems, and dynamic problems.

title: Computational Economics I
description: In this course, topics include solutions of linear and nonlinear systems of equations, numerical integration and differentiation, optimization, Monte  Carlo and stochastic simulation, finite element and spectral solution methods for ordinary and partial differential equations, dynamic programming and stochastic optimal control, and asymptotic perturbation methods.

title: Econometrics I
description: This course is an introduction to econometric methods focusing on the statistical foundation for estimation and inference in the classical regression model.

title: SAS for Economists
description: This course uses the SAS programming language to manipulate data and to estimate econometric models. Topics that are covered include: database construction using the output and retain statements; conducting multivariate regressions; and the use of the SAS macro facility.

title: Programming for Economists
description: This course familiarizes students with modern programming tools and how to use these tools to analyze typical economic issues. Topics covered in this course include how to create, input, clean, and manipulate data, combine datasets, perform statistical analyses, including linear and non-linear regressions and data visualization. Students are exposed to programming languages including Python, SQL, and SAS.

title: Applied Econometrics
description: This course introduces statistical concepts used in econometric thinking, reviews the classical linear regression model, and discusses applications to economic data.

title: BASIC APPLIED ECONOMETRICS
description: COURSE INTRODUCES STATISTICAL INTERFERNCE, ESTIMATION THEORY,MODEL BUILDING, AND FORECASTING METHODS. EMPHASIS IS ON MODEL BUILDING AND POLICY ANALYSIS. EXTENSIVE USE IS OF PC ECONOMETRIC SOFTWARE.

title: Econometrics II
description: This course considers extensions of the classical regression model. Topics include nonlinear least squares, instrumental variables estimation, and generalized least squares.

title: Econometric Methods for Panel Data
description: This course provides students with the tools necessary for working with panel data, in order to evaluate different methods and their applicability to particular estimation problems. Topics are typically not included in econometrics core courses, yet are important in empirical research. Focus is on the analysis of cross-section and panel data, and on the discussion of linear and nonlinear models.

title: Time Series Analysis
description: Univariate and multivariate time series methods including: univariate ARIMA, transfer function models, state space models, vector autoregression models, vector error correction models, spectral analysis, causality tests, and unit root tests. Data analysis and model building are emphasized.

title: Bayesian Methods in Econometrics and Economics
description: An introduction to Bayesian inference in econometrics and economics. A comparison of Bayesian and orthodox methods of inference. Emphasis will be on application of Bayesian methods rather than theory.

title: Limited Dependent Variable Models
description: This course introduces graduate students to logit, probit, tobit, multinomial logit, selection, and hazard models.

title: Data Analytics for Economists
description: This course employs Data Camp, an online learning platform, to help students gain experience using SQL and R. Students also have the opportunity to take Data Camp courses in AI, machine learning, Tableau, and spreadsheets.

title: Advanced Experimental Economics
description: This course provides PhD level introduction to experimental economics. The first section of the course focuses on how to run an economic experiment. After this, the course studies four major areas of economics that have been changed by experimental economics: (1) decision making under risk and uncertainty, (2) models of fairness and reciprocity, (3) game theoretic models and  (4) models of markets. The course stresses student participation, and ends with a presentation of student research projects.

title: Empirical Methods in Applied Economics
description: This course examines empirical methods used in performing applied economics research using a wide range of academic papers that have used the relevant techniques.

title: Introduction to Research Methods in Economics
description: This course demonstrates how to apply economic analysis and teaches how to construct these analyses in a variety of research settings. In addition to exposing students to ongoing research in a variety of fields and current research ongoing in the department, this course teaches students how to communicate their research both in written form and in presentations.

title: Public Economics
description: This course examines the principles of taxation and debt, shifting and incidence, public expenditures and redistribution theory.

title: Public Goods
description: This course explores the theory, empirical evidence, and experimental evidence regarding how human societies provide public goods.

title: PUBLIC FINANCE
description: PRINCIPLES OF TAXATION AND DEBT; SHIFTING AND INCIDENCE; PUBLIC EXPENDITURES; AND REDISTRIBUTION THEORY.                                   SHIFTING/INCIDENCE;PUBLIC EXPENDITURES;REDISTRIBUTION THEORY.

title: PUBLIC CHOICE
description: THE ROLE OF GOVERNMENT;PUBLIC GOODS AND EXTERNALITIES;VOTING AND COLLECTIVE CHOICE;BUREAUCRACY THEORY;AND POLITICAL STRUCTURE AND ECONOMIC ORGANIZATION.

title: Public Choice
description: This course focuses on the the role of government, public goods and externalities, voting and collecive choice, bureaucracy theory, and political structure and economic organzation.

title: STATE AND LOCAL PUBLIC FINANCE IN THE UNITED                                                STATES
description: STATE AND LOCAL REVENUES; EXPENDITURES; AND BORROWING;       INTERGOVERNMENTAL RELATIONSHIPS.

title: SEMINAR IN PUBLIC ECONOMICS
description: PRIMARILY FOR ADVANCED STUDENTS INTERESTED IN RESEARCH IN    PUBLIC ECONOMICS ISSUES.STUDENT WILL ANALYZE RESEARCH OF                      OTHERS;INITIATE RESEARCH PROJECT OF THEIR OWN.              0

title: Urban and Regional Economics
description: This course introduces students to the evolution of cities, along with issues with which cities and regions must deal (e.g., sprawl, pollution, congestion, transportation, poverty, housing and neighborhood development, public finance) to be examined from an economic perspective. The content lays the foundation for an analysis of policy alternatives to deal with these issues.

title: Seminar in International Trade Theory and Policy
description: This course explores the theories of the cause, magnitude, and patterns of real trade among nations, ranging from comparative cost explanations to Heckscher-Ohlin theories and recent approaches. Policy issues regarding contemporary international trade problems, the role of tariffs, and quotas also are covered.

title: Seminar in the Theory and Policy of International Finance
description: This course examines institutional characteristics of international financing of trade, relations between money flows and real trade, theories of foreign exchange and short-term capital flows, long-term capital flows, alternative exchange rate systems. Contemporary international monetary problems and the role of aid in economic development also are discussed.

title: Supervised Research
description: May be repeated to a maximum of five semester hours.

title: Professional Development for Economists
description: This course covers issues of ethics and responsibilities for professional economists. Faculty and visiting economists offer presentations and discussions of the work of professional economists in the public and private sectors. May be repeated to a maximum of three semester hours.

title: Graduate Tutorial in Economics
description: This course is an in-depth study of specific topics in economics. Enrollment limited to five students. May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Seminar in Political Economy
description: This course covers a changing agenda of contemporary topics in political economy. Students are expected to write and present major research papers for discussion in the seminar. Topics vary from term to term and writing assignments are associated with the seminar topics.

title: Special Topics
description: This course covers special topics of current interest or of benefit from the specialties of visiting faculty. May be repeated in the same semester.

title: Supervised Teaching
description: May be repeated to a maximum of five semester hours.

title: Applied Economics Internship
description: This course is intended to facilitate the transition from the academic world to the workplace for students in the project-track Economics MS degree.

title: Thesis
description: A minimum of six semester hours is required.

title: Extended Master's Paper
description: Course Description not on file

title: Applied Master's Project
description: As the capstone for the applied master's degree, this project requires students to use theory, research methods, and analytical procedures learned in the program to reserach an applied economics question.  Finding are presented in both oral and written format.  The three-hour project is taken in both six-week summer sessions, for a total of six semester hours.  May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours.

title: Economic Analysis: Solving and Communicating a Consulting Project
description: This course is a companion course to the Applied Project (ECO 5973) for students in the MS in Applied Economics degree. The course focuses on how to analyze and solve consulting projects. In addition, students discuss how to effectively communicate results to clients. The course also helps students to quickly adapt to the life of an economist in private, state or federal organizations. May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours.

title: Topics in Behavioral Economics
description: This course is an overview of behavioral economics using both empirical and theoretical contributions from economics and psychology. The course aims to identify alternative assumptions, in line with observed choice patterns for use in mainstream economic theory.

title: SEMINAR IN ADVANCED MACROECONOMICS
description: REVIEW OF POPULAR ECONOMETRIC MODELS TO ILLUSTRATE ISSUES    REGARDING DEGREE OF DISAGGREGATION; LENGTH OF LAPS; SUPPLY-                   SIDE CHARACTERISTICS;INTERNATIONAL DIMENSION OF MACROECONOMIC

title: Topics in Macroeconomics
description: This course surveys recent developments in macroeconomic theory with an emphasis on developing research skills in an applied context. Topics include endogenous growth and technological diffusion across countries, money and growth, and modern business cycle theory.

title: Monetary Theory and Policy
description: This course builds the skills needed to perform research in monetary economics and to survey recent literature in the area. The role of the money market in the macroeconomy and the policy tools, policy objectives, and history of the Federal Reserve are also emphasized.

title: Advanced Monetary Theory and Policy
description: Current controversies in the demand and supply of money; effectiveness and incidence of monetary policy in the United States and in foreign countries.

title: Open Economy Macroeconomics
description: This is an advanced PhD course on open economy macroeconomics and finance. Canonical models of open-economy are explored, including the real business cycle model and sticky price models.

title: Monetary Theory and History
description: Monetary thought; evaluation of monetary and financial structure; the pre- and post-National Bank Act eras; the Federal Reserve era; international monetary and financial organization.

title: Topics in Microeconomics
description: This course discusses competitive general equilibrium (theory and applications); fundamental results of welfare economics; market failure (externalities and public goods); game and decision theory; the economics of uncertainty (theory and applications).

title: Doctoral Workshop
description: This course consists of informal seminars and colloquia for critical review of research work in progress and advanced research topics, presented by doctoral students, faculty, and visitors. Registration for credit requires departmental approval. May be repeated without limit.

title: Teaching Workshop
description: This course consists of informal seminars and colloquia on topics and issues related to teaching economics at the college level, presented by doctoral students, faculty, and visitors. May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Preliminary Examination Preparation
description: This course is open to students who have completed the core PhD theory courses and are engaged in intensive study for their PhD preliminary examinations.

title: Dissertation
description: Course Description not on file

title: Master's Comprehensive Exam
description: Course Description not on file

title: Preliminary Doctoral Examination
description: Course Description not on file

title: Master's Thesis Defense
description: Course Description not on file

title: Dissertation Defense
description: Course Description not on file

title: Current Economic Issues of the US
description: This course aims to familiarize students with tools and institutions economists use to form educated and insightful opionions on important current and future issues.

title: Economics of Art and Culture
description: This course allows students to use traditional economic analysis of supply and demand to examine the markets for "high art". Students discover in the class that many of the standard approaches to economic analysis apply to these markets, but there are also features of the art markets that are unique.

title: Economics of Population
description: This course examines determinants and consequences of world population growth and changes, components of population growth in more- and less-developed countries, population and food supply, nonrenewable resource interrelationships.

title: Afro-Americans in the American Political Economy
description: This course examines the market, institutional, governmental, and social processes that have contributed to the economic well-being of African-Americans. Also covers theoretical material related to wage determination, labor market discrimination, and marriage and transitions in family structure, as well as interaction between race and class as determinants of the life chances of African-Americans.

title: Economics of Industrial Relations
description: "Industrial relations in the public and private sectors examined from the perspective of history, theory, and institutions."

title: Economics of Natural Resources, Energy, and the Environment
description: This course focuses on the relationship between natural resource availability and growth, capital theory, economics of the environment, the U.S. energy problem and alternatives for the future, an economic appraisal of U.S. energy policy.

title: Economics of Living Marine Resources
description: The economic role of commercial and recreational fishing industries in the U.S. and world, fishery management schemes, resources depletion and environmental deterioration, issues on current and potential food from the sea. An interdisciplinary course with no previous economics courses required.

title: POPULATION AND DEVELOPMENT IN FLORIDA
description: REVIEWS THE DEMOGRAPHIC TRENDS PRESENT IN FLORIDA AND TRACES THEIR CAUSES AND CONSEQUENCES. SYD 3010 OR EPC 3113 RECOMMEN                  DED.

title: POPULATION AND DEVELOPMENT IN FLORIDA
description: REVIEWS THE DEMOGRAPHIC TRENDS PRESENT IN FLORIDA AND TRACES THEIR CAUSES AND CONSEQUENCES. SYD 3010 OR EPC 3113 RECOMMEN                  DED.

title: ECONOMICS OF INDUSTRIAL RELATIONS
description: INDUSTRIAL RELATION IN PUBLIC/PRIVATE SECTIONS EXAMINED FROM PERSPECTIVE OF HISTORY;THEORY; AND INSTITUTIONS.                                                                                          0

title: Economics and the Law
description: This course is focused on the impact of the legal system on economic activity and the role of economic analysis in assessing the relative efficiency of alternative legal rules and institutions.

title: Economics of Health
description: This course provides an overall view of the U.S. health care system, focusing on the following topics: demand for medical care, health insurance, hospitals, physician services, regulation versus competition for cost containment, alternative delivery care systems, financing uncompensated care, Medicare prospective payment system, long-term care, and economic issues concerning health-related behavior (i.e., alcohol, tobacco).

title: Land Use, Housing and Government Regulation
description: This course provides the theoretical and institutional machinery for analyzing land, housing and mortgage markets, with special attention given to the intended and unintended effects of government regulation of these markets. Important empirical evidence is also reviewed.

title: Seminar in Entrepreneurship and Economic Policy Writing
description: This course is designed to equip students with the skills needed to communicate economic policy ideas through policy writing for non-academic audiences with little background in economic theory or empirical techniques while focusing on writing in a policy-oriented work setting, developing critical thinking skills, identifying and applying creative uses of data to problems, and working as analysts.

title: Public Policy and the Quality of Life
description: This course examines the differences between market allocation of resources and government planning to allocate resources, and illustrates how the market mechanism can be used to improve the quality of life in areas where government allocation is often advocated. These include environmental protection, land use planning, the regulation of product quality, health care, education policy, and more.

title: Economics of Sports
description: This course presents an economic analysis of sports and entertainment. Focus is on industrial organization of the sports market, public finance and sports, sports labor market, and college and non-profit sports. Similar issues related to entertainment and artistic industries may also be covered.

title: Introduction to Economic Demography
description: This course focuses on the acquisition of demographic and related socioeconomic data and provides an introduction to the analytical tools and skills necessary to understand and interpret the data.

title: Economics of Aging
description: This course considers the factors which cause populations to become older and systematically analyzes the economic consequences of this aging. Topics considered include social security and health care financing.

title: Labor Economics
description: This course explores theoretical and empirical examination of wage determination, income maintenance programs, labor force, employment, unemployment, functioning of labor markets, and manpower programs.

title: Economics of Energy, Environment and Sustainability
description: This course explores the idea that in addressing public policy issues involving sustainability of the environment and energy systems, engineering is important for obvious reasons, but without consideration of the human factor, engineering solutions are incomplete. In the course, students are educated on economic models and analysis of sustainability in energy and environmental systems.

title: Industrial Organization
description: This course is an introduction to the economic analysis of industry. Game theory is applied to explore how firms create, maintain and exploit market power. We will also study the welfare implications of market power.

title: ECONOMIC FUNCTIONS OF THE STATE
description: SURVEY OF ECONOMICS RETIONALES FOR BUSINESS REGULATION;      EFFECTS OF REGULATION;PUBLIC UTILITIES;NATIONALIZATION                   SUBSIDIES;CONTROLS; AIMED AT POLLUTION; HEALTH FOR SAFETY GOALS.

title: Government Regulation of Business
description: This course combines elements of economics, political science, history, and law to understand the rationale for government regulation and its effects on economic performance. Regulatory economics is an extremely broad field of study, ranging from public choice to legal studies, to public economics, to industrial organization, to numerous other sub-fields within economics.

title: Economics of Corruption
description: This course carefully defines corruption and examines its causes and effects, both at the level of the individual and at the level of the society. Different theoretical models of corruption are discussed, along with empirical studies and various successful and unsuccessful anti-corruption policies in a number of countries.

title: Economics of Crime
description: This course examines crime and criminal justice policy using the tools of  economics. The focus is on crimes against persons and property, and drug policy. Rational behavior, opportunity cost, markets, bureaucratic behavior and policy analysis are studied in this context.

title: Urban Economics
description: This course is an analysis of trends in urban economies in the U.S. and elsewhere. Introduction to economic and demographic data sources for analysis of urban areas; issues confronting contemporary urban places.

title: Research Methods for Studying Housing, Entrepreneurship, Land and Housing Finance
description: This course explores the use of quantitative research methods to evaluate and understand the performance of economies, with a focus on land use, housing, urban economic growth, housing finance and public finance. Each student identifies a research topic, defines the research question, and conducts original research as part of the course through a workshop format supervised by the instructor.

title: Regional Economics
description: The economics of subareas of nations with special emphasis on regional economic developments. Includes the theory of the location of industry.

title: Human Capital
description: "This course focuses on the acquisition of human capital, returns to investment in human capital, the determinants of the distribution of income, theory of employment discrimination, and earnings differences among race-sex groups. "

title: Seminar in the Economics of Population
description: This course examines theoretical and empirical treatment of the determinants of demographic behavior in less and more developed nations, the economic consequences of the behavior, and implications of both sets of findings for population and economics policy.

title: Applied Economic Demography
description: Applications of economic demography, including techniques for dealing with missing or incomplete data. Projections and estimates of population, demographic and economic characteristics. Determining the effects of demographic change on economic and social variables.

title: Mathematical Demography
description: This course is an introduction to the central analytical techniques of modern population study. Analysis including stable population theory and indirect estimation, continuous and discrete time formulations are considered; generalizations of the standard model with fixed mortality and fertility are also examined. Parametric models of fertility, mortality, stable populations, and the curve-fitting techniques underlying these approaches are reviewed.

title: Population Data
description: This course is an introduction to fundamental demographic data, measures, and methods. This required first-semester course for Master's students in Demography introduces basic vocabulary, standard methodology, and standard data repositories used by applied and academic demographers. Lectures and problem sets require students to practice on realistic applications using current demographic data.

title: Labor Markets
description: This course covers the following primary topics: the determinants of labor demand and supply, wage differentials, human capital, the operation of labor markets, labor mobility, and the dynamics of labor markets.

title: LABOR MARKETS
description: PRIMARY TOPICS INCLUDE DETERMINANTS OF LABOR DEMAND/SUPPLY;  WAGE DIFFERENTIALS;UNIONS/OPERATION OF LABOR MARKETS;LABOR                    MOBILITY;DYNAMICS OF LABOR MARKETS.                         0

title: Labor Compensation, Contracts, and Collective Bargaining
description: Focuses on the structure and level of compensation, economic analysis of unions, and on public-sector labor markets.

title: Natural Resource Economics I
description: "Theory of optimal resource use. Tests of resource scarcity. Case studies of fisheries economics, environmental economics, and the economics of outdoor recreation. "

title: Natural Resource Economics II
description: "Capital theory. Benefit cost analysis. The structure of resource industries. Case studies of energy, forestry, and water resources. Critique of natural resource policy. "

title: Social Control of Business
description: This course focuses on the role of the state in establishing the framework of the market economy; including enforced competition, regulated industries, and nationalized industries.

title: Law and Economics
description: This course immerses students in the literature on "law and economics" including the seminal contributions to this field. The differences between the most important "schools" of thought (approaches to the analysis of law and economics) are examined in the process of an exploration of the economic analysis of property law, contract law, tort law, and criminal law. The impact of economic incentives and objectives is explored, as well as objectives on the procedures of each on economic behavior. Students also engage in research by applying an economic approach to study and write about an issue in law or legal processes.

title: Applied Health Economics
description: This course is designed to teach students to work with large survey data sets, especially panel data. Upon completion of the course, students have an extensive familiarity with Stata. They should be able to display data visually, tabulate information, and run multivariate panel regressions.

title: Health Policy Statistics
description: This course introduces students to quantitative research methods and analytical techniques. Upon completion of the course, students should be able to read empirical articles and conduct statistical analyses.

title: URBAN ECONOMICS
description: URBAN DEVELOPMENT;LOCATION THEORY;HOUSING AND TRANSPORTATION ECONOMICS;LOCAL PUBLIC FINANCE; AND OTHER URBAN PROBLEMS ARE                  ANALYZED IN THE CONTEXT OF PUBLIC POLICY.                   0

title: REGIONAL ECONOMICS
description: EMPHASIZE THEORIES/POLICIES REGARDING REGIONAL ECONOMIC      GROWTH/DEVELOPMENT.INCLUDES ELEMENTS OF LOCATION THEORY                       FUNDAMENTALS TO UNDERSTANDING URBAN/REGIONAL ECONOMICS.     0

title: Economic Concepts for Business Decisions
description: An examination of fundamentals underlying business activity as related to the process of price management and decision making.

title: Economic Concepts for Business Decisions
description: An examination of fundamentals underlying business activity as related to the process of price management and decision making.

title: Economic Analysis for Management
description: This course is an examination of managerial concepts underlying business activity as related to the production of management and the process of decision making.

title: Personnel Economics
description: This course applies the tools of modern economics (e.g., game theory, econometrics, lab and field experiments) to the traditional topics of human resource management. Topics cover the design of optimal incentive mechanisms, but also norms, teamwork, and peer relationships at the workplace.

title: Labor Policy and Analysis
description: This course examines the theoretical and empirical research literature related to labor policy. In particular, students examine theoretical and empirical issues related to the wage and employment effects.

title: Comparative Economic Systems
description: This course focuses on theoretical and practical aspects of the principal forms of economic organization. Emphasis on the varieties of market-based systems, and economies in transition from centrally-planned toward market economies.

title: Social Entrepreneurship and Economic Development
description: This seminar incorporates the practical realities of economic development program implementation into the classroom, using a discussion format and case studies of real world applications. The readings are used to provide a broader context for the discussion of specific cases and more general theories. An emphasis is placed on "lessons learned" and discussion of the constraints and potential for implementing effective economic development programs in low-income areas of the United States and world, with a special focus on cities.

title: Economics of Asia
description: This course is a survey of economic development in the economies of East Asia. The course includes an economic analysis of the factors that contributed to the substantial growth in East Asia from 1960-1989 and the subsequent financial crisis that ensued in the 1990s.

title: Economics of Native Americans
description: This course examines and challenges the traditional view that the economic systems of Native Americans before Europeans arrived were communal. The historical evolution of Native economies is considered in light of the evolving relationships (both conflict and cooperation) between Europeans and Natives. Finally, the economic conditions of modern Native American communities are examined.

title: Economics of Development
description: This course provides a general introduction to development economics, a subject that studies the economic transformation of developing countries. It first presents the main definitions of economic growth and economic development and the most important theories that try to explain the development process of less developed countries and then focuses on key issues related to developing countries.

title: Transition of Soviet and Eastern European Economies
description: This course describes and analyzes the formerly centrally planned soviet economy and its transformation. Case studies include Russia, members of the Commonwealth of Independent States, and Eastern European countries. Analyzes the successes and failures of the transition process, particularly privatization and marketization.

title: Economics of the Caribbean
description: This course provides a detailed introduction to the analysis of economic development of Caribbean countries, including a discussion of the basic characteristics of Caribbean economies, a discussion of alternative theories and models of development, as well as a range of particular economic and social issues of concern to policy makers within Caribbean countries. Focus is on the actions available to Caribbean nations for addressing their development concerns within their region and/or country.

title: Economics of the Middle East
description: This course discusses the economic history of the Middle East and reviews events and policies bringing these nations to their current positions and examines prospects for future economic growth.

title: Seminar in Comparative Economics Systems
description: This course examines the utilization of basic economic tools and concepts to analyze efficiency and optimality considerations of various economic systems. Both theoretical models of economic systems and actual case studies are utilized.

title: Economic Development: Theory and Problems
description: This course dicusses the overall determinants of pace and structure of development, and specific issues, e.g., industrialization, human resources, foreign sector, income distribution, rural development, technology, etc.

title: Economic Development: Policy and Programming
description: "Strategic economic choices in developing countries, macro and micro planning techniques, project analysis, policy instruments to influence development. "

title: Economies in Transition
description: The analytical focus of this course is concentrated on the most important features of transition from centrally planned command economies toward market economic systems.

title: Economic Development of Southeast Europe
description: This course covers topics such as industrialization, agriculture, foreign trade, economic integration, prospects, with emphasis on Yugoslavia and the system of worker self-management.

title: Internship
description: May be repeated to a maximum of twelve semester hours.

title: Organization and Control of Education
description: This course examines relationships among local school districts, intermediate units, state departments of education, and the federal government.

title: DIRECTED INDIVIDUAL STUDY
description: MAY BE REPEATED TO A MAXIMUM OF 8 SEMESTER HOURS.                                                                                                                                                      0

title: Introduction to Leadership Development
description: This course allows students to consider and apply leadership development models and the competencies required of educational leaders and relates thosemodels to the field of practice.

title: Educational Administration
description: Fundamental concepts of the administrative process; administrative organization; communication; decision making; leadership.

title: Ethics in Educational Leadership
description: This course examines educational leaderships as an ethical endeavor; covers the assumptions, values, and beliefs that inform school practice and policies; discusses systemic constraints to educational leadership; and also covers social-justice concerns in education.

title: EDUCATIONAL SYSTEMS ANALYSIS
description: INTRODUCTION TO OPERATIONS RESEARCH TECHNIQUES APPLIED TO EDUCATIONAL DATA;FITTING CURVES TO ADMINISTRATIVE DATA;MATHEMATICAL MODELING;INTERPRETATION OF QUANTITATIVE DATA.                            0

title: Educational Leadership and Change
description: This course is designed to teach aspiring educational leaders how to lead change in an educational setting. Students are exposed to comprehensive tools that are grounded in research that lead to the transformation of schools.

title: Educational Management Development
description: This course presents history, rationale, and current status of educational management development (EMD) in Florida. Studies management competencies, acquisition, and their assessment; establishes individualized growth planning. Relates EMD to effective schools and school improvement; addresses implications for prospective administrators and supervisors.

title: Leadership for Diversity
description: This course integrates DOE requirements of ESOL Standards for School Administrators with a) an understanding of the Consent Decree, accountability and equity issues related to LEP students; b) an understanding of compliance with federal and state regulations; and c) an understanding of cultural proficiency in the school environment.

title: Educational Leadership
description: This course covers basic leadership theories, motivation, group dynamics, planning, and change processes in educational settings. The course emphasizes knowledge, analysis, and applications that draw from multidisciplinary perspectives, including organizational analysis, psychology, anthropology, and sociology.

title: FISCAL MANAGEMENT IN EDUCATION
description: BUDGETARY PROCESS; FINANCIAL ACCOUNTING AND REPORTING; COST ANALYSIS; DEBT MANAGEMENT; CENTRAL SERVICES.                                                                                               0

title: Application of Leadership Theory
description: This course applies leadership theory to problems of practice through role plays and through models of reflective practice.

title: Resource Management for Educational Leaders
description: This course examines public education as an economic institution, emphasizing the relationship between the purposes of schooling and the human and fiscal-resource allocation role of the principal. The principal's role in selected strategies and techniques in critical thinking and problem solving as applied to school improvement are presented. Procedures involved in school funding are examined, as well as the role of the principal in implementing statutes, audits, procedures, and policies. Recruitment, selection, retention of school personnel, and collective bargaining are examined as they relate to state and federal law.

title: Personnel Administration in Education
description: This course emphasizes the theoretical and practical aspects of planning, implementing, and evaluating functions in education. Special attention is given to the role of the building principal in personnel administration and collective bargaining.

title: ADMINISTRATION OF LABOR RELATIONS AND COLLECTIVE                                    BARGAINING IN ED
description: COURSE PROVIDES INDEPTH VIEW OF LEGAL ASPECTS OF EDUCATIONAL LABOR RELATIONS;NEGOTIATIONS STRATEGIES;ROLE OF THIRD PARTY                   NEUTRALS AND PROCESS OF COLLECTIVE BARGAINING.

title: The Role of the Woman Administrator in Education
description: This course focuses on basic understandings of the role of the woman administrator in education, with focus upon her preparation and performance as reflected in the literature.

title: Applications of Policy
description: This course explores the roots of the educational process, the role of different stakeholders in policy formation and implementation, and applications of these educational policies in schools for the purpose of improving teaching and learning.

title: Legal Aspects of Public School Administration
description: This course is designed to enable students to identify and apply legal principles that place limits on authority, define individual and corporate liability and inform standards of educational practice in public school settings. It emphasizes knowledge, analysis, and application that explores a range of leadership competencies, including concept formation, organizational sensitivity, problem solving and decisiveness. The course includes readings offering an overview of key legal and ethical issues for school administrators and case scenarios designed for small group and individual analysis.

title: School Finance
description: This course examines public education as an economic institution. The sources and methods of distribution of public school revenue at the various levels of government. The social-economic-political context in which public finance decisions are evolved and their relationship to current educational issues.

title: The Politics of Education
description: This course is an introduction to the study of the nation's largest social institution, public education. Using concepts based in the discipline of political science, the course explores how ideologies, institutions, and social groups have interacted to shape formal schooling in the United States. Class discussions and readings focus on the distribution of power and leverage in the political process of American society and the utilization of communication and analytic skills by educational administrators and policy analysts.

title: DECISION-ORIENTED EDUCATIONAL RESEARCH   CHG TO     EDA5423
description: CONCEPTS AND METHODS FOR PRODUCING INFORMATION FOR THE PURPOSE OF SCHOOL IMPROVEMENT.

title: Applied Data Analysis and Assessment for Educational Leaders
description: This course provides participants with skills and knowledge in using the results of screening, diagnosis, process monitoring and outcome reading assessments to guide instructional decision-making in grades K-12, as described in Florida's Reading Program Specification 3.3 and 3.4. Topics include differentiating reading instruction, flexible grouping and assessment-driven intervention, including the selection of core and supplemental reading programs and appropriate instructional strategies.

title: Data Driven School Improvement
description: This course builds expertise in using data for a variety of school-improvement purposes, including instructional decision-making in grades K-12. Collaborative action-research skills are developed to solve school-based problems.

title: The Assistant Principal
description: This course provides an overview of research on the position of the K-12 Assistant Principal. The course addresses issues of role ambiguity, socialization, ethics, discipline, instruction, and various aspects of personnel management.

title: INSTRUCTIONAL PROBLEMS AND SCHOOL ORGANIZATION
description: INTERACTIONS OF CLASSROOM MANAGEMENT WITH SCHOOL;DISTRICTS   AND COMMUNITY GOALS;EXPECTATIONS; ORGANIZATIONAL                              CHARACTERISTICS.                                            0

title: The Principal Leadership for Vision and Mission
description: This course prepares aspiring principals to analyze gaps between current school performance and a desired future state, develop leadership strategies to build collective ownership, and communicate the vision with clarity and purpose. Effective educational leaders collaborate with parents, students, and other stakeholders to develop, communicate, and enact a shared vision, mission, and core values to promote the academic success and well-being of all students. They apply knowledge of effective, data-driven decision-making processes grounded in research, best practices, and leadership theory to support the vision, mission, and improvement priorities of schools. Emphasis is placed on coherence between school vision and instructional systems, and stakeholder engagement to promote sustained school improvement. Students also demonstrate their understanding of the FELS 1-8 through a practical internship with a supervising principal.

title: Leadership for Building Professional Capacity
description: Effective educational leaders build the collective and individual professional capacity of school personnel by creating support systems and offering professional learning to promote the academic success and well-being of all students. This course prepares candidates to recruit, support, and retain a high-performing faculty through research-based strategies. Students will examine professional learning systems, coaching and feedback cycles, adult learning principles, and collaborative communities of growth. Emphasis is placed on the development and implementation of professional development plans aligned to school goals and student learning outcomes.

title: Organization and Administration of Higher Education
description: "Basic principles of organization and administration in junior colleges, senior colleges, and universities."

title: Planning Effective Instruction
description: This course explores the components and relationships that make up effective classroom instruction. Working on the assumption that effective classroom instruction is a necessary foundation for student achievement, school leaders need to know how to coach and monitor teachers use of effective instructional practices.

title: Teacher Leadership Development
description: This course is based on the increasingly important role of a school leader in identifying and developing teacher leaders in K-12 schools. School leaders need to be able to work with teacher teams effectively and to cultivate the talents of teachers as leaders in various aspects of school life. This course explores how school leaders can be prepared to successfully operationalize these dynamics.

title: State Education Policy
description: This course examines the development of education policy through the state legislature, state boards of education, and the state budgeting process. Emphasizes eclectic research methods in the conduct of limited scope educational policy studies at the state level.

title: APPLICATION OF LEADERSHIP THEORY
description: STUDENTS APPLY LEADERSHIP THEORY TO PROBLEMS OF PRACTICE THROUGH ROLE PLAYS AND MODELS OF REFELCTIVE PRACTICE.

title: Directed Individual Study
description: May be repeated to a maximum of five semester hours.

title: Special Topics in Educational Administration
description: This course content varies to provide opportunity to study current issues in educational administration and topics not offered in other courses. May be repeated as topics vary to a maximum of twelve semester hours.

title: Supervised Teaching
description: May be repeated to a maximum of five semester hours. A maximum of three hours may apply to the master's degree.

title: Practical Experiences in Educational Leadership
description: This practicum course's primary purpose is to provide students an experiential orientation into the components for fulfilling certification requirements in Educational Leadership/Administration toward Level I Educational Leadership Certification. The practicum provides the infrastructure that bridges leadership practice with leadership theory as students acquire the skills, knowledge, and dispositions to make a positive impact on improving schools and student achievement.

title: Practicum in Educational Leadership I
description: This course integrates Department of Education requirements of experiential learning through field experiences, school-based mentoring from an expert in the field, and the Florida Leaders Web site for professional development for aspiring school leaders.

title: Practicum in Educational Leadership II
description: This course applies experiential learning and mentoring in a school context under the guidance of an expert in the field. Particular attention is paid to issues of diversity, decision-making, and effective communication.

title: Practicum in Educational Leadership III
description: This course provides interaction with experts in the field, reflection and evaluation of oneself as a leader, participating in on-going mentorship experiences, and studying contemporary topics in educational leadership.

title: Educational Administration and Organizational Practice
description: This course provides an introduction to educational administration and organizational practice for graduate students interested in education and policy. The course is composed of a social-systems model of which critical elements such as structure, motivation, culture and politics are covered. Opportunities and constraints for schools are explored along with key administrative processes and contemporary research on the effectiveness of schools.

title: Education Policy to Practice for Educational Practitioners
description: This course provides a broad overview of the field of K-12 educational policy. Students will learn central theories that have been used to understand the goals and mechanisms of different state and federal policy efforts aimed at improving schools and schooling.

title: Organizational Theory
description: This course is an overview of organizational concepts and theories to enable the advanced graduate student to develop alternative bases for utilizing organizational theory in future study and practice within educational settings.

title: Perspectives on Leadership Theory
description: This course examines traditional and non-traditional education leadership theories, including analyses of purposes and meanings inherent in formal and informal perspectives.

title: Laboratory of Practice I
description: This course re-introduces students to key concepts/foundations of research design relevant to their dissertation. Students continue revisions on Chapter 1 of their dissertation and conduct an audit of their local context and/or organization.

title: Laboratory of Practice II
description: This course facilitates students’ ability to engender original thinking and research on important educational issues as they relate to issues of human rights and social justice in local and global contexts.

title: Laboratory of Practice III
description: In this third and final Laboratory of Practice course, students revise and finalize their preliminary exam manuscripts and supplemental materials, including IRB applications. As part of this process, students conduct a pilot study testing instruments or protocols that they may use to collect data.

title: Leading Learning
description: This course presents a rationale, history, and the current research and practice of instructional leadership and the skills required among the students. This investigation takes place within the context of school accountability and federal guidelines.

title: Leadership for School Renewal
description: This course assists students in developing catalytic leadership for creating a vision description of totalquality school/school district and a strategic plan for realizing that vision.

title: INTRODUCTION TO ECONOMICS OF EDUCTION
description: Course Description not on file

title: ECONOMIC RESEARCH IN EDUCATION
description: A SEMINAR IN NEW AND EMERGING ECONOMIC PROBLEMS CONFRONTING ADMINISTRATORS IN VARIOUS EDUCATIONAL INSTITUTIONS                                                                                         0

title: FORECASTING TECHNIQUES FOR EDUCATIONAL PLANNING
description: INCLUDES CURVE-FITTING, COHORT SURVIVAL METHOD, DELPHI, AND REGRESSION METHODS.                                                                                                                        0

title: Research in Schools
description: This course provides students with the knowledge and skills required to conduct systematic, empirical research in Pre-K-12 school settings. Working in collaboration with superintendents, principals, teachers, and other practitioners, students identify one or more research questions addressing issues of critical importance to educators in the field.

title: Literature Review for Educational Research
description: This course is designed to guide advanced doctoral students through the research process, including identifying and developing a research interest, refining research questions, and writing a review of the literature.

title: Seminar in Literature, Research and Professional Writing
description: This course is a weekly seminar on current educational problems. May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours.

title: Internship in Educational Administration
description: This internship allows field experience in administration, including supervision and curriculum. May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours.

title: Diagnostic Exam
description: This diagnostic exam appraises the student's ability to pursue the EdD doctoral degree and to facilitate advising in the development of the student's program of studies. The diagnostic exam is taken during the second semester or after a doctoral student has completed nine to twelve hours of EdD coursework.

title: Dissertation
description: This course consists of research on the dissertation topic. A minimum of twenty-four credit hours is required.May be repeated within the same term.

title: Preliminary Doctoral Examination
description: This preliminary examination determines if students have mastered the content area and are prepared to plan and conduct independent and scholarly research. Upon successful completion of the preliminary examination, students are admitted to candidacy and may begin taking dissertation hours. May be repeated within the same term. May be completed two times.

title: Master's Comprehensive Examination
description: Course Description not on file

title: Specialist in Education Comprehensive Examination
description: Course Description not on file

title: Dissertation Defense
description: Course Description not on file

title: COOPERATIVE EDUCATION WORK EXPERIENCE
description: Course Description not on file

title: CAREERS IN ELEMENTARY EDUCATION
description: TO PROVIDE PRE-EDUCATION STUDENT WITH DIRECT EXPERIENCE BASIS FOR DECISION TO ENTER ELEMENTARY TEACHER EDUCATION.                                                                                      0

title: The Teacher in the Elementary and Middle School
description: This course is an investigation of the role of the teacher from perspectives of planning, curriculum, organization, management, and problem solving.

title: The Child in the Elementary and Middle School
description: This course is an investigation of children from diverse cultural settings in the context of elementary school learning, curriculum, and teaching.

title: THE CHILD IN THE ELEMENTARY AND MIDDLE SCHOOL.      CHG TO EDE3322
description: INVESTIGATION OF CHILDREN FROM DIVERSE CULTURAL SETTINGS IN  THE CONTEXT OF ELEMENTARY SCHOOL LEARNING, CURRICULUM,                     AND TEACHING.

title: Literacy Assessment and Instruction
description: This course is designed to provide students with competencies in the use and development of assessments and in using assessments to inform instruction. Emphasis is placed on the administration of formal instruments and informal assessment procedures. This course addresses the Florida Educator Accomplished Practices and Competency 3 of the Florida Reading Endorsement.

title: Differentiating Reading and Content Area Literacy Instruction
description: This course addresses research-based literacy strategies and content essential for differentiating reading instruction across content areas, using assessments to inform instruction, and implementing tiers of intervention.

title: Technology and Learning for Elementary and Middle School
description: Designed to help preprofessional teachers use technology for the development of higher learning skills. Included in the technology content will be animated graphics, scanned pictures, and linked files. Students will be working with students in schools applying concepts from the class. Students will acquire technology skills that will allow them to develop electronic professional portfolios.

title: Directed Individual Study
description: May be repeated to a maximum of twelve semester hours.

title: Directed Field Experiences
description: This course requires participation in a public school classroom with University supervision. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve semester hours, to be taken in the following manner: at least one hour in Block I, one hour in Block II, and two hours in Block III.

title: Student Teaching in Elementary Education
description: This course provides teacher candidates with a controlled transition to the role of professional educator; provides a laboratory setting in which critical teaching behaviors may be systematically developed and demonstrated; provides the elementary education faculty and the profession with an opportunity to assess the development and the needs of the teacher candidate; and provides a situation in which the remaining needs can be met. May be repeated to a maximum of 12 credit hours.

title: Honors in the Major Research
description: In this course, students accepted into the Honors in the Major program complete an original research or creative project in their major area of study. This course must be repeated at least twice to complete a minimum of six credit hours total, but may be repeated to a maximum of 12 credit hours.

title: The Elementary School, K-6
description: This course examines the foundations for establishing an elementary school program, including the nature of knowledge, social issues, child development, and content development.

title: The Integrated Curriculum in the Elementary and Middle School
description: This course analyzes the reasons for integrating the curriculum and teaches how to implement an integrated approach in the elementary and middle schools.

title: Current Issues and Trends in Elementary Education
description: This course is designed for students to perform a critical analysis of a number of issues and trends important to the public elementary school. May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours.

title: Current Issues and Trends in Elementary Education

Topics include flexible grouping, instructional and curricular accommodations, using assessment to inform instruction and implementing tiers of intervention.

title: Technology in Elementary and Middle School
description: This course is designed to help professional teachers use technology for the development of higher-order thinking. Emphasis is given to current trends and issues in technology, such as Hypermedia and Internet. Teachers develop plans for their own classes that are consistent with recommendations for school improvement.

title: Organization for Classroom Instruction in the Elementary School
description: This course is an analysis and critique of current organizational patterns related to teaching in the elementary school.

title: Systematic Procedures of Observation

title: Supervised Research

title: Internship in Elementary Teaching
description: This culminating internship provides teacher candidates the opportunity to demonstrate mastery of the state-approved teacher preparation standards in a classroom setting, focusing on the areas of planning, instructional delivery, assessment, professional growth, and ethical behavior.

title: Elementary Teaching Field Practicum
description: This course provides teacher candidates with the opportunity to participate in an elementary classroom, focusing specifically on curriculum design and instruction, classroom management and discipline, and assessment.

title: Perspectives of Teacher Professional Development
description: This course is for advanced graduate students preparing for leadership positions associated with professional development of teachers at pre-service, induction, and in-service levels. Model programs are viewed from historical, sociological, psychological, philosophical, and anthropological perspectives.

title: Doctoral Seminar in Elementary Education
description: This seminar was developed to explore a variety of topics related to childhood education, curriculum, teacher education, and other areas relevant to professional preparation and thought. May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours.

title: RESEARCH SEMINAR IN ELEMENTARY EDUCATION
description: COURSE TO FACILITATE STUDENT PROGRESS ON BEGINNING DOCTORAL  DISSERTATION.

title: Advanced Research Seminar in Elementary Education
description: This seminar is to assist students to master tasks required for a prospectus of a dissertation.
