description: This course discusses central theories and topics in developmental psychology across the life span, focusing especially on the implications of developmental theory and empirical research on counseling and other helping professions.

title: Group Processes in Instruction
description: This course examines the theory, research, and practice in interpersonal interaction, group dynamics, and management of group processes in the classroom and school setting. Topics include group development, leadership, conflict management, organizational dynamics, values.

title: Topics in Educational Psychology
description: This course surveys major theories with respect to students' learning, motivation, individual differences in abilities, and development of cognitive  and social skills as they apply to instructional decision making.  The course components enable students to put research into practice and balance theoretical and practical perspectives of students' learning and teachers' practices.

title: SEMINAR IN EDUCATIONAL PROCESSES AND SCHOOL                                       PSYCHOLOGY
description: SELECTED TOPICS PERTAINING TO PREVENTION; DIAGNOSIS; AND     RESEARCH QUESTIONS IN SCHOOL SYSTEMS.                                         REPEATABLE NINE HOURS MAXIMUM.                              0

title: Special Topics in Educational Psychology
description: This course is an in-depth investigation of a variety of topics in educational psychology with different topics offered each year.

title: Masters Comprehensive Examination
description: This comprehensive examination gives students an opportunity to reflect on the competencies acquired in the program and to provide evidence of acquired skills by preparing a professional portfolio consisting of various work products, deliverables, and other artifacts completed in their courses, internship, and other settings.

title: Preliminary Doctoral Exam
description: This preliminary examination determines if students have mastered the content area and are prepared to plan and conduct independent and scholarly research. Upon successful completion of the preliminary examination, students are admitted to candidacy and may begin taking dissertation hours.

title: SUPERVISION OF INSTRUCTION:CONCEPTS AND ISSUES
description: INTRODUCTION TO FIELD OF INSTRUCTIONAL SUPERVISION-ITS NATURE AND SCOPE,FOUNDATIONS,MAJOR FUNCTIONS,BASIC CONCEPTS,FRONTIERS,AND CURRENT ISSUES.                                                       0

title: SUPERVISION:TASKS AND PROCESSES
description: SUPERVISION:  IDENTIFICATION OF TASKS; ANALYSIS OF VARIED COMPETENCIES NEEDED BY MEMBERS OF SUPERVISORY TEAM; REAL AND SIMULATED EXPERIENCES; DESIGN OF PROGRAMS; AND PROBLEMS.                        0

title: Supervision of Associate Teaching
description: This course focuses on the function of public schools in teacher education programs, basic knowledge and skills needed by classroom teachers to become effective supervising teachers. Emphasis given to the Florida Performance Measurement System/Beginning Teacher Program. Practical laboratory experience included.

title: Administration and Supervision of Clinical Education
description: May be taken with                                                         EDS 5942r.

title: PRACTICUM:ADMINISTRATION AND SUPERVISION OF FIELD EXPERIENCES
description: MAY BE REPEATED FOR A MAXIMUM OF 9 SEMESTER HOURS.                                                                                                                                                     0

title: ORIENTATION TO EARLY CHILDHOOD EDUCATION
description: INTRODUCES PROSPECTIVE TEACHERS OF YOUNG CHILDREN TO THE     EARLY CHILDHOOD FIELD AND OFFERS OPPORTUNITIES TO DEMONSTRATERELEVANT ENTRY COMPETENCIES IN RELATED DISCIPLINES & SELF-EVALUATION TECH.   0

title: Early Childhood Education Curriculum
description: This course examines the design and implementation of thematic curricula and direct instruction appropriate for children age 3 to grade 3. Microteaching required.

title: Early Childhood Education Foundations
description: This course introduces students to ECE primary education and provides a background of ECE theory and research to be used for determining education practices.

title: Expressive Arts for the Young Child
description: This course examines the role and value of the arts for the child, while exploring developmental trends, appropriate practices, methods, media and curricula of music, art, movement, dance, and drama.

title: Parents as Teachers
description: This course is an examination of the need and importance of parental involvement in the education of young children. Includes strategies for promoting home/school interactions.

title: Techniques of Child Study and Authentic Assessment
description: In this course, students investigate and utilize data collection techniques/instruments to acquire information about young children.

title: Observation and Participation in Early Childhood Education
description: This course offers students direct experiences with young children, organized in such a way that they have opportunities to put into practice those insights, principles, and understandings gained in the theory courses. May be repeated to a maximum of eight credit hours.

title: Special Topics in Early Childhood
description: In this course, current topics in early childhood are studied in depth. May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours. May be repeated during the same semester.

title: Student Teaching in Early Childhood Education
description: Course Description not on file

title: Thematic Curriculum and Direct Instruction for Young Children
description: This is one of three courses designed to provide theory/research bases for the development of curriculum and practices for educating children age 3 tograde 3. The course focuses on thematic curriculum and direct instruction.

title: Curriculum and Play for Young Children
description: One of a three-course series designed to provide theory/research bases for the development of appropriate curriculum and practices for educating children ages 3 years to grade 3. This course focuses on active learning through play.

title: Curriculum and Play for Young Children
description: This is one of a three-course series designed to provide theory/research bases for the development of appropriate curriculum and practices for educating children age 3 years to grade 3. The course focuses on active learning through play.

title: Methods and Experiences with Young Children and Families
description: Provides direct experiences in working with young children and families and requires seminar attendance and field placement with young children.

title: Methods and Experiences with Young Children and Families
description: This course provides direct experiences in working with young children and families and requires seminar attendance and field placement with young children.

title: Teachers and Parents: Partners in Education
description: This course focuses on the effects of parental involvement on children's educational development and achievements; designing/implementing strategies for enhancing parent-teacher partnership in education.

title: Children's Centers
description: Investigate the basic principles involved in establishing and operating centers for the young child.

title: Techniques of Classroom Management and Child Study
description: This course identifies and analyzes theories, programs, and essential components in classroom management. Explores techniques for classroom teachers to use in developing a child study with emphasis on educational implications.

title: Issues and Trends in Early Childhood Education
description: This course identifies issues and trends in the area of early childhood education and addresses possible causes and relationships.

title: Historical and Theoretical Bases of Early Childhood Education
description: This course compares, analyzes, and synthesizes the different philosophical and psychological theories that form the foundation of early childhood education programs and practices. It also studies the historical events that influenced the direction and nature of the care and education of young children.

title: Research in Early Childhood Education
description: This course comprehensively investigates the field through surveying, delineating, searching, and synthesizing research in early childhood education.

title: DEVELOPMENT OF COGNITIVE SKILLS IN EARLY CHILDHOOD                            EDUCATION
description: DRAWS ON CHILD DEVELOPMENT THEORY TO GIVE THEORETICAL SUPPORTFOR THE DEVELOPMENT OF COGNITIVE CURRICULUM FOR YOUNG CHILD-                  REN.

title: Special Topics in Early Childhood Education
description: This course provides an in-depth examination of topics related to early childhood. May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours.

title: Field Laboratory Internship
description: Course Description not on file

title: Thesis
description: A minimum of six semester hours is required.

title: EARLY CHILD CARE EDUCATION
description: EDUCATIONAL IDEAS AND WORLDWIDE PRACTICES STUDIED TO DEVELOP SELF-RENEWING CURRICULUM AND EFFECTIVE METHODS OF CHILD CARE FOR CHILDREN 0-8 YEARS.                                                      0

title: Educational Environments for Infants and Toddlers
description: This course updates research in first years of life to kinds of environment and learning experiences which promote and insure optimum development.

title: Theory and Research in Young Children's Play Curriculum
description: This course is a seminar on the advanced study of young children's play and curriculum.

title: Theory and Research in Young Childrens Play Curriculum
description: Seminar on the advanced study of young children's play and curriculum.

title: Doctoral Seminar in Early Childhood Education
description: Course Description not on file

title: Master's Thesis Defense
description: Course Description not on file

title: Specialist in Education Thesis Defense
description: Course Description not on file

title: Practicum in Emotional Disturbance/Learning Disability
description: Observation and participation with LD/ED children in public and private settings.

title: Electronics I
description: This course covers diode models and circuits, DC biasing of bipolar-junction and field-effect transistors, small- and large-signal transistor models, and frequency analysis of single-stage AC amplifiers.

title: Electronics I Laboratory
description: This laboratory supports EEE 3300, Electronics.

title: Biomimetic Sensors and Signal Processing
description: In this course, biomimetic implies the mimicry of biology. This course covers biologically-inspired structure and function concepts used for novel sensor designs and signal processing. Cursory descriptions of biological phenomena are followed by electronic sensor designs and natural signal processing algorithms. This course focuses on natural sensory systems and innovative engineering applications inspired by them.

title: Electronics II
description: This course uses computer-aided design programs and covers multistage amplifier analysis and design. The course focuses on feedback and operational amplifiers, A-to-D and D-to-A converters, and waveshaping and waveforming generators, including oscillators, voltage regulators, and power circuits.

title: Electronics II Lab
description: This course is an advanced electronic laboratory.

title: CMOS Digital IC Design
description: This is an elective course designed to introduce students to the design of CMOS digital IC circuits using IC layout techniques.

title: Microelectronics Engineering
description: This course covers design and fabrication of solid-state devices. Topics include oxidation, diffusion, metallization, photolithography, and device characterization.

title: Solid-State Electronic Devices
description: This course covers solid-state physics as applied to electronic devices. The course focuses on semiconductor materials, conduction process in solids, device fabrication, diffusion processes, and negative conduction devices.

title: Feedback Amplifier Principles
description: This course introduces basic concepts of multi-stage audio-frequency amplifiers, including feedback and stability principles and power-supply criteria.

title: Introduction to Analog Integrated Circuit Design
description: This course covers the design and analysis of bipolar and MOS analog integrated circuits. The course focuses on operational amplifier design, analog multipliers, active loads, current sources, and active filters.

title: Mixed Signal Integrated Circuits
description: This course introduces mixed-signal processing using analog and digital integrated circuits. The course focuses on fundamentals of sampled data systems, nonlinear and dynamic analog circuits, Nyquist-rate data converters, over-sampling data converters and digital filters, as well as the use of computer-aided design programs.

title: Modeling and Simulation of Semiconductor Devices
description: This course covers various numerical techniques for the modeling and simulation of semiconductor devices, such as pn-junctions, metal-oxide semiconductor contacts, metal-oxide-semiconductor field effect transistors, and bipolar devices. Special emphasis is on the description and simulation of electron and hole transport in semiconductor devices.

title: Digital Signal Processing
description: This course covers topics such as sinusoids, periodic signals, and Fourier spectra. Sampling of continuous-time signals, aliasing. Impulse response of linear, discrete-time systems, convolution. FIR filters and implementation. Frequency response of FIR filters. Z-transforms. IIR filters, poles and zeros, frequency response. Realization of IIR filters. Discrete Fourier transform and the FFT algorithm. MATLAB exercises are assigned.

title: Principles of Communications Systems
description: This course offers an introduction to Fourier analysis of noise and signals; information transmission; modulation techniques; AM, FM, and pulse; as well as analog multiplexing.

title: Radar
description: This course examines basic concepts of radar systems including radar range equation, radar cross-section calculcations, random processes and noise, array antennas, beamsteering, doppler and range processing, FM and CW systems, pulse compression, synthetic aperture radar, and clutter.

title: Machine Learning
description: This course is designed for senior undergraduate students from engineering disciplines and introduces students to the theory and engineering applications of machine learning including neural networks, fuzzy logic, genetic algorithms, supervised and unsupervised learning algorithms. This course places emphasis on engineering applications in controls, power systems, and robotics.

title: Artificial Intelligence
description: This course instructs students in basic artificial intelligence (AI) techniques of search, machine learning, natural language processing, robotics, and image processing. In this course, potential/current limitations are analyzed; as are human interaction in a decision-making environment.

title: Biomimetic Systems Theory
description: This course covers natural systems and signal-processing theory as revealed by biological sensory systems. Focus is on innovative engineering applications inspired by nature sensory systems. Application of the biomimetic theory is reinforced through a course project which demonstrates successful mimicry of a natural sensory system concept.

title: Digital integrated circuit design
description: This course covers the design of integrated circuits, applications, solid-state-device switching characteristics, memory, computer-aided design, and layout.

title: Power Electronics
description: This course helps students develop a basic understanding of using switched electronic circuits for the conversion and regulation of electric power. The course focuses on basic converters and their steady state analysis, and covers dynamic-modeling analysis, converter-controller design, power-semiconductor device, and converter simulation.

title: Solid State Sensors
description: This course covers the fabrication of solid-state sensors, their characterization, operational principles, and applications for acoustic, mechanical, magnetic, radiation, thermal, chemical, and biological sensors.

title: Analysis of Quantum Scale Semiconductor Devices
description: This course presents techniques for the analysis and simulation of nanometric scale semiconductor devices (SD), with focus on the analysis of quantum induced effects on the electronic trasport and characteristics of SD. Topics include generation-recombination processes in semiconductors, quantum and semiclassical modeling of SD, noise and fluctuations in SD, and numerical techniques for the simulation of SD.

title: Random Processes
description: This course cover topics such as random processes; analysis and processing of random signals; modeling of engineering systems by random processes; selected applications in detection; filtering; reliability analysis; and system performance modeling.

title: Semiconductor Device Theory
description: This course covers solid-state physics as applied to electronic devices, semiconductor materials, conduction processes in solids, device fabrication, diffusion processes, and semiconductor devices.

title: Digital Signal Processing I
description: This course discusses the fundamentals of digital signal processing and design of a variety of digital processors and filters. Introduction to DFT-FFTand spectral estimation theory and practice.

title: INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL ENGINEERING
description: INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL ENGINEERING CONCEPTS FOR NON ELECTRICAL ENGINEERING MAJORS.  BROAD RANGE OF TOPICS INCLUDING BASIC CIRCUIT THEORY;SEMICONDUCTOR DEVICES;MICROPROCESSORS;AMPLIFIERS;& MACHINES

title: INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL ENGINEERING LABORATORY
description: THIS COURSE ACCOMPANIES EEL2003  AND PROVIDES EXPERIMENTS IN ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS; AMPLIFIERS; INSTRUMENTATION AND MACHINESS

title: ECE Engineering Tools Lab
description: This is an introductory laboratory for students entering the electrical and computer engineering programs. The basic topics include: lab safety issues; solving engineering problems using software tools such as MATLAB and Mathematica; electric circuit simulations using c software packages such as Multisim and OrCad; electric circuit design and instrumentation; the proper use of test and measurement equipment.

title: Introduction to Electrical Engineering
description: This course is an introduction to electrical engineering concepts for non-electrical engineering majors. The course focuses on circuit theory for interfacing sensors and actuators. Operational Amplifiers are included. Not accepted for credit towards BSEE.

title: Introduction to Electrical Engineering Laboratory
description: This laboratory supports EEL 3003. Must be taken concurrently with first enrollment in EEL 3003. Must be dropped if EEL 3003 is dropped.

title: Statistical Topics in Electrical Engineering
description: Use of probability and statistical concepts in electrical engineering applications such as measurement uncertainty, linear regression, reliability, and noise in electrical systems. Elementary probability-sets, sample spaces, axioms, joint and conditional probability. Random variables-distribution and density functions. Operations in random variables-expectation, moments, transformation of random variables. Multiple random variables. Random processes-distribution and density functions, correlation functions, spectra characteristics. Elements of statistics: parameter estimation and hypothesis testing.

title: Circuit Analysis I
description: This course explores topics such as current, voltage, and power; resistors, inductors, and capacitors; network theorems and laws; operational amplifiers, phasors; impedances; sinusoidal steady-state analysis.

title: Circuit Analysis II
description: This course examines sinusoidal steady-state power analysis; three-phase circuits; operational amplifier; transient and forced response; frequency response; two-port networks; and circuit analysis.

title: Circuit Analysis II Lab
description: This laboratory provides hands-on experience to electrical engineering majors in the fundamental topics of circuit analysis. It reinforces concepts introduced in the associated lecture.

title: INTRODUCTORY CIRCUIT ANALYSIS
description: VOLT-AMPERE CHARACTERISTICS OR CIRCUIT ELEMENTS; IDEAL SOURCE;TRANSIENT RESPONSE OF RL; RC AND RLC CIRCUITS; SINUSOIDAL ANALYSIS AND COMPLEX FREQUENCY.

title: Signal and Linear System Analysis
description: This course focuses on the classification and representation of signals and systems; Laplace transform; Z-transform; convolution; state variable techniques; stability and feedback.

title: ADVANCED CIRCUITS WITH COMPUTERS
description: STEADY-STATE POWER ANALYSIS; POLYPHASE CIRCUITS; FREQUENCY RESPONSE; TRANSFORMERS; TWO-PART NETWORKS; FOURIER AND LAPLACE;ANALYSIS AND DESIGN WITH COMPUTER APPLICATIONS.

title: CIRCUITS LABORATORY WITH COMPUTERS
description: REISTOR COLOR CODES; RESISTIVE NETWORKS; CURRENT AND VOLTAGE MEASUREMENT; RL;RC AND RLC CIRCUITS; LISSAJOUS PATTERNS; FREQUENCY RESPONSE A.C. AND D.C. DESIGN; COMPUTER APPLICATION.

title: ELECTRICAL CIRCUITS
description: GRAPH THEORY AND NETWORK FUNCTIONS; POLES AND ZEROS          MULTITERMINAL AND MULTIPORT NETWORKS.

title: Fundamentals of Power Systems
description: This course is an introduction to the fundamentals of energy conversion; structure of power systems; and power system components: transformers, rotating machines, and transmission lines. The operation and analysis of power systems are presented.

title: Electromechanical Dynamics
description: Magnetic circuits; transformers; electromagnetic torques and induced voltages; induction motors; synchronous machines; DC motors.

title: Electronics
description: Diode models and circuits; DC biasing of bipolar-junction and field-effect transistors; small- and large-signal transistor models; frequency analysis of single-stage AC amplifiers.

title: ELECTRONICS
description: INTRO SEMICONDUCTOR PHYSICS, DEVICE CHARACTERISTICS, MODELS  OF DIODES, JUNCTION 7 FIELD-EFFECT TRANSITORS, ANALOG & DIGI-TAL CIRCUIT DESIGN W/BIASING & TEMP.

title: Electronics Laboratory
description: Laboratory in support of EEL 3300.

title: DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
description: INTRODUCTION TO DIGITAL LOGIC. NUMBERING SYSTEMS;BOOLEAN ALGEBRA;COMBINATIONAL LOGIC;REGISTERS;COUNTERS;DIGITAL INTEGRATED                 CIRCUIT TYPES.

title: DIGITAL ELECTRONICS LAB
description: Course Description not on file

title: Fundamentals of Electromagnetic Fields
description: This course explores many topics: transmission lines; the electrostatic field; Gauss’ law; boundary conditions; capacitance; Laplace’s and Poisson’s equations; energy, forces, and torques; the steady electric current; the magnetostatic field; vector potential; Ampere’s and Biot-Savart laws; inductance; energy, forces, and torques; quasistatic fields; electromagnetic induction; and electromagnetic wave propagation.

title: Electromagnetic Fields II
description: This course examines topics such as Maxwell's equations, plane electromagnetic waves, group velocity, polarization, Poynting vector, boundary conditions, reflection and refraction of plane waves, skin effect, transmission line analysis, impedance matching, wave guides and cavity resonators, fundamentals of radiation and antennas.

title: Introduction to Communications
description: This course discusses topics such as signal analysis, Fourier series/Fourier transform, sampling theorem, distortions and attenuation in signal transmission, and analog modulation AM, FM, pulse-code modulation, and pulse shaping.

title: Digital Logic Design
description: This course covers fundamental topics in digital logic design including the use of a hardware description language, as well as number systems, Boolean algebra, logic simplification, combinational and sequential logic circuits.

title: Digital Logic Laboratory
description: This laboratory supports EEL 3705. This course introduces Electrical and Computer Engineering majors to various practical aspects of Digital Logic. This includes analysis, design and testing of digital logic circuits. Design and implementation are covered using Altera devices.

title: Engineering Design Concepts
description: This course introduces the skills and knowledge necessary to effectively complete a capstone project. Students are presented with concepts in design, systems engineering, project management, engineering team organization, ethics, and professionalism.

title: Experiential Learning
description: This non-credit, experiential learning course offers students an opportunity to gain "real world" on-the-job work experience related to a specific academic field of study. Students must register for this course through the FSU Career Center.

title: Measurements and Instrumentation for Electrical Engineers
description: This course introduces various measurement methods and instrumentation techniques used in electrical engineering practice and research.

title: Introduction to Energy Storage
description: This course introduces students to energy storage technologies and devices with major focus on electrochemical storages including advanced rechargeable batteries, electrochemical capacitors, and fuel cells.

title: Advanced Linear Networks
description: This course explores topics such as synthesis of LC one-port networks, synthesis of LC two-port networks; operational amplifier applications; active filters; approximation methods; switched-capacitor filters.

title: ADVANCED LINEAR NETWORKS
description: AN ALGORITHMIC AND MATRIX-ORIENTED APPROACH TO LINEAR ANALY- SIS. RIGOROUS MATHEMATICAL METHODS ARE DEVELOPED THAT LEND                    THEMSELVES TO COMPUTER ANALYSIS.

title: Power Systems I
description: This course focuses on the analysis of electric power systems using system modeling for large-scale power networks; admittance and impedance matrix formation; power flow; optimal dispatch; symmetrical components; balanced and unbalanced fault analysis; and transient stability studies.

title: Power and Energy Lab
description: This course is designed to provide hands-on experience with various electrical devices, and machines commonly used in power generating, transmission, and distribution systems, including motors, generators, transformers, and power system instrumentation equipment.

title: Converter Modeling and Control
description: This course provides a study of DC-AC and DC-DC converter-modeling techniques and control schemes. Topics include average switch models, voltage-source and current-source converter models, current programmed control, and active filter control.

title: Power Conversion and Control
description: This course introduces solid-state power conversion and control circuits, including analysis and design of nonlinear multiple-phase circuits with sinusoidal and non-sinusoidal variables; constant-frequency and variable-frequency input conversions; variable-frequency inverters: sensing and processing circuits supporting control systems; and embedded microprocessor control systems.

title: Renewable Energy Generation I
description: This course is an introduction to renewable energy generation. Topics covered include smart grid system, hybrid electric vehicle, and grid-connected PV inverters. Emphasis is placed on the energy conversion techniques applied in the renewable energy source and energy storage elements.

title: Photovoltaics
description: This course educates students in the design and applications of solar energy technology. This course focuses on theoretical fundamentals of solar energy conversion, types of solar cells and their operations, optical engineering, and energy storage and distribution systems. The course covers solar energy insolation and global energy needs, current trends in photovoltaic energy engineering, solar cell material science, design and installation of solar panels for residential and industrial applications and connections to the national grid and cost analysis of the overall system.

title: Renewable Energy Generation II
description: This course is an introduction to renewable energy generation. Topics include smart grid system, hybrid electric vehicle, and grid-connected PV inverters. Emphasis is placed on the energy conversion techniques applied in the renewable energy storage elements.

title: Electronic Circuits and Systems Laboratory
description: Advanced electronic laboratory.

title: Introduction to Digital Integrated Circuit Design
description: Semiconductor device physics, digital logic fundamentals, static inverter  analysis, static logic gate analysis, dynamic switching analysis, combinational logic design.

title: Introduction to Cybersecurity
description: This course is an introduction to computer security: symmetric ciphers, public-key cryptosystems, digital signatures, hashes, message authentication codes, key management and distribution, authentication protocols, vulnerabilities and malware, access control, network security.

title: Introduction to Analog IC Design
description: "Design and analysis of bipolar and MOS analog integrated circuits. Topics include operational amplifier design, analog multipliers, active loads, current sources, and active filters. "

title: Mixed Signal ICs
description: This course introduces mixed signal processing using analog and digital integrated circuits. Topics include fundamentals of sampled data systems, nonlinear and dynamic analog circuits, Nyquist-rate data converters, over-sampling data converters, and digital filters, as well as the use of computer-aided-design programs.

title: Sonar
description: This course introduces basic concepts of sonar systems including acoustic propagation, transducers and projectors, target strength, reverberation, beamsteering, beamforming, beampatterns, and synthetic aperture sonar.

title: Electromagnetics Laboratory
description: This course focuses on the applications of electromagnetic field theory. Experiments include field mapping, transmission lines, spectrum analysis, impedance matching, waveguides, antennas, radar, and fiber optics.

title: Optoelectronics and Optical Systems
description: This course examines the theory and applications of optical techniques in modern electronics and communications. Includes a study of optical fibers, sources, detectors, optical communication systems, integrated optics, holography, and principles of optical signal processing.

title: Optical Sensors
description: This course examines the basic concepts of optical sensors and essential optics. Topics include intensity, phase, and frequency modulated optical fiber sensors and their applications, distributive sensing systems, and optical fibers in signal processing.

title: Antenna Systems
description: This course covers topics such as antenna theory, including Hertzian dipoles, thin linear antennas, aperture antennas, arrays, loop antenna, slots, horns, and waveguides.

title: Digital Communication Systems
description: This course covers topics such as sampling principle, spectral analysis of digital waveforms and noise, pulse and digital transmission systems, digital multiplexing, error probabilities, and system performance.

title: Optical Fiber Communications
description: This course offers a review of the characteristics of basic optical components for optical communications systems. Topics include optical fibers, light sources, optical detectors and fiber connectors; signal degradation in optical fibers, optical analog and digital communication systems; and coherent optical fiber communications.

title: Wireless Communications and Networking
description: This course covers the fundamentals of wireless communications and systems. The core topics include radio-wave propagation characteristics of wireless channels; modulation and demodulation techniques for mobile radio; reception techniques for wireless systems; fundamentals of cellular communications; multiple access techniques; wireless networking; and hybrid networking of a wireless system and the Internet.

title: Advanced Topics in Communications
description: This course is designed to provide an in-depth knowledge of some of the advanced topics in communications. Topics covered include ideal communication systems, signal to noise ratio (S/N) for amplitude and angle modulation, design of systems to improve S/N ratio, satellite communication, and mobile communication.

title: Digital Control Systems
description: Discrete time systems; Z-transform; sampling and reconstruction; system time-response characteristics; stability analysis; digital controller design.

title: Analysis and Design of Control Systems
description: This course focuses on the control of linear time-invariant systems with an emphasis on continuous system modeling, stability analysis of linear systems, frequency response methods, the root locus method, and state-space methods.

title: Instrumentation for Measurement and Control
description: Design and application of sensors and transducers commonly used in industrial control and laboratory automation. Concepts and application of statistical process control are introduced.

title: Introduction to Very High Speed Integrated Circuit Hardware Description Language
description: This course offers an introduction to the VHDL hardware description language: data type, operations, combinational, sequential, components, functions, and procedures using VHDL. The course provides an overview of FPL devices and design tools.

title: Introduction to VHDL Laboratory
description: This laboratory course supports EEL 4710. The course introduces Electrical and Computer Engineering majors to various practical aspects of circuit design using Very High-Speed Integrated Circuits Hardware Description Language (VHDL).

title: Introduction to Field Programmable Logic Devices
description: Overview of PLD, CPLD and FPGA devices; introduction to hardware descripti on languages (HDLs); combinational, sequential and FSM design using HDLs; introduction to top down design methodologies.

title: Computer Architecture
description: This course presents modern computer architectures by studying how the relationships between hardware and software impact performance, machine language definition, processor data path and control designs, interfacing, and advanced topics.

title: Digital Signal Processing with Field Programmable Gate Arrays
description: This course is a review of Field Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs), HDL, mathematics, signals and systems. Computer arithmetic concepts, DSP system design of FIR filters, IIR filters, DFT, FFT, and wavelets filter banks are also covered.

title: Advanced Digital Hardware
description: This course covers number representations, addition/subtraction, multiplication, division, real arithmetic, hardware algorithms for function evaluation, and implementation issues.

title: Advanced Microprocessor-Based System Design
description: This course covers advanced concepts in microprocessor-based system design. Topics include microprocessor architectures, hardware/software synchronization, interrupts, interface protocols, power management, and introduction to real-time operating systems.

title: Advanced Microprocessor-Based System Design Laboratory
description: This course is a laboratory in support of EEL 4742 Advanced Microprocessor-Based System Design.

title: Microprocessor-Based System Design
description: This course explores fundamental concepts in microprocessor-based system design. Topics include: C and assembly level programming, computer architecture and organization, hardware timers, interrupt controllers, and device interfacing utilizing parallel and serial I/O.

title: Microprocessor-Based System Design Laboratory
description: Laboratory course in support of EEL 4746.

title: Embedded Microcomputer Design Project
description: This course allows students to work on individual projects selected with consent of instructor. Selected lectures and an open-door Motorola 68000 laboratory.

title: EMBEDDED MICROCOMPUTER DESIGN PROJECT
description: PROJECTS SELECTED WITH INSTRUCTOR CONSENT, PROJECT SUPPORT   INCLUDES FORMAL LECTURES AND AN OPEN-DOOR MOTOROLA 68000

title: Digital Image Processing
description: This course is an introduction to image processing techniques, including theoretical development, analysis, and practical implementation. A project that includes implementation grounds the successful student in current engineering practice.

title: Computer Networks
description: This course covers the fundamentals of computer network design and analysis; network architecture using a layered approach; analysis and examples of network protocols and standards; techniques for evaluating network performance selecting network protocols.

title: Introduction to Neural Networks
description: This course covers fundamentals of neural networks: dynamical systems, associative memories, perceptrons, supervised/unsupervised learning algorithms. Applications in signal processing, pattern recognition, control, optimization, and communications.

title: Embedded Microprocessor System Design
description: This course teaches students to be able to design, configure, and implement a complete embedded microprocessor system using soft-core, parameterized, or hard core microprocessors for FPGAs including required peripherals and software tools.

title: CpE Languages: Introduction to Python, Verilog and MatLab/Simulink
description: In this course, computer programming is used to improve quantitative problem-solving skills. This is a comprehensive course using the PYTHON, VERILOG and MATLAB/SIMULINK programming languages.

title: Directed Individual Study
description: Normally may be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours. Requires departmental approval.

title: Honors Work in Electrical Engineering
description: This course consists of independent or directed research in a specialized area beyond the current curriculum in electrical engineering. May be repeated to a maximum of nine (9) credit hours; repeatable within the same term.

title: Senior Design Project
description: This course introduces designing for electrical and computer engineers, as well as system design concepts of specifications, analysis, synthesis, and manufacturability. Project management skills and team dynamics concepts are developed. Oral and written presentations are required.

title: Senior Design Project I
description: This course exposes senior students to concepts in design, project management, engineering team organization, and professionalism. Students are grouped into design teams where these principles are put into practice in organizing, proposing, and developing an engineering project. Periodic written reports and oral presentations and a final written proposal are required. The lecture material and texts provide instructions on project management, ethics, and design skills.

title: Electrical Engineering Senior Design Project Laboratory
description: The focus is on a major multi-disciplinary design project in electrical or computer engineering that involves hardware, software, and/or theoretical design. The project should incorporate engineering standards and realistic constraints. Formal oral and written presentations of the project are required.

title: Computer Engineering Senior Design Project II
description: This course exposes senior students to the concepts in design, project management, engineering team organization, ethics, design skills, and professionalism. Students are grouped into design teams where these principles are put into practice in organizing, proposing, and developing an engineering project. Periodic written reports and oral presentations, and a final written report are required.

title: Computer Engineering Senior Design Project Laboratory
description: Course Description not on file

title: Electrical Engineering Senior Design Project II
description: This course exposes senior students to the concepts in design, project management, engineering team organization, ethics, design skills, and professionalism. Students are grouped into design teams where these principles are put into practice in organizing, proposing, and developing an engineering project. Periodic written reports and oral presentations, and a final written report are required.

title: Special Topics in Electrical Engineering
description: This course covers special topics in electrical engineering with emphasis on recent developments. Topics and credit vary; consult the instructor. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve (12) credit hours; repeatable within the same term.

title: Computational Electrical Engineering
description: This course covers a broad range of computational methods and their applications to electrical engineering. Methods include solution of equations, matrices, differentiation, integration, solution of differential equations, Fourier analysis, and boundary-value problems. Applications include circuit analysis, signal analysis, signal processing, electromagnetics, and optics.

title: ADVANCED CIRCUIT THEORY
description: THEORY AND IMPLEMENTATION OF PASSIVE AND ACTIVE NETWORKS AND FILTERS. DESIGN AND UTIIZATION OF CIRUIT CAD PACKAGES.

title: Signal and System Analysis
description: This course focuses on continuous and discrete dynamic models with an emphasis on state variable models; Laplace transform, z-transform, and the time domain solutions. Includes real-time digital simulation and sampling theory.

title: Power Systems Analysis
description: This course examines power system planning and operational problems. Subjects covered include load flow, economic dispatch, fault studies, transient stability, and control of problems. System modeling and computer solutions are emphasized through class projects.

title: Power System Transients
description: In this course, topics include electrical transients in power systems; study of time domain, frequency domain and traveling wave techniques for transient analysis; study of switching transients associated with loads, capacitors, faults, line reclosing and single-pole switching; study of interaction between lighting and power systems; introduction to insulation coordination.

title: Integration of Distributed Generation
description: This course introduces the concept of integration of alternate renewable resource based power generation technologies known as ‘Distributed Generation’. The course familiarizes students with various DG sources such as Wind, Solar, Hydro, Wave and Tidal, Geothermal, and Bio-fuel based energy generation technologies, however, PV and wind technologies are studied in details. The course also covers the modeling and simulation of distribution networks, modeling of PV and wind technologies, their integration technologies with the grid, possible impacts on grid due to the integration of DG, tariffs (feed-in tariff, net-metering etc.) for DG integrations, impact of variability, microgrids and its controls, IEE interconnection standards etc.

title: Digital Integrated Circuit Design
description: Design of digital integrated circuits, applications, solid state device sw itching characteristics, memory, computer aided design, and layout.

title: MICROELECTRONICS ENGINEERING
description: COVERS THE DESIGN OF SOLID STATE MICROELECTRONIC DEVICE STRUCTURE; AND THE FABRICATION TECHNIQUES FOR SUCH DEVICES.  ATTENTION IS GIVEN TO INTERNAL DEVICE STRUCTURE AS WELL AS PACKAGING & ASSEMBLY.

title: ANALOG INTEGRATED CIRCUIT DESIGN --CHGNG TO EEL5375
description: DESIGN OF ANALOG INTEGRATED CIRCUITS; CONCEPTS AND APPLICATIONS; INCLUDING CURRENT SOURCES; INPUT/OUTPUT STAGES; GAIN STAGES; MULTIPLIERS; PHASE-LOCKED LOOPS; ACTIVE FILTERS.

title: RF/Microwave Circuits I
description: This course is an introduction to passive RF/microwave circuit design. Topics include distributed transmission line theory; lumped circuit and network analysis; impedance matching; and the design of various microwave components such as filters, couplers, detectors and mixers.

title: RF/Microwave Circuits II
description: This course focuses on active RF/microwave circuit design. Topics include two-port characterization of RF/microwave transistors; matching networks; RF/microwave transistor amplifier design using low-noise, high-gain, broadband and high-power design methods; and RF/microwave transistor oscillator design.

title: MICROWAVE ENGINEERING
description: ELECTROMAG THRY/WAVE EQUATIONS FOR TRANSM'N LINES & WAVEGUIDES;TRANSMISSION LINE & WAVEGUIDE CIRCUIT ANALYSIS; RESONATORS & SLOW-WAVE STRCTRS;MICWAVE OSCILLATORS & AMPS;ANTENNAS; Ã¿ÂPURGED SUMMER 1997Ã¿Â¨

title: Electromagnetics and Optics
description: This course will cover a number of topics including basic electromagnetic wave theory--Maxwell's equations, plane waves, energy and power flow; geometrical optics; applications to optical systems, optical fibers, and resonators; wave propagation in layered media; applications to lasers and integrated optics; quantum theory of lights; black-body radiation; introductory quantum electronics; and other selected research topics.

title: Antenna Theory
description: This course covers topics such as electromagnetic fields; radiation from simple sources and apertures; receiving antennas; arays-uniformly spaced, non-uniform, pattern synthesis; cylindrical antennas and arrays; radiation from conical and spheroidal structures; slot antennas; open waveguides and small horns.

title: ADVANCED ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY I--CHNG TO EEL5486
description: FUNDAMENTAL CONCEPTS AND THEOREMS IN ELECTROMAGNETIC FIELDS; PLANE; CYLINDRICAL; & SPHERICAL WAVE FUNCTIONS; PERTURBATION & VARIATIONAL TECHNIQUES; & MICROWAVE NETWORKS.

title: PROPAGATION OF ELECTROMAGNETIC SIGNALS
description: ELECTROMAGNETIC RADIATION; ANTENNA CONCEPTS; TROPOSPHERIC & IONOSPHERIC PROPAGATION; GROUND WAVES; RADIO LINK CALCULATION;MICROWAVE & MILLIMETER-WAVE PROPAGATION;ATTENUATION BY ATOMOSPHERIC CONSTITU.

title: Advanced Electromagnetic Theory
description: This course discusses advanced concepts and theorems in electromagnetic fields; plane, cylindrical, and spherical wave functions; perturbation and variational techniques; microwave networks.

title: Digital Communication Theory
description: This course examines principles of modern digital communication systems including pulse-code modulation, error-control coding, optimal signal protection, and information theory.

title: STATISTICAL COMMUNICATION THEORY
description: SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS; DETECTION THEORY; ESTIMATION THEORY; APPLICATIONS.

title: Applied Cryptography for Engineers
description: This course introduces the mathematics behind modern encryption algorithms so that engineers can implement them efficiently on resource-constraint devices. Topics covered include relations, groups, and finite fields as applied to block ciphers such as DES and AES; Hash algorithms such as SHA256; and modern public key encryption techniques such as DH, RSA, El-Gamal, and DSA algorithms.

title: SIGNAL ANALYSIS
description: DISCRETE SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS; ANALOG SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS;DIGITAL SIMULATION; Z-TRANSFORMS; DIGITAL FILTERS; FOURIER ANALY SIS; FAST FOURIER TRANSFORMS.

title: Securing Connected Devices and Networks
description: This course provides an overview of technical and theoretical aspects of network security with emphasis on the Internet of Things (IoT). Topics include design of secure networks, concerns at all architectural levels, modern security protocols and their applications, public key infrastructure (PKI), and digital certificates.

title: Advanced Topics in Communication
description: This course is designed to provide an in-depth knowledge of some of the advanced topics in communications. Topics covered include ideal communications systems; signal to noise ratio (S/N) for amplitude and angle modulation; design of systems to improve S/N ratio; satellite and mobile communication.

title: Introduction to Mobile Robotics and Unmanned Systems
description: This course provides a thorough technical overview of autonomous vehicles for engineering students interested in understanding the basics of unmanned systems. The principles and methodology involved for the systems development is discussed. The course uses practical examples of developing autonomous unmanned vehicle systems.

title: Foundation for Advanced Control Methods
description: This course introduces the basic background for most control theoretic developments. The course considers standard treatments of the necessary mathematical background for such development. In this course, mathematical techniques are developed that are required for advanced control design, including: Linear Algebra, Numerical Optimization, and Nonlinear Analysis.

title: Multivariable Control
description: This course covers H2 and H? control design for linear systems with multiple inputs and multiple outputs. Globally optimal techniques, fixed-structure(e.g., reduced-order) techniques. Includes introductory concepts in robust control.

title: Robot Kinematics and Dynamics
description: This course is an introduction to robot kinematics and dynamics, including forward kinematics, inverse kinematics, and differential kinematics. Also covers rigid motion and homogenous transformations, velocity and force/torque relations and resolved motion rate control; serial, parallel and kinematically redundant manipulators.

title: OPTIMAL CONTROL
description: APPLICATION OF OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES TO THE ANALYSIS AND DESIGN OF CONTROL SYSTEMS.

title: Principles of Autonomous Systems
description: The course provides an in-depth review of the principles of autonomy by reviewing probability theory and covering topics in pattern recognition, computer vision/perception, localization/SLAM, planning, and unsupervised/supervised learning.

title: ASIC Systems Design I
description: This course is an introduction to Application Specific Integrated Circuit (ASIC) families. Overview of programmable ASICs. Introduction to the VHDL design entry and simulation language. Programmable ASIC design methodology   will be introduced.

title: Computer System Architecture
description: This course focuses on advanced topics, including memory hierarchy such as caching and virtual memory, pipelining, and others.

title: Advanced Neural Networks
description: This course is designed to provide students with an in-depth knowledge of advanced topics in neural networks such as universal approximation networks, transformation-based neural networks, information theoretic models, and foundations of neurodynamics.

title: CODING THEORY
description: FINITE VECTOR SPACES; LINEAR BLOCK CODES; CYCLIC CODES; BCH CCODES; FINITE GEOMETRY AND ERROR-CORRECTING CODES.

title: Engineering Data and Device Security
description: Students learn how to identify vulnerabilities and threats related to devices, computers, and data, so they can perform risk analysis and develop engineering solutions to avoid vulnerabilities and develop controls and counter-measures. Existing types of security models used for controlling access are discussed.

title: Supervised Research
description: This course cannot be used as credit toward a degree. May be repeated to a maximum of three semester hours for candidates in master's degree, and five semester hours for candidates in doctoral degree.

title: Supervised Teaching
description: Cannot be used as credit toward degree. May be repeated to a maximum of three semester hours for candidates in master's degree, and five semester hours for candidates in doctoral degree.

title: Modern AC Drives
description: This course offers an advanced study of AC drives. Topics covered include pulse-width modulation, drive system modeling, and vector controls. Emphasis is placed on the drives of induction and synchronous machines.

title: Power Systems Operation and Control
description: This course examines modern power system operational and control problems and solution techniques, including state estimation, contingency analysis, load-frequency control, and automatic generation control. Additional subjects covered include load-flow analysis, unit commitment, and external equivalents for steady-state operations.

title: Advanced Topics in Optoelectronic Systems
description: In this course, typical offerings include: waves and fields in electro-optics; modern optics and coherence; optical data processing; nonlinear optics;laser technology; electro-optical circuits and systems for signal processi ng; electro-optical devices.

title: ADVANCED TOPICS IN MICROWAVES AND ANTENNAS
description: NUMERICAL MTHDS IN ELECTROMAGNETICS; PROPAGATION OF ELCTRMGNTC SGNLS; ADVCD ELCTRMGNTC THEORY II; ADVCD ANTENNA THRY;     MICROWAVE & MILLIMTR-WAVE CIRCUITS. MAY BE REPTD TO MAX TWELVE SEM HOURS.

title: Advanced Topics in Digital Signal Processing
description: In this course, typical offerings include: advanced digital signal processing; fast DSP algorithms; image processing; data compression; computer vision; pattern recognition; VLSI based DSP design; advanced signal and systems theory.

title: Robust Control
description: Course covers control design for systems with uncertain dynamics; robust H design; structured singular value synthesis; LMI and Riccati equation solution techniques.

title: ASIC Systems Design II
description: "Course covers MOS transistor theory, CMOS logic gate design, ASIC standard cell library design, ASIC partitioning, floorplanning and placement. Introduction to routing algorithms and test methods."

title: ADVANCES COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
description: PARALLEL PROCESSING TECHNIQUES; ARRAY PROCESSORS; FAST       ALGORITHMS; AND MULTIPROCESSORS.

title: Advanced Topics in Computer Engineering
description: Typical offerings include: digital systems design; advanced computer architecture; local area networks; telecommunication networks; data and computer communications; queuing systems; artificial intelligence; expert systems; distributed computer systems; artificial neural networks; automata theory.

title: Special Graduate Topics in Electrical Engineering
description: This course explores special topics in electrical engineering at the graduate level with emphasis on recent research and developments. Content, credit, and prerequisites vary-consult instructor. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve semester hours.

title: Electrical and Computer Engineering Seminar
description: May be repeated a maximum of ten times. Presentations by faculty, students and visiting scholars. All full-time graduate students must enroll each semester.

title: Master's Thesis Research
description: This course provides a means of registering for thesis research work and recording progress towards its completion. Student must consult with the academic department for appropriate registration of course credit hours.

title: Dissertation
description: May be repeated to a maximum of forty-eight semester hours.

title: Preliminary Doctoral Examination
description: May be repeated one time.

title: Master's Comprehensive Examination
description: May be repeated a maximum of two times.

title: Master's Thesis Defense
description: Course Description not on file

title: Dissertation Defense
description: May be repeated to a maximum of three times.

title: TECHNOLOGY, ENVIRONMENTAL AND HUMAN HEALTH
description: ENVIRONMENTAL AND PUBLIC HEALTH IMPACTS OF TECHNOLOGY IN     ANCIENT TO MODERN CIVILIZATION.

title: Environmental Engineering Chemistry
description: This course covers applications of fundamental principles from general, organic, and biological chemistry to major environmental processes. Emphasis is placed on organic/inorganic pollutants in water, soil, and air; chemistryof treatment processes; and toxicological chemistry.

title: Introduction to Environmental Engineering
description: This course is a broad introduction to environmental engineering topics. Includes fundamental concepts in mass balance, water quality, water and wastewater treatment, air quality, and solid/hazardous waste management, with considerations to environmental and societal impacts, as well as technical limitations. This course serves as the foundation for all other environmental engineering courses.

title: Introduction to Environmental Engineering Science Laboratory
description: This course covers the use of field and laboratory instruments for measuring air and water quality indicators. Includes site visits.

title: Applied Behavior Analysis for Special Educators
description: This course is an introduction to behavioral principles and procedures useful for managing the behavior of students with handicaps. For majors only.

title: CLINICAL TEACHING SEMINAR
description: OVERVIEW OF INDIVIDUALIZATION OF INSTRUCTION FOR EXCEPTIONAL CHILDREN DURING THE PAST TWO HUNDRED YEARS AND CURRENT                        DEVELOPMENTS IN EXCEPTIONAL CHILD EDUCATION.                0

title: Practicum in Direct Observation
description: Direct observation and recording techniques for analysis of classroom management strategies.

title: Practicum in Direct Observation
description: This course employs direct observation and recording techniques for analysis of classroom management strategies.

title: Foundations of Special Education
description: This introductory course includes classic and contemporary readings in the field of special education. An overview of how society has responded to the various conceptualizations of human exceptionality is presented. Students are introduced to the trends and people that formed the foundation of contemporary special education. Current policies and practices are also examined.

title: Introduction to Mental Disabilities
description: This course is designed to provide students with an overview of the basic knowledge pertinent to mental disabilities. Special attention is given to the educational, social and psychological aspects of mental disabilities.

title: Introduction to Learning and Behavior Disorders
description: This course examines the fields of learning and behavioral/emotional disorders from historical, theoretical, practical, and public school perspectives.

title: Including Students with Disabilities in the General Education Curriculum
description: This course provides participants with the knowledge and skills to include students with disabilities in the general education curriculum by adapting instruction and assessment procedures and processes.

title: The Development of Individuals with Special Needs
description: Examination of the impact of disabilities on development throughout the life cycle.

title: Typical and Atypical Development and Learning
description: This course examines typical and atypical learning and development throughout the lifespan.

title: Educational Assessment for Students with Disabilities
description: This course is designed to provide an opportunity for students to develop performance skills in the administration of formal and informal assessment instruments and processes.

title: Individualized Educational Planning
description: This course provides students with the opportunity to demonstrate effective use of diagnostic skills. For majors only.

title: Individualized Instruction for Exceptional Students
description: Knowledge and performance in implementing individualized instructional practices with exceptional students. For majors only.

title: DIAGNOSIS AND PROGRAMMING FOR THE MILDLY RETARDED                                  INDIVIDUAL
description: INDIVIDUALIZATION OF INSTRUCTION IN ACADEMIC SCHOOL SUBJECTS WITH EMPHASIS ON ADAPTATIONS TO ACCOMODATE PERCEPTUAL-MOTOR  AND INTELLECTUAL DISABILITIES.

title: PROGRAMMING FOR THE MILDLY RETARDED INDIVIDUAL                                           LABORATORY
description: EXPERIENCE IN WRITING EDUCATIONAL PRESCRIPTIONS AND PROVIDING INDIVIDUALIZED INSTRUCTION.

title: Technology for Students With Disabilities
description: This course introduces classroom applications of technology for students with disabilities.

title: Individualized Reading Instruction for Students with Disabilities
description: This course reviews methods for teaching reading to individuals with disabilities.

title: Teaching Mathematics to Learners with Disabilities
description: In this course, instructional methods and curriculum to teach mathematics to students with disabilities are examined.

title: Access to the General Education Curriculum for Individuals with Moderate/Severe Disabilities
description: This course examines methods for teaching functional reading and life skills to individuals with disabilities.

title: MOBILITY FOR THE EXCEPTIONAL INDIVIDUAL
description: PROVIDE THE FUTURE TEACHERS OF THE EXCEPT. STUDENT WITH:     L) GREATER APPLICATION & REALISTIC UNDERSTANDING OF THE PROBLEMS  WITH MOBILITY;2) TECHNIQUES TO ASSIST THESE INDIVIDUALS TO MOVE BETTER.

title: Foundations of School to Community Transition
description: "This course addresses the ways in which schools, agencies, and the community can work cooperatively to prepare students for the transition from their school years to their adult years."

title: Characteristics and Education of Learners with Autism Spectrum Disorder
description: This course examines the characteristics and etiology of autism spectrum disorders (ASD), and effective intervention strategies for individuals with ASD. Additionally, the focus is on assessing and addressing the core challenges of learners with ASD (e.g., language, social communication, repetitive behaviors, and behavior regulation) using evidence-based practices in school settings. Students learn to identify and implement assessment and intervention strategies based on learner strengths and needs.

title: Differentiated Instruction for Students with Exceptionalities
description: This course is designed to provide future teachers with the knowledge and skills needed to meet the diverse learning needs of students found in today's classrooms.

title: Social Studies and Science Curriculum for Special Educators
description: This course provides an overview of and foundation for teaching science and social studies to learners with disabilities (P-12). Students develop knowledge and skills in using a variety of instructional methods and materials appropriate for providing access to the general education science and social studies curriculum for learners with disabilities.

title: Classroom Management for Special Educators
description: This course provides class participants with the knowledge and skills to effectively manage the behavior of learners with disabilities within a classroom setting. Course content is organized around the Pyramid Model that includes three tiers of behavior management based on priniciples of positive behavior support 1) Universal supports, 2) Prevention, and 3) Intervention.

title: Positive Behavior Support
description: This course provides students with the knowledge and skills necessary to develop, implement, and evaluate the impact of positive behavior supports. Emphasis is placed on understanding the communicative function of challenging behaviors, the teachings of new skills that make the challenging behavior unnecessary, and the prevention of the reoccurrence of challenging behaviors.

title: Collaboration with Families, Schools, and the Community
description: This course provides the knowledge and skills necessary for collaborating  with families, other professionals, and community members.

title: Study of Human Exceptionality
description: This course increases learner knowledge and awareness of the characteristics and needs of people with exceptionalities, and acquaints learners with the resources, issues, and trends related to appropriately meeting these needs.

title: Introductory Practicum in Special Education
description: This practicum provides experience with individuals with a range of disabilities. This course also provides participants with experience with different special education service delivery models in a variety of grade levels ranging from K to 12.

title: Supervised Practicum
description: Designed as a pract                                                        icum for the assessment of the profoundly handicapped or the moderately/severely retarded and to provide experience in various aspects of assessment. For majors only.

title: Practicum in Severe Cognitive Disabilities and/or Autism Spectrum Disorder
description: This course provides teacher candidates with field work experience teaching K-12 students with severe cognitive disabilities and/or autism spectrum disorder. The practicum provides experience in developing, implementing, and evaluating functional and academic skills for K-12 students with these disabilities.

title: Practicum in Individualized Planning
description: Diagnose individual exceptional children and plan appropriate instruction.

title: Student Teaching in Special Education
description: In this course, student teachers teach students with disabilities for one semester within a public school setting, full-time, and under the supervision of a certified special education teacher.

title: Pre-Student Teaching Seminar
description: This course prepares last-term majors in special education for the transition from student to professional, lays the groundwork for the culminating activity of student teaching, and acquaints the new professionals with their future role and responsibility to the field of exceptional student education.

title: Special Topics in Special Education
description: In this course, topics vary from term to term. May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours.

title: Practicum in High Incidence Disabilities
description: This practicum provides experience with individuals with high incidence disabilities.

title: Honors Work in Special Education
description: This course is designed to provide qualified upper-division majors in special education an opportunity to undertake an independent and original research project. May be repeated to a maximum of nine thesis hours with instructor approval.

title: Typical and Atypical Early Development
description: This course focuses on typical and atypical development in the early years (birth through 8 years). Particular attention is paid to factors influencing development and the impact of disabilities and biomedical risk factors on learning, development, and behavior. Recent research and its implication for evidence-based practices is a major component of the course.

title: ISSUES AND TRENDS IN SPECIAL EDUCATION
description: ISSUES;TRENDS;LEGISLATION AND LIGIGATION IN THE EDUCATION    OF EXCEPTIONAL INDIVIDUALS.

title: Addressing Misinformation in Special Education
description: This course teaches students how to think critically about the data and models that constitute evidence used to support the use of interventions aimed at improving educational outcomes for learners with special education needs.

title: Foundations of Evidence-Based Practices in Special Education
description: This course introduces participants to the scientific concepts and principles underlying evidence-based practices in special education. The course emphasizes application of practices with individuals with disabilities in natural contexts.

title: Teaching High Risk Adolescents in Alternative Settings
description: This course examines teaching in alternative settings (e.g., alternative schools, juvenile justice facilities, therapeutic residential treatment centers). Students learn how to (a) overcome the barriers and capitalize on the facilitating factors to providing effective instruction in these settings, (b) identify and plan lessons incorporating evidence based instruction for high risk adolescents, and (c) collaborate with personnel in both the alternative and regular educational settings to support students.

title: EVALUATION OF CURRICULA IN SPECIAL EDUCATION
description: EVALUATION;SELECTION;ADAPTATION IN SPECIAL EDUCATION         CURRICULA INCLUDING COORDINATION/DESIGN OF THE TOTAL SPECIAL                  EDUCATION PROGRAM.

title: Middle and Secondary Curriculum for Learners with Disabilities
description: This course assists participants to develop curricular planning skills for middle and high school students with disabilities. Emphasis is placed on evidence-based instructional strategies appropriate for teaching middle and high school students receiving special education services.

title: General Curriculum Access for Learners with Extensive Support Needs
description: This course focuses on strategies for developing curricular priorities for students with extensive support needs, including ways to link instruction to state standards in reading, math, writing, science, and other content areas. the course places emphasis on evidence-based practices that allow the K-12 individual with extensive support needs to access the general education curriculum.

title: Adaptations and Accommodations for Students with Disabilities
description: This course provides information regarding adaptations and supports that enhance the education of children and youth with learning and behavior challenges. Emphasis is placed on procedures that adapt the general education curriculum.

title: Teaching Learners with Autism Spectrum Disorder
description: This course provides a comprehensive overview of autism spectrum disorder (ASD). The impact the characteristics of ASD have on student participation and learning in the general education curriculum, and adaptations to enhance and support learning while emphasizing individual goals and objectives are addressed.

title: Assessment and Diagnosis of Autism Spectrum Disorder and Intellectual Disability
description: This course provides students with an understanding of the core features associated with and diagnostic criteria used to identify autism spectrum disorder (ASD) and intellectual disability (ID). Students learn a transdisciplinary framework for the assessment process, and develop the skills necessary to identify, design, and administer assessments used to build appropriate, assessment-driven educational plans.

title: Assessment of Students with Disabilities
description: This course provides students with competency in the assessment of students with disabilities. In addition to exploring issues related to assessment, the course focuses on the administration and interpretation of formal instruments and informal assessment procedures.

title: Development and Assessment of Individuals with Severe Cognitive Abilities/Autism Spectrum Disorder
description: This course examines the knowledge and skills necessary to understand the effects of severe cognitive disabilities (SCD) and autism spectrum disorder (ASD) on development and learning. Participants learn to assess academic and functional achievement of  individuals with SCD and ASD in a variety of areas that impact academic and functional outcomes for these individuals.

title: Instructional Environments: Ethical, Legal, Safety, and Classroom Management Considerations
description: This course is designed to provide participants with the knowledge and skills necessary to organize the physical, social, and instructional environment of a classroom that includes a heterogeneous group of learners.

title: Methods for Teaching Students with Low Incidence Disabilities
description: This course offers an overview of curriculum and instructional needs of students with low incidence disabilities.

title: Assessment and Methods in Early Childhood Special Education
description: This course focuses on the formal and informal evaluation techniques and individualized instruction for young children with disabilities. The course provides class participants with the knowledge and skills to develop, implement, and evaluate evidence-based, developmentally appropriate intervention for preschool children who are developmentally delayed, are at risk for developmental delay,

title: Introduction to Special Education Technology
description: Introduction to ways technology (computers) is used with special education students.

title: Mathematics for Students with Disabilities
description: This course equips teachers to address the needs of learners with high incidence disabilities in grades K-12 when teaching mathematics skills. Methods and techniques learned are appropriate for a variety of classroom settings. Emphasis is placed on accommodations, supports, and modifications needed to access the general education mathematics curriculum.

title: Instructional Environments: Ethical, Legal, Safety, and Classroom Management Considerations
description: This course is designed to provide participants with the knowledge and skills necessary to organize the physical, social, and instructional environment of a classroom that includes a heterogeneous group of learners.

title: Literacy for Learners with Disabilities
description: This course introduces the major reading components of scientifically-based reading research as applied to learners with disabilities: phonological awareness, phonics, fluency, vocabulary, and comprehension. Additional topics include models of typical and atypical reading development and principles and practices of differentiated instruction.

title: Advanced Reading Instruction for Students with Disabilities
description: This course examines methods for assessing and teaching reading skills to individuals with disabilities.

title: Literacy for Learners with Disabilities
description: This course introduces the major reading components of scientifically-based reading research as applied to learners with disabilities: phonological awareness, phonics, fluency, vocabulary, and comprehension. Additional topics include models of typical and atypical reading development and principles and practices of differentiated instruction.

title: Differentiating Mathematics Instruction in Middle and High School
description: This course provides an overview of the purpose and rationale for differentiated instruction in middle and high school mathematics classes. Classroom strategies for differentiating mathematics and ways to relate initial assessments (diagnostic) and assessment for learning (formative) to these strategies are examined.

title: Seminar in Transition
description: This course addresses the range of postsecondary education, transitional services, employment training programs and community living and recreation available to adults with disabilities.

title: Preparing Individuals for Transition
description: This course focuses on planning and implementing appropriate transitional services for youths with disabilities in the public schools.

title: Teaching Students with Autism
description: This course provides teacher candidates with the knowledge needed to develop effective communication, social, and language assessments and interventions for individuals with autism spectrum disorder. Emphasis is placed on establishing appropriate learning goals and implementing research-based instructional methods to maximize each learner’s progress and access to the general education curriculum. An overview of the characteristics of autism spectrum disorders (ASD) is provided.

title: Capstone in Special Education Research
description: This course engages students in applied research and inquiry related to a socially significant problem of practice that impacts the education of individuals with disabilities. May be repeated to a maximum of six credit hours.

title: Assessment and Methods in Early Childhood Special Education
description: Focuses on formal and informal evaluation techniques and individualized instruction for young children with disabilities.

title: Program Development for Young Children with Disabilities
description: This course focuses on issues related to providing comprehensive services to young children with disabilities. Emphasis is placed on topics surrounding the development and evaluation of developmentally appropriate programs, family-focused intervention, providing services in normalized settings, and the utilization of evidence-based practices for children birth to age five years who are developmentally delayed, are at risk for developmental delay, or who have a specific disability.

title: Universal Design for Learning
description: This course examines Universal Design for Learning (UDL) as a framework for addressing the educational needs of K-12 learners. Elements of UDL to be discussed include teaching content in multiple formats so that all students can access it, providing students with various ways to demonstrate their learning, and stimulating students' interests and motivation for learning in a variety of ways.

title: Leadership Skills in Exceptional Education
description: "Designed to study problems of public attitudes, regulations, finance, specialized personnel, facilities, and identification of pupils and their special needs as they pertain to education services for exceptional children and youth."

title: Nonviolent Crisis Intervention
description: This course provides class participants with skills in non-physical methods for preventing or managing disruptive behavior, including positive behavior support (PBS) at the tertiary level. In addition, the course includes the study of crisis prevention methods including restraint positions, transport techniques and team strategies. Students who successfully complete the course are eligible for a two-year Crisis Prevention Institute (CPI) blue card that validates their completion of the Nonviolent Crisis Intervention training program.

title: Early Childhood and Elementary Education Curriculum for Special Educators
description: This course provides special educators with knowledge of general early childhood and elementary curriculum. Emphasis is placed on evidence-based supports, modifications, and accommodations to allow the child with disabilities to access the general education curriculum.

title: Teaming with Families, Schools and the Community
description: This course provides students with the knowledge and skills to collaborate and team with professionals from a variety of disciplines in the schools and other community agencies, to include family members in the collaboration process, and to support families of children with disabilities throughout the life cycle.

title: Cognitive and Social Implications of Maltreatment of Students with Exceptional Needs
description: This course focuses on the topic of child maltreatment and its impact on students with disabilities.

title: Introduction to Special Education Technology
description: This course introduces the way technology (specifically computers) is used with special education students.

title: Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) for Learners with Autism Spectrum Disorder
description: This course provides students with knowledge of best practices in facilitating communication and learning of children and youth with complex communication needs including those with autism spectrum disorder (ASD) and other developmental disabilities in school settings. This course focuses on the use of assistive technologies including augmentative and alternative communication (AAC) to enhance student participation in social and academic learning opportunities. Emphasis is placed on effective assessment and instruction for AAC and other assistive technology users.
