The course examines domestic violence, infidelity, substance abuse, physical health issues, and major psychiatric disorders.

title: Introduction to Systems Theory
description: This course is an introduction and overview of Systems Theory as applied in the field of Marriage and Family Therapy. The course covers original formulations of general systems theory, communication systems, chaos theory, and cybernetics, and subsequent translations of those ideas into family systems theory used in therapeutic contexts. Focus is on family metaphors, patterns, interaction, and communication to describe human behavior and relationships. Readings and concepts are also applied to students’ family of origin to enhance “self-as-therapist” development.

title: Systemic Assessment and Treatment Planning in Marriage and Family Therapy
description: This course establishes students’ foundation in clinical assessment, diagnosis, and treatment planning in MFT. It elaborates on the theoretical assumptions and values underlying alternative assessment techniques, and how those assessments move to diagnosis and development of treatments for major mental health issues and other presenting concerns. The course also covers risk assessment and crisis intervention.

title: Group Psychotherapy
description: In this course students learn theoretical concepts and methods of systemically oriented group psychotherapy. Students develop core competencies in fundamental skills and approaches used in group psychotherapy. The role of contextual factors in group therapy are examined. Students participate in experiential group work to develop skills to propose and plan group psychotherapy.

title: Substance Use Theory and Techniques
description: This course prepares students to apply a systemic perspective with clients presenting with substance use and addiction. Students learn systemic assessment skills and empirically-supported interventions to address substance use disorders. It emphasizes sensitivity to individual, social, and cultural diversity, as well as the alternative forms of substance use including co-occurring psychiatric and substance use disorders.

title: Theories in Marital and Family Therapy
description: Doctoral students only.

title: Psychopathology, Diagnosis and Systemic Treatment
description: This course familiarizes students with diagnostic procedures using the DSM-5, while challenging students to understand and evaluate each mental health disorder from a broader, societal perspective. Emphasis on psychiatric diagnostic classification systems, particularly in the context of treatment and collaborative relationships with other medical and mental health providers.

title: Marriage and Family Therapy Capstone
description: This course provides advanced marriage and family therapy (MFT) students with the opportunity to demonstrate a cumulative understanding and integration of core theoretical knowledge and clinical competence in the field of MFT. Assignments integrate major theoretical approaches, ethical guidelines, multicultural awareness, and research methods. Supervision evaluations and professional development feedback will also be included as a measure of clinical competency and professional readiness.

title: Applied Research in Human Sciences
description: This course provides an introduction to the use of statistical inference and data analysis for students majoring in human sciences, using unique examples and datasets that are discipline specific. Laboratory experiences emphasize the use of various analytic techniques.

title: Applied Research in Human Sciences
description: This course provides an introduction to the use of statistical inference and data analysis for students majoring in human sciences, using unique examples and datasets that are discipline specific. Laboratory experiences emphasize the use of various analytic techniques.

title: Qualitative Research in Family and Child Science
description: The course prepares students to critically engage with qualitative research, conduct qualitative studies, and incorporate qualitative components in research involving mixed methods regarding family systems, children, and other relational systems. This course focuses on narrative research, with additional investigation of ethnographic and case study research.

title: Readings in Family and Child Sciences
description: In this course, topics vary and each topic may be taken only once.

title: Seminar in Family and Child Sciences
description: In this seminar, topics vary and each topic may be taken only once.

title: Internship-Family/Child
description: This course consists of supervised practical field experiences in various professional settings related to family/child development including human services, agencies, hospitals, educational facilities, and government.

title: Special Project
description: This course is open to course option master's degree students who are near completion of their course requirements. Permission of major professor     required.

title: Thesis
description: A minimum of six semester hours is required.

title: Family Development and Interaction
description: The course integrates development and systems theory to provide an understanding of family dynamics and development. Relationships at the micro level are the primary forces.

title: Diversity Considerations for Family Research and Practice
description: This course examines human development and family processes within diverse contexts. Specifically, students focus on the impact of systems of oppression and privilege on developmental and family processes, discuss best practices for research and therapy, and critically review theory for inclusion of individuals and family from diverse groups.

title: Theories of Family Sciences
description: This course is a review of current theories in family studies.

title: Human Sexuality
description: This course explores the biological, psychological, sociological, and familial aspects of human sexuality during the life span. Emphasis on examining socio-cultural values and norms regarding human sexuality, understanding sexual health and its treatment, and providing sex education and support through helping professions.

title: Advanced Clinical Marriage and Family Therapy Theory
description: This course is an in-depth, advanced study of traditional and contemporary theories within the field of marriage and family therapy (MFT).

title: Supervision in Marriage and Family Therapy
description: This course teaches the fundamentals of marital and family therapy supervision through didactic presentation, supervised experience of actual supervisory practice, and reflective interaction.  This course meets the specifications required for the Approved Designation of the American Association of Marriage and Family Therapy.

title: Randomized Clinical Trials
description: This course presents state-of-the-art methods in the design, implementation, interpretation, and reporting of randomized clinical trail (RCT) research applicable to marriage and therapy. Methodological controversies, proposed solutions, and ethical issues are discussed.

title: Effectiveness and Translation Research
description: This course provides students with knowledge of research approaches that investigate the effectiveness, dissemination, implementation, and adaptation of clinical interventions to communities and populations. Emphasis is given to issues of effectiveness and translation research relevant to marriage and family therapy.

title: MFT Social Justice and Diversity
description: This course is an in-depth, advanced study of theory, research, and intervention best-practices for couples and families in the context of today’s society, with an emphasis on cultural awareness to prepare students as researchers and clinicians to work effectively with diverse populations within the field of marriage and family therapy (MFT).

title: Professional Studies in Marital and Family Therapy
description: "Examines the ethical standards of family therapy and issues, such as public laws and policies, which impact on the practice of marital and family therapy."

title: Divorce and Postdivorce Counseling
description: Doctoral students only. Family disorganization and reorganization are examined in the context of a multidisciplinary developmental perspective. Assessment and treatment issues are addressed in relationship to polarized couples.

title: Systems Evaluation: Assessment Intervention and Case Planning
description: This course is specifically designed to assist students in systems assessment at the supra, system, and subsystem levels. It focuses on key substantive material and research in the area of theory and practice. The course provides a seminar format for the application of theory to practice.

title: Sex Therapy
description: Course Description not on file

title: Advanced Longitudinal Modeling in Family Science
description: This course focuses on a structural equation modeling approach for analyzing longitudinal data in family science. Topics include longitudinal designs, missing data, latent growth curve model, longitudinal confirmative factor analysis, longitudinal panel model, and longitudinal mediation and moderation.

title: Intervention Research in Family and Child Sciences
description: This course examines the efficacy and effectiveness of interventions in Family and Child Sciences. This course covers many of the complex decisions that investigators must make in designing, implementing, interpreting, and reporting intervention research.

title: Outcome Research in Marriage and Family Therapy
description: This course is designed to provide students with an overview of psychotherapy outcome research in general and in marriage and family therapy (MFT) outcome research in particular.

title: Research Methods in Family and Child Sciences
description: This course is an overview of research methods currently in use in studying individuals, families, and children.

title: Special Topics: Marital and Family Therapy-Topics Vary
description: Each topic may be taken only once. May be repeated within the same term to a maximum of nine semester hours.

title: Special Topics: Family and Child Development (Topics Vary)
description: This course is an in-depth exploration of contemporary topics and issues. Repeatable for up to nine hours.

title: Practicum in Marital and Family Therapy
description: May be repeated to a maximum of twenty-one semester hours.

title: Internship in Marriage and Family Therapy
description: This internship provides supervised practical field experience in various professional settings related to couple and family therapy, including human services, agencies, hospitals, educational facilities, and government. may be repeated to a maximum of twelve semester hours. S/U grade only.

title: Master's Thesis Defense
description: Course Description not on file

title: Dissertation Defense Examination
description: Course Description not on file

title: President's Seminar in Motion Picture, Television, and Recording Arts
description: Course Description not on file

title: Elements of Film
description: This course provides the student with an opportunity to study classic examples of superior filmmaking and develop critical skills that will help the individual understand and evaluate film communication, literature, and art.

title: Introduction to Cinema Studies: Analysis and Practice
description: This course introduces students to Cinema Studies theories and techniques (e.g., narrative structure, cinematic language, formal analysis, cultural analysis). Through film screenings, discussions, and film analysis exercises, students compare and interpret films representing a variety of genres, aesthetic traditions, and cultural contexts. The course includes selections from the Western canon.

title: History of Motion Pictures
description: This course provides a historical and critical survey of the development of narrative motion pictures.

title: History and Practice of Visual Effects and Animation
description: This course introduces computer graphics in the context of historical approaches to visual effects and animation. Students apply historical techniques to create original animations and visual effects.

title: Professional Communication
description: This course provides instruction in oral presentation and communication skills for professional settings in the motion picture industry. May be repeated to a maximum of three semester hours.

title: Screenwriting I
description: Introduction to the basic techniques of screenwriting and storyboarding. Exercises in story structure, dialogue, and character development. Introduction to screenwriting and storyboarding computer software. This course will satisfy up to 7,000 words of writing requirements for FAC 6A-10.030.

title: Story Development and Screenwriting I
description: This course introduces the basic techniques of story development and screenwriting through exercises in story structure, dialogue, and character development.

title: Screenwriting Workshop
description: This course provides a story room workshop in which students develop original story concepts and scripts for motion picture shorts. Through a variety of weekly activities and writing assignments, students examine and practice a variety of story development techniques as well as industry standard screenwriting practices. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve(12) credit hours.

title: Motion Picture & TV Production I
description: Course Description not on file

title: Film Editing
description: "Analyzes, discusses, and puts into practice the skills and techniques required to edit a narrative motion picture."

title: Film Sound
description: Teaches the principles of sound and the basic practices involved in creating a motion picture sound track. Also covers the aesthetic use of sound in motion pictures.

title: Filmmaking I: Fiction
description: This course provides a basic introduction to the craft of fiction filmmaking with an emphasis on film production, visual storytelling, and acting techniques. Students apply course concepts through a variety workshops and practical exercises, culminating in the development, production, and post- production of a short film.

title: Practicum in Technical Support
description: This course introduces students to the technical skills and protocols employed in below-the-line (BTL) positions in the motion picture industry. The course stresses the protocols observed by below-the-line crew to ensure the effective and safe operation of production equipment and adherence to workflows through all stages of production. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve credit hours; repeatable within the same term.

title: Acting for Filmmakers
description: This course instructs students in a variety of actor training techniques that can be used to enhance on-camera performances and directing actors. Students apply course concepts through practical acting exercises. Special focus is given to learning the language of actors and experiencing first-hand the actor approach to a role, the challenges of acting for the camera, the rehearsal process, and script analysis. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve credit hours; repeatable within the same term.

title: Motion Picture Sound
description: This course teaches the principles and aesthetics of sound and the basic practices involved in creating a motion picture sound track. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve semester hours.

title: Motion Picture Editing
description: This course teaches introductory principles, aesthetics, and theory of motion picture editing and their application in editing narrative shorts. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve semester hours.

title: Visualization I
description: This course provides an introduction to the visual communication skills and techniques required for the development and previsualization of narrative motion pictures.

title: Compositing I
description: This course introduces layer-based compositing concepts and techniques, including their application in visual effects for motion pictures.

title: Compositing II
description: This course introduces node-based compositing theory and practice with an emphasis on best practices and professional standards used in the visual effects industry. May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Introduction to 3D Computer Graphics
description: This introductory course provides a comprehensive foundation in 3D modeling, encompassing both hard-surface and organic techniques. Students will gain proficiency in industry-standard software to create a diverse range of three-dimensional forms.

title: Introduction to 3D Computer Animation
description: This course provides instruction in the foundation principles of animation. Students apply course concepts to create original animation that conveys emotion and demonstrates basic understanding of body mechanics. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve credit hours. May be repeated within the same term.

title: Introduction to Rigging
description: This course introduces the skills and techniques required for the rigging of props and characters in CG. It provides students with a foundation in core craft skills needed on any CG animated project.

title: Film Styles
description: This course allows students to develop deep understanding of cinema as a form of cultural expression through critical analysis of motion picture form and content. Students will closely analyze a selection of films from different cultural and historical contexts and develop foundational knowledge of cinema through exploration of genres, movements, and other topics of interest. The course will include selections from the Western canon, as well as from other parts of the world.

title: ELEMENTS OF FILM
description: THE PURPOSE OF THIS COURSE IS TO PROVIDE THE STUDENT WITH AN OPPORTUNITY TO STUDY CLASSIC EXAMPLES OF SUPERIOR FILMMAKING AND DEVELOP CRITICAL SKILLS THAT WILL THE STUDENT EVALUATE FILM COMMUNICATION.

title: SCREENWRITING AND STORYBOARDING I: BASIC TECHNIQUES
description: INTRODUCTION TO BASIC TECHNIQUES OF SCREENWRITING AND STORYBOARDING. EXERCISES IN STORY STRUCTURE, DIALOGUE, AND CHARACTER DEVELOPMENT. INTRO. TO SCRWRG & STYBRD COMPUTER SOFTWARE.

title: Film and Television Writing
description: "Process and techniques of film scripting, including documentary, rhetorical, and narrative forms, for the film and television media."

title: Screenwriting II
description: Development of sophisticated story ideas, treatments, character biographies, storyboards, step-outlines, first drafts, and revisions of both original ideas and adaptations. This course will satisfy up to 7,000 words of writing requirements for FAC 6A-10.030.

title: Cinematography
description: Emphasis on narrative; non-narrative potential and examination of film as an alternative means of image-making.

title: Filmmaking II
description: Provides understanding of objective oriented acting techniques and developseffective methods for working with actors in rehearsal and on set.  Requires directing a short film project.

title: MOTION PICTURE AND TELEVISION PRODUCTION I: BASIC                      TECHNOLOGY AND TECHNIQUES
description: PROVIDES A BASIC UNDERSTANDING OF FILM/VIDEO PRODUCTION      TECHNOLOGY, EQUIPMENT OPERATION, TERMINOLOGY, AND TECHNIQUES.

title: Documentary Filmmaking
description: View and discuss documentary films from various eras, countries, and points of view as a means of understanding personal aesthetic as a documentary filmmaker. Students plan, script, budget, shoot, edit, and mix documentaries.

title: Film Camera and Lighting
description: "Provides a theoretical and practical knowledge of all aspects of cinematography: cameras and lenses, filmstocks, exposure, lights, lighting, and composition."

title: ADVANCED FILMAMAKING
description: ADVANCED PRINCIPLES AND PRACTICE OF MAKING A SHORT, SYNC-    SOUND, 16MM FILM.  MAY BE REPEATED UP TO NINE (9) SEMESTER                 HOURS.

title: FILMS AND FILMMAKING II
description: PRODUCTION OF CLASSIC SOUND FILMMAKING AND FILM STUDY        TECHNIQUES USING 16 MM FILM.

title: Filmmaking II: Nonfiction
description: This course allows students to view and discuss documentary films from various eras, countries, and points of view as a means of understanding personal aesthetic as a documentary filmmaker. Students plan, script, budget, shoot, edit, and mix documentaries. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve credit hours. May be repeated within the same term.

title: HISTORY OF MOTION PICTURES
description: AN HISTORICAL ANALYSIS OF THE MOTION PICTURE AS A MEANS OF   COMMUNICATION;A SOCIAL FORCE;AND AN ART FORM.                .

title: HISTORY OF MOTION PICTURES
description: AN HISTORICAL ANALYSIS OF THE MOTION PICTURE AS A MEANS OF   COMMUNICATION;A SOCIAL FORCE;AND AN ART FORM.                .

title: HISTORY OF THE MOTION PICTURE I: 1895-1945
description: PROVIDES AN OVERVIEW OF FILM AS AN INDUSTRY, MASS MEDIUM,    AND ART FORM, FROM ITS TECHNOLOGICAL INCEPTION AND EARLY USESAS ENTERTAINMENT THROUGH THE SILENT ERA TO THE HEYDAY OF HOLLYWOOD STUDIOS.

title: HISTORY OF THE MOTION PICTURE II: 1946-PRESENT
description: PROVIDES AN OVERVIEW OF THE CONTEMPORARY CINEMA FROM THE     ADVENT OF TV THROUGH THE COLLAPSE OF THE STUDIOS AND RISE OF INDEPENDENTS TO THE CURRENT ERA OF THEATRICAL, TV, CABLE AND VIDEO MARKETS.

title: Filmmaking Techniques
description: This course emphasizes visual storytelling and directing techniques. Students apply concepts by developing and creating motion pictures. Special focus is given to script analysis and interpretation, directing actors, blocking techniques, and methods of visual storytelling.  May be repeated to a maximum of twelve credit hours. May be repeated within the same term.

title: Acting For the Camera
description: This course combines fundamental acting technique with the special needs of acting for the camera. The course explores how actors prepare, rehearse, audition, and perform differently in front of the camera as compared to an onstage production. Students engage in acting exercises and scene work, working with film school student directors under the guidance of the instructor. May be repeated to a maximum of fifteen (15) credit hours; repeatable within the same term.

title: The Contemporary Cinema: Theory and Practice
description: Review and analysis of post-1950 motion pictures with emphasis on technique and industrial evolutions.

title: ITALIAN CINEMA
description: VIEWING AND DISCUSSION OF ITALIAN FILMS WITH ATTENTION TO    CINEMATIC NARRATIVE AND TO POPULAR FILM AS AN EXPRESSION OF                   CULTURAL VALUES. REPEATABLE FOR UP TO SIX(6) CREDIT HOURS.

title: FRENCH CINEMA
description: STUDIES IN HISTORY AND EVOLUTION OF FRENCH CINEMA. REPEATABLEUP TO SIX(6) CREDIT HOURS.

title: GERMAN CINEMA
description: STUDIES IN HISTORY AND EVOLUTION OF GERMAN CINEMA FROM EXPRESSION TO NEW GERMAN CINEMA. REPEATABLE FOR UP TO SIX(6) CREDIT                 HOURS.

title: HISPANIC CINEMA
description: STUDY AND VIEWING OF MAJOR FILMS OF SPAIN AND LATIN AMERICA; INCLUDING THOSE OF BUNUEL; SAURA; CIRO DURAN; EQUINO; LITTIN;GIRAL; BORAU AND PASTOR VEGA. COURSE MAY BE REPEATED ONCE.

title: Motion Picture Production Management
description: This course provides instruction in motion picture production management roles and chain-of-title responsibilities in pre-production, production, and post-production such as script breakdowns. scheduling, budgeting, daily production protocols, post-production supervision, and final delivery. May be repeated to a maximum of fifteen credit hours. May be repeated within the same term.

title: Digital Studio Department Leadership
description: This course provides instruction and practice in the leadership and management of departments within the field of visual effects and animation. The course also addresses positions such as digital effects producer, supervisor, as well as modeling, animation, and compositing leads. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve semester hours.

title: Lighting, Texturing, and Rendering I
description: This course provides an introduction to concepts of CG lighting and rendering to enhance original animations and visual effects. The course also addresses basic lighting theory needed to create virtual lighting effects. May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Visualization II
description: This course provides an intermediary-level instruction in the visual devel-opment skills required for the preproduction and previsualization of narrative motion pictures.

title: Stop Motion Animation
description: This course teaches principles of stop-motion and forward-animation. Through the creation of original animations, it also explores stop-motion cinematography, claymation armature and set-construction.

title: Character Animation I
description: This course provides instruction in developing and creating believable and compelling animated characters. Topics and skills covered include body mechanics, facial animation, lip synching, conveying human emotion, acting theory for animators, and production processes from planning shots to final polishing.

title: Light, Texture, & Rendering II
description: This course provides an in-depth look at the construction of nodal based texturing networks and their implementation in current industry lighting and rendering pipelines. Through demonstrations, lectures and assignments, students learn the process and pipeline of lighting, texturing and rendering for CG. May be repeated to a maximum of 12 credit hours; may be repeated in the same term.

title: Visual Effects Aesthetics
description: This course provides continuing theory and practice in modeling, texturing, and lighting to create visual effects that are believable and fit the aesthetic style of a given cinematic world. May be repeated to a maximum of nine credit hours.May be repeated within the same term.

title: Visual Effects Cinematography
description: This course surveys techniques used in both practical and digital effects cinematography through though the hands-on planning and execution of visual effects cinematography projects.

title: The Contemporary Cinema: Theory and Practice
description: This course is a review and analysis of post-1950 motion pictures with emphasis on technique and industrial evolutions.

title: International Human Rights and Film
description: This course explores issues concerning international human rights using film as the key medium. Course lectures and weekly film viewings will examine how film directors have explored human rights themes through commercial Hollywood films, independent films, and documentary films.

title: Film Genres and Film Makers
description: This course provides students the opportunity to view historical and current films followed by discussions in an academic forum. May be repeated to a maximum of six credit hours. May be repeated within the same term.

title: Special Topics
description: This course analyzes specialized topics in motion pictures. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve credit hours. May be repeated within the same term.

title: BFA Qualifying Exam
description: Evaluates the progress of the student and recommends continuance in the film school or directs the student toward other areas of study.

title: BFA Comprehensive Exam
description: May be repeated up to eight times.

title: Thesis Film Support
description: This course covers the principles and responsibilities of below-the-line filmmaking roles, as well as the performance of these responsibilities on the set of BFA thesis films. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve semester hours.

title: SCREENWRITING II:  ADVANCED TECHNIQUES
description: DEVELOPMENT OF SOPHISTICATICATED STORY IDEAS, TREATMENTS,    CHARACTER BIOGRAPHIES, STORYBOARDS, STEP-OUTLINES, FIRST DRAFTS, AND REVISIONS OF BOTH ORIGINAL IDEAS AND ADAPTATIONS.

title: Screenwriting III
description: Development of thesis script story ideas, treatments, character biographies, storyboards, step-outline, first drafts, and revisions of both original ideas and adaptations. This course will satisfy up to 7,000 words of writing requirement for FAC 6A-10.030.

title: Feature Screenwriting: Development
description: "Teaches the various techniques of scene breakdown, setting up, sequencing, character development and dialogue development. Also teaches rewriting techniques to strengthen first drafts."

title: Feature Screenwriting
description: "Teaches aspects of feature screenwriting format, pitching ideas, creating and developing character, story, and dialogue."

title: Thesis Development
description: This course teaches conceptual and practical approaches to developing stories intended for short films. Students develop an original screenplay through multiple drafts and iterations.

title: SCREENWRITING III: THESIS SCRIPTS
description: DEVELOPMENT OF SOPHISTICATED STORY IDEAS, TREATMENTS CHARACTEBIOGRAPHIES, STORYBOARDS, STEP-OUTLINES, FIRST DRAFTS, AND                 REVISIONS OF BOTH ORIGINAL IDEAS AND ADAPTATIONS.

title: FILM COMMUNICATION
description: MOTION PICTURE PRODUCTION;CINEMATOGRAPHY;WRITING;EDITING;PRODUCTION OF SHORT FILMS IN DRAMATIC AND NON-DRAMATIC FORM

title: CINEMA PRODUCTION
description: BUILDS ON THEORETICAL AND PRACTICAL ASPECTS OF CINEMATOGRAPHYCONCENTRATES TECHNICAL/TECHNIQUE DIMENSIONS OF SOUND/PICTURE INTEGRATION FOR TV AND THEATER.DOUBLE/SINGLE SYS CINEMATOGRAPHY EDITING EXPLD

title: Advanced Filmmaking
description: Advanced principles and practice of making a short, sync-sound, 16 mm film.

title: MOTION PICTURE AND TELEVISION PRODUCTION II: CAMERA                    AND LIGHTING
description: PROVIDES A THOROUGH GROUNDING IN THE PRINCIPLES AND PRACTICESOF CINEMATOGRAPHY, VIDEOGRAPHY AND STUDIO AND FIELD LIGHTING               INSTRUMENTS AND TECHNIQUES.

title: MOTION PICTURE AND TELEVISION PRODUCTION III:       SYNCHRONOUS AND NON-SYNCHRONOUS SOUND
description: PROVIDE A THOROUGH GROUNDING IN FILM AND VIDEO SYNC AND NON- SYNC SOUND RECORDING IN BOTH FIELD AND STUDIO PRODUCTION     SETTINGS.

title: MOTION PICTURE AND TELEVISION PRODUCTION IV:                           EDITING
description: PROVIDES A THOROUGH GROUNDING IN FILM AND VIDEO EDITING AND  POST PRODUCTION.

title: Production: Advanced Editing
description: Offers advanced study in film editing techniques and styles.

title: MOTION PICTURE AND TELEVISION PRODUCTION VI:                           PRODUCING PROJECT
description: PROVIDES AN OPPORTUNITY TO PRODUCE A MAJOR FILM/VIDEO        PRODUCTION FROM INCEPTION TO PRE-PRODUCTION, PRODUCTION, POSTPRODUCTION AND MANAGING OF ALL OF THE OTHER ELEMENTS OF FILM/VIDEO MAKING.

title: Production: Advanced Cinematography
description: Introduces advanced lighting techniques and allows hands-on exercises emphasizing the creative use of lighting for mood and storytelling.

title: Production: Advanced Sound
description: "Course provides students with a thorough understanding of digital sound recording, sound mixing, and various stages of sound post production as it applies to 16mm filmmaking."

title: DOCUMENTARY FILMMAKING
description: VIEW & DISCUSS DOCMNTRY FILMS FROM VARIOUS ERAS COUNTRIES&   PNTS OF VIEW AS MEANS OF UNDRSTNDNG PERSNL AESTHETIC AS DOCUMETARY FILMMAKER... REPEATABLE TO 6 HOURS.

title: THE CINEMA AS A SOCIAL FORCE
description: AN ANALYSIS OF THE ROLE AND FORCE OF THE MOTION PICTURE IN OUR CONTEMPORARY SOCIETY.                                                                                                                   0

title: Film Aesthetics
description: Analysis of film with regard to three basic questions: 1) What is film? 2) How do we perceive film? 3) How is an aesthetic developed?

title: MOTION PICTURE AND TELEVISION STYLES
description: FOCUSES ON THE VIDEO/FILM STYLES OF FILMMAKERS, TV SERIES,   GENRES, NATIONAL MOVEMENTS, AND OTHER TOPICS OF INTEREST.                  MAY BE REPEATED TO A MAXIMUM OF SIX (6) SEMESTER HOURS.

title: PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT I: PLANNING AND MANAGING                         RESOURCES
description: PROVIDES A THOROUGH GROUNDING IN MANAGEMENT SKILLS IN THE    ENTERTAINMENT BUSINESS INCLUDING WORKING RELATIONSHIPS,      BREAKING DOWN SCRIPTS AND PLANNING PRODUCTION SCHEDULES.

title: Film Business Planning
description: This course exposes students to current business trends and issues in the film industry; introduces case studies that examine all business aspects surrounding a feature film; introduces current readings on the film industry.

title: Film Exhibition and Advertising
description: "To provide background in the marketing, advertising, and exhibition of film to audiences."

title: Film Producing and Finance
description: An introduction to the techniques necessary to create a responsible business approach for the production of motion pictures and to create a greater individual awareness of the motion picture producer as a career opportunity.

title: Film Law
description: "Provides a working knowledge of the specialized concepts and vocabulary pertaining to entertainment-related forms of intellectual property and the contractual relationships necessary to finance, create, and license various forms of entertainment."

title: Motion Picture Marketing and Exhibition
description: This course introduces the marketing and exhibition of motion pictures, with an emphasis on current methods and practical techniques for promoting, publicizing, and distributing short films. Throughout the course, students develop original marketing materials to be used primarily for film festival submissions. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve credit hours. May be repeated within the same term.

title: Visualization III
description: This course provides an advanced-level instruction in the visual development skills required for the preproduction and previsualization of narrative motion pictures.

title: Character Art
description: This course provides theory and practice in developing digital characters and figures including concept art, modeling sheets, digital sculpture, texture and preparation for rigging and animation.

title: Character Animation II
description: This course provides continuing theory and practice in character and creature animation with an emphasis on animating believable multi-character dialogue and combat scenes and on implementing professional workflow standards. May be repeated to a maximum of nine credit hours. May be repeated within the same term.

title: Directed Individual Study
description: May be repeated to a maximum of twelve credit hours. May be repeated within the same term.

title: Application of Research and Creative Methods
description: In this course, students participate in a faculty or graduate student research and/or creative project. May be repeated to a maximum of six credit hours.

title: PROFESSIONAL SEMINAR IN MOTION PICTURE, TELEVISION                     AND RECORDING ARTS
description: PROVIDES STUDENTS WITH UP TO DATE CPMTACTS AMD CONSULTATION  WITH INDUSTRY REPRESENTATIVES ON THE LATEST DEVELOPMENTS IN  THE MOTION PICTURE AND TELEVISION INDUSTRY.  MAY BE REPEATED A MAX OF 3 TIMES.

title: Undergraduate Film Seminar
description: This course consists of the development of a creative film project under the direction of a faculty member or industry professional in various areas. May be repeated to a maximum of six credit hours. May be repeated within the same term.

title: Professional Development
description: This course prepares students to enter the professional film work arena; addresses the search for employment within the film industry and the search for funds to produce independent work.

title: Application of Instruction Methods
description: This course allows students to participation in the instructional process under the strict supervision of a faculty member. May be repeated to a maximum of six credit hours.

title: Professional Internship
description: This internship gives apprenticeship experience with a company involved in film/video production, distribution, or exhibition. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve semester hours. May be repeated within the same term.

title: Portfolio Production
description: This course takes students through the process of developing materials for a professional demo reel/portfolio, starting with development of ideas/themes around which to organize portfolio content and culminating in the production and organization of portfolio assets.May be repeated to a maximum of fifteen credit hours. May be repeated within the same term.

title: Thesis Defense
description: This course involves the presentation of a senior thesis film to the faculty and student body for review and approval. May be repeated within the same term.

title: BFA THESIS FILM I: BEGINNING PRODUCTION
description: STUDY OF PRINCIPLES AND RESPONSIBILITIES OF GRIPS, GAFFERS,  ASSISTANT DIRECTORS, ASSISTANT CAMERAPERSONS, AND PRODUCTION MANAGERS AND PERFORMANCE OF THESE RESPONSIBILITIES ON THE SET OF BFA THESIS FI

title: Thesis Film Production Management
description: This course teaches production management for BFA thesis films. May be repeated to a maximum of 15 credit hours. May be repeated within the same term.

title: BFA Thesis Production
description: This practicum course focuses on all creative aspects of BFA thesis production. May be repeated to a maximum of 22 credit hours or six instances of course enrollment. May be repeated within the same term.

title: BFA THESIS FILM IV:  POSTPRODUCTION
description: STUDY AND PRACTICE OF ALL POSTPRODUCTION ELEMENTS ASSOCIATED WITH THE BFA THESIS FILM.

title: Honors in the Major Research
description: In this course, students accepted into the Honors in the Major program complete an original research or creative project in their major area of study. This course must be repeated at least twice to complete a minimum of six (6) credit hours total, but may be repeated up to a maximum of twelve credit hours in total.

title: Thesis Script Rewrite
description: This course teaches students to articulate on paper an idea that is both discernible and visual in the form of a collection of interesting moments that add up to tell a story.

title: Introduction to the Motion Picture, Television, and Recording Arts Industry
description: Comprehensive overview of the production, distribution, and exhibition of motion pictures and television programming.

title: History and Criticism I
description: This course focuses on the historical survey of the film medium worldwide, from its invention to the modern era.

title: History and Criticism II
description: This course is a survey of theories and movements in motion picture history.

title: Writing for Television
description: This workshop-style course examines basic narrative elements in the context of writing for television. Through developing an original concept of a television show, plotting the pilot, and writing a large portion of the script, students learn script language and process. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve credit hours; may be repeated within the same term

title: TV Writer's Room Simulation
description: This workshop-style course simulates a television writer’s room. Students develop skills and practice writing original content for television episodes in a collaborative writing environment. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve credit hours; may be repeated within the same term.

title: Writing the Dramatic Series Pilot
description: This course instructs students on how to create and write a professional-quality pilot script for an original dramatic series. Special focus is given to world-building, idea sustainability, creating unique, dimensional, and believable characters, and cultivating compelling drama. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve semester hours.

title: Writing the Dramatic Series
description: This course instructs students on how to create and write a professional-quality spec script for an episodic series. Special focus is given to series formats, characters, and conventions. Students apply course concepts by developing and writing original spec scripts for existing series. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve (12) credit hours.

title: Screenwriting I: Techniques and Treatments
description: "Introduction to the basic elements of screenwriting including treatment, plot or story construction, dialogue, character development, action, and storyboarding. "

title: Screenwriting: Short Format
description: This workshop-style course examines basic narrative elements in the context of writing a short form script. Through developing, writing, and rewriting a script, students learn script language and process.

title: Screenwriting II: Narrative Techniques
description: Survey of the various motion picture and television genres from the writer's perspective. Requires students to develop original characters and plots for television programs and motion picture screenplays and to write and rewrite a variety of scenes for original and preexisting dramas and comedies.

title: Screenwriting: Feature Format
description: This workshop-style course examines how to create dimensional characters and a well-structured story in the context of writing a feature film script. Students pitch, outline and write a screenplay with a strong focus on believable characters and situations that draw the reader/audience into the world they have created and bring the story to a satisfying conclusion.

title: Screenwriting III: Comedy Workshop
description: "Comedy writing styles and techniques including comic business, devices, and dialogue for feature motion picture and television situation comedies. "

title: Screenwriting 3: Advanced Workshop
description: This course offers an advanced approach to writing a fifteen page script. Students analyze narrative problems in preparation for a rewrite. Through workshops, redeveloping, and then rewriting a fifteen page thesis script, the student gains a better understanding as to how to make a story idea more compelling through rewriting.

title: Screenwriting IV: Television Workshop
description: To produce a full-length television episode for a specific series for the established characters and settings.

title: Adaptation Workshop
description: This workshop-style course instructs students on how to adapt work for film and television. Special focus is given to creating intellectual property in the short story form for the purpose of adaptation and interpretation for various audiences across genres, as well as industry practices around the use of intellectual property development.

title: Screenwriting: Motion Picture Workshop
description: This course focuses on writing screenplays that apply dramatic storytelling concepts. Through regular writing workshops, students examine and practice story development techniques as well as industry-standard practices involved in creating motion picture scripts. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve credit hours; repeatable within the same term.

title: Basic Film Production
description: "Introduction to 16mm film production, studio and location lighting, sound recording, and flatbed editing. Student projects require shooting film and editing nonsound projects. May be repeated to a maximum of six (6) semester hours. "

title: Producing I
description: Provides information about and experience in producing feature-length programs.

title: Producing 1
description: Course Description not on file

title: Producing II
description: Background in the development of business structure for the purposes of producing feature films.

title: Producing 2
description: Course Description not on file

title: Basic Video Production
description: Provides a comprehensive overview of the production and delivery of television programming.

title: CAMERA AND LIGHTING WORKSHOP
description: PROVIDES AN UNDERSTANDING OF THE BASIC THEORY, TECHNOLOGY,   AND TECHNIQUES OF VIDEOGRAPHY AND CINEMATOGRAPHY INCLUDING                 THE SELECTION AND OPERATION OF CAMERAS AND LIGHTING EQUIPMENT.

title: Synchronous and Nonsynchronous Sound Workshop
description: "Theory, technology, and techniques of recording synchronous and nonsynchronous sound and the uses of sound in motion pictures and television production. "

title: Sound Workshop
description: Course Description not on file

title: Editing and Postproduction Workshop
description: Conventional and advanced techniques of motion picture and videotape editing. Via lectures and labs, students study the principles of editing and become proficient in editing film picture and sound on simple and sophisticated 16mm systems and in editing video picture and sound using todays latest technologies.

title: Production Design Workshop
description: "Overview of production design principles and practices used in the creation of sets, costumes, props, makeup, and special effects for motion pictures and television. "

title: Postproduction Sound Workshop
description: "This course will provide a comprehensive survey of all processes related to building, editing, and mixing effects tracks, ambience tracks, dialogue tracks, and background music."

title: Post-production Sound Workshop
description: Course Description not on file

title: Directing: Single-Camera Techniques
description: Intermediate-level study of single-camera directing techniques, with emphasis on script interpretation, complex storyboarding and pre-production planning, and the development of aesthetic consistency throughout all facets of production. May be repeated to a maximum of six (6) semester hours. May be repeated during the same semester.

title: Directing Actors
description: Course Description not on file

title: Directing: Multicamera Workshop
description: "The study, development, and direction of multicamera television productions. May be repeated to a maximum of nine (9) semester hours."

title: Directing: Single-Camera Workshop
description: "Direction of single-camera dramatic productions, from preparation to shooting and editing. May be repeated to a maximum of nine (9) semester hours. "

title: Directing
description: "Advanced film directing for students specializing in direction with emphasis on creative use of lighting, actors, and camera."

title: Advanced Directing
description: Course Description not on file

title: Camera and Light Mechanics
description: "Technical overview of the film process including technical discussions on cameras, lenses, lighting instruments, and film stock response. May be repeated to a maximum of six (6) semester hours. "

title: Lighting Workshop
description: "Cinematography is illustration and illusionpainting with lightthen enhancing that with lenses; this course will explore ways to define a lighting look for a scene, an entire film, or television production. "

title: Intermediate Television Editing
description: "Comprehensive survey of online video-editing methods and techniques, including a thorough exploration and usage of time-code technologies, A/B roll editing, switchers, digital video effects, and character generators."

title: Advanced Television Editing
description: Students will develop an understanding of and skill with various computer-controlled editing systems from personal computers to Sony 910 computer controller.

title: Acting for the Camera
description: This course will provide students with a survey of traditional acting techniques and will contrast and compare those techniques to more commonly used contemporary techniques of on-camera actors. Students will learn to prepare and execute discussion with on-camera actors from various educational backgrounds.

title: Advanced Sound
description: Course Description not on file

title: Preproduction and Production Planning
description: This course provides students with provides student with advanced instruction related to their chosen field of specialization while requiring them to coordinate their efforts with those of their crew counterparts, up to the beginning of the thesis project production phase. Credit hours determined bywork load assigned, according to student's area of emphasis. Preproduction of MFA thesis projects. May be repeated during the same semester.

title: HISTORY AND CRITICISM III: THE MODERN ERA
description: ANALYSIS OF CONTEMPORARY AMERICAN & INTERNATIONAL FILM;      REVIEW OF THE TRENDS OF FILMMAKING IN DIFFERENT CULTURES;    USE OF TECHNOLOGY IN DIFFERENT COUNTRIES; PREDICTION OF TRENDS.

title: SYNCHRONOUS AND NON-SYNCHRONOUS SOUND WORKSHOP                    CHANGED TO FIL5256 12/04/90
description: PROVIDES AN UNDERSTANDING OF THE THEORY TECHNOLOGY, AND      TECHNIQUES OF RECORDING SYNCHRONOUS AND NON-SYNCHRONOUS SOUNDAND THE USES OF SOUND IN MOTION PICTURE AND TELEVISION PRODUCTION.

title: Principles and Practices of Technical and Creative Support
description: This course is an introduction to the principle technical and creative support positions in motion picture and television production. Delineates the responsibilities and interrelationships of all preproduction, production, and postproduction personnel.

title: Motion Picture Acting
description: This is a performance-based course designed to teach the basic tenets of acting. Students are introduced to the language of acting, do a variety of acting exercises, and participate in a performance project crafted with the actor’s process in mind. Through this process, students learn skills that can be used to enhance on-camera performances and directing actors. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve credit hours; repeatable within the same term.

title: Critical Studies in Film and Television
description: Examination of a particular theoretical or critical approach to film and television.

title: Critical Methods in Motion Picture, Television, and Recording Arts
description: Principles and practices of writing film/video criticism.

title: Critical Methods of Film Analysis
description: "Film study course providing students with an advanced understanding of the construction of the motion picture narrative language, stressing the students need to develop fluency in visual storytelling through a conscious building of a film literacy. "

title: Advanced Editing
description: This course teaches advanced theories in film editing by experiencing the step-by-step evolution of motion picture editing involving dailies, rough-cut, fine-cut, critique, and addressing story, emotion, structure, transition, pace, rhythm, point-of-interest, stage-line and the smooth cut.

title: Foundations in Directing
description: Through a series lectures, practicum workshops, and weekend assignments, students explore the effective use of visual narrative grammar, the communication tools for directing actors, and practical blocking / shooting techniques used to work on professional sets. May be repeated to a maximum of 12 credit hours and may be repeated within the same term.

title: PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT WORKSHOP
description: PRODUCTION OF MOTION PICTURE, VIDEO PRODUCTS AND TELEVISION  PROGRAMS WITH SPECIAL EMPHASIS ON PRODUCTION AND MANAGEMENT.               MAY BE REPEATED UP TO SIX (6) SEMESTER HOURS.

title: Distribution and Financing Workshop
description: "A comprehensive analysis of the financing and distribution of motion pictures, video products, and television programs with an emphasis on rights acquisition, banking, and legal and contractual procedures and practices."

title: Advanced Workshop in Area of Specialization
description: "Advanced, specialized production training in the student's primary area of production. May be repeated to a maximum of nine (9) semester hours. "

title: Production Management
description: This course introduces students to the production management process as it relates to both short film and feature film production. Through lecture, text and simulated practical application, students acquire a working understanding of film producing from development to exhibition. May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Production Management Workshop
description: "Production of motion pictures, video products, and television programs with special emphasis on production and management. May be repeated to a maximum of six (6) semester hours. "

title: Pre-Visualization
description: This course instructs students in pre-visualization techniques by using industry-standard, 3D tools to model simple structures and objects, animate basic character rigs for staging, and work with virtual cameras to block out shots and narrative sequences. Students apply course concepts in a variety of practical exercises. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve credit hours; repeatable within the same term.

title: Visual Effects
description: This course instructs students in how to make effective choices with practical and digital visual effects. By means of classroom lecture and lab exercises, students practice techniques for creating visual effects that are common within the motion picture industry. Students also practice using industry workflows for planning, communicating, and executing visual effects. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve credit hours; repeatable within the same term.

title: Supervised Research or Creative Activity
description: This course may be repeated to a maximum of six credit hours. However, no more than six credit hours of FIL 5912 and FIL 5941 in combination may be applied toward graduation.

title: Colloquium in Motion Picture, Television, and Recording Arts
description: This course is a specialized study in film/video history, criticism, theory, genres, movements, and filmmakers. May be repeated to a maximum of six credit hours; may be repeated within the same term.

title: Proseminar in Motion Picture, Television, and Recording Arts
description: This course involves interaction with professional film/video makers in screenings and discussions of each other's work. Five semester hours are required for graduation. May be repeated to a maximum of six credit hours.

title: Special Topics in Motion Picture, Television, and Recording Arts
description: This course provides focused instruction in a special topic within the field of motion picture arts. May be repeated during the same term to a maximum of twelve semester hours. May be repeated within the same term.

title: Supervised Teaching
description: May be repeated; however, no more than five (5) semester hours of FIL 5941r and FIL 5912r in combination may be applied toward graduation. May be repeated within the same term.

title: Feature Film Production
description: This advanced practicum course focuses on all aspects of feature film production. Students work in a variety of creative and crew roles on a feature film according to their area of emphasis. May be repeated to a maximum of 12 credit hours. May be repeated within the same term.

title: Teaching Methods in Motion Picture Arts
description: This course introduces students to the theory and practice of learner-centered teaching and the implications for teaching in motion picture arts. Students develop strategies for planning lessons, fostering student engagement, creating an inclusive learning culture, and handling common student concerns and difficulties.

title: MFA Capstone Work in Motion Picture Production
description: This course provides students with an opportunity to design and execute a major creative effort. This includes advance work related to one's chosen area of specialization. May be repeated up to 45 credit hours in total.

title: MFA Capstone Work in Motion Picture Writing
description: This course provides students with an opportunity to develop and write a major creative project. This includes working closely with a faculty advisor throughout the process of writing and revision. May be repeated up to 45 credit hours in total.

title: Apprenticeship
description: This course provides professional, on-the-job training in an area of specialization. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve credit hours.

title: MFA Qualifying Project
description: This project provides students with advanced instruction related to their chosen field of specialization while requiring them to coordinate their efforts with those of their crew counterparts, up to thesis project completion.Credit hours determined by work load assigned, according to student's area of emphasis. Postproduction of MFA thesis projects.

title: MFA Qualifying Exam
description: This exam is an evaluation of first-year progress including public screening of MFA qualifying project and oral examination.

title: Comprehensive Exam
description: Course Description not on file

title: THESIS
description: DESIGN, EXECUTION AND REPORTING OF A MAJOR CREATIVE EFFORT.  (S/U GRADE ONLY) MAY BE REPEATED TO A MAXIMUM OF TWELVE                    (12) SEMESTER HOURS.

title: MFA PROJECT
description: PRODUCTION OF A FULL LENGTH FILM/VIDEO WITH STUDENTS         CONTRIBUTING IN THEIR AREAS OF SPECIALIZATION. (S/U GRADE    ONLY) MAY BE REPEATED ONCE, NO MORE THAN 15 SEMESTER HOURS MAY BE APPLIED.

title: Thesis
description: In this course, students are given the opportunity to design, execute, and report a major creative effort. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve semester hours.

title: Master's Thesis Defense
description: Course Description not on file

title: MFA Thesis Production
description: This course is the production of MFA thesis projects. The course provides students with advanced instruction related to their chosen field of specialization while requiring them to coordinate their efforts with those of theircrew counterparts, up to the thesis project postproduction phase. Credit hours determined by work load assigned, according to student's area of emphasis. May be repeated one time to a maximum of fifteen semester hours. May berepeated during the same semester.

title: Defense of MFA Project
description: Course Description not on file

title: MFA QUALIFYING PROJECT
description: POST-PRODUCTION OF MFA THESIS PROJECTS. PROVIDES STUDENTS    WITH ADVANCED INSTRUCTION RELATED TO THEIR CHOSEN FIELD OF   SPECIALIZATION....MAY BE REPEATED UP TO FIFTEEN (15) SEMESTER HOURS.

title: Personal Investment Concepts I
description: This course offers an in-depth application of investment concepts for financial planning, including an introduction to equity and fixed income securities, investment risk and return, behavioral finance, portfolio theory, and asset valuation. Current and emerging issues, regulations, and strategies are an integral part of the course.

title: PERSONAL INVESTMENT MANAGEMENT
description: INTRODUCTION TO INVESTMENT PLANNING FOR NON-BUSINESS MAJORS.  BASIC TYPES OF INVESTMENT MEDIA; THE OPERATIONS OF SECURITY MARKETS; RISKS AND RETURNS IN PLANNING.                                      0

title: Personal Risk Management & Insurance Planning
description: This course covers the essentials of risk management and insurance planning for financial planning practice. Broad topics include health insurance, disability insurance, life insurance, long-term care insurance, Social Security and pension policies, financial markets, risk mitigation, and the various statutes that govern and regulate financial planning.

title: Introduction to Financial Planning
description: This course introduces the field of financial planning, covers economic and finance principles relevant to financial planning, and provides contemporary knowledge about the practice areas of financial planning.

title: Personal Finance
description: This course is a study of the concepts and processes in planning, analyzing, and controlling personal financial resources. The course emphasizes financial planning, cash and credit management, managing expenditures, income and asset protection, investment planning, and retirement and estate planning. For non-business majors only. Credit not allowed for business majors.

title: Psychology for Financial Planning
description: This course examines topics within Psychology that apply to personal and family financial planning. Emphasis is placed on the six Principal Knowledge Topics within the Psychology of Financial Planning domain assessed on the CFP® exam. These topics include: client and planner attitudes, values, and biases; behavioral finance; sources of money conflict; principles of counseling; etc.

title: Ethics for Financial Planning
description: This course is designed to apply concepts of ethics from the perspectives of philosophy, psychology, business, and the financial planning fields. Students study the use of ethics in practice, considering client needs and the ethical and legal issues that apply to the financial planning field. These include business models, sources of compensation, regulatory requirements.

title: Financial Markets, Institutions, and International Finance Systems
description: This course focuses on money and capital markets, financial institutions, financial systems, and financial environment including an introduction to investments. Emphasizes the microfinancial decision-making process of the business firm.

title: Financial Management of the Firm
description: This course is an examination of the basic concepts involved in the investment, financing, and dividend decisions of the business firm. Managerial orientation with emphasis on identification, analysis, and solution of financial problems confronting the firm.

title: Personal Investment Concepts II
description: This course offers an in-depth application of investment strategies used by professional asset managers and institutional investors, including passive and active management techniques, stock options, derivatives, futures, and alternative asset classes. Taxation of investment securities is also covered. Current and emerging issues, regulations, and strategies are an integral part of the course.

title: Advanced Financial Planning
description: This course provides an advanced and in-depth application of personal and family financial management principles with an emphasis on analyzing the elements of a personal financial plan: income and expenses, credit, savings, insurance, investments, taxes, and financial behavior. Completion demonstrates an understanding of the first give steps in financial planning.

title: Estate Planning for Financial Planning
description: This course covers advanced topics in estate planning for financial planning practitioners, including legal and tax strategies for preservation and transfer of family wealth based on personal financial goals and circumstances.

title: Retirement Planning Concepts for Financial Planning
description: This course provides an in-depth understanding of retirement planning, and factors associated with planning for retirement, so that students obtain a strong foundation for developing financial plans. This course covers an array of topics determined by the financial planning industry to be pertinent to financial planning practice.

title: Commercial Bank Administration
description: This course is a study of the operations and administration of commercial banks and their role in the money and capital markets. Examines banking regulation, the lending function, investments, and the financial decision-making process.

title: Current Issues in Banking
description: An examination of current and topical issues in banking that are important for policy formation and the future of the banking industry.

title: Short-Term Financial Management
description: This course focuses on the decisions impacting the short-term cash flows of organizations-public, private, governmental, and non-profit. Topics include: cash management, treasury management, and working capital management.

title: Problems in Financial Management
description: This course gives students an understanding of the underlying principles of finance from the decision-making perspective of financial managers. Topics include: project valuation and capital budgeting decisions, return and risk analysis, capital structure, payout policy, and corporate governance. Case studies are included.

title: VENTURE CAPITAL AND PRIVATE EQUITY
description: This course provides a comprehensive overview of the private equity industry with an emphasis on venture capital investing. The private equity industry is the set of investment funds that raise capital to buy ownership (equity) in private companies. This industry provides an important source of capital and managerial expertise throughout the life cycle of a firm.

title: Financial Modeling and Forecasting
description: This course is an introduction to financial modeling and forecasting. Emphasis is on computer models and forecasting financial variables.

title: Investments
description: This course is an introduction to investment/security analysis. Includes an examination of investment instruments, the investment environment, the concept of risk-return, and the interactive forces between the economy, industries, and individual firms.

title: Security Analysis and Portfolio Management
description: This course is an advanced and comprehensive coverage of investment topics including bond analysis, stock options, interest rate futures, options on futures contracts, portfolio analysis and management, and security market efficiency.

title: Derivatives and Risk Management
description: This course examines the theoretical foundations and practical applications of derivative securities, including forwards, futures, options, and swaps. Students explore how derivatives are structured, priced, and used to manage risk, while analyzing their role in innovation, speculation, and financial crises.

title: Fixed Income
description: This course focuses on fixed income securities and global debt markets. This course defines the key characteristics of fixed income securities, and discusses the primary types of bonds. Students receive an overview of the Chartered Financial Analyst (CFA) Institute Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct.

title: Multinational Financial Management
description: This course introduces the environment of international capital and foreign exchange markets and examines the effects of the international business environment on risk, capital budgeting, working capital management, and capital structure decisions of the firm.

title: Strategic Consulting for Finance
description: This course helps students leverage creative skills to find solutions for business challenges. The course uses hands-on techniques and exercises including case studies and in-class activities to teach students how to gather data, transform it into useful information, locate problem areas, generate ideas, and choose optimal solutions rooted in ethics and values. This course helps students determine the right approach to understanding business problems by teaching them to ask the right questions, improving their judgment, and optimizing their decision making. Current consulting strategies and techniques, including best practices, continuous improvement, business process outsourcing and others will be reviewed and discussed.

title: Directed Individual Study
description: This course permits study or exploration into a specialized topic of finance that is not included in one of the other finance courses. It also permits advanced and extensive study of finance topics beyond that included in the other finance courses. The study is conducted with the direct supervision of an individual faculty member. This course may not be used as one of the two required finance electives detailed in the major requirements. May be repeated to a maximum of five times with the requirement that the topic changes each time. Consent of the department chairperson is required.

title: Career Readiness and Capstone in Personal Financial Planning
description: In this course, students apply the concepts and strategies that they have learned throughout their coursework in the major by developing a comprehensive financial plan.

title: Senior Seminar in Finance
description: May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours as topics vary. Additional prerequisites may be required depending on the topic

title: Finance Internship
description: This internship is designed for College of Business students who desire to gain real world experience in the finance field through on-the-job practice. Students work under the direction of an approved industry professional, a faculty advisor, and the internship director.

title: Securities Industry Essentials
description: This course is an introduction to the securities industry and provides preparation for the FINRA Securities Industry Essentials (SIE) examination. Individuals interested in pursuing employment as a representative-legal registrant (e.g., analysts at banks, brokerage firms, investment managers, and wealth managers) must pass the SIE examination.

title: Honors in the Major Research
description: In this course, students accepted into the Honors in the Major program complete an original research or creative project in their major area of study. This course must be repeated at least twice to complete a minimum of six (6) credit hours total, but may be repeated up to a maximum of twelve credit hours in total.

title: Fundamentals of Personal Finance
description: This course is an introduction to an individual's financial decisions. Course topics include budgeting, saving, insuring, debt servicing, investing, retiring, and estate planning by individuals. Cannot be applied for credit for any graduate business degree.

title: Investment Banking
description: This course introduces the structure and major activities of investment banks, including trends in the investment banking business.

title: Administration of Financial Institutions
description: Analysis of institutions constituting the money markets within the framework of their respective operation and administration.

title: Financial Institutions and Risk Management
description: This course covers the identification of key risks facing bank managers, as well as modern techniques for measuring, pricing, and managing those risks.

title: Introduction to Financial Management
description: "An introduction to the terminology, methodology, and basic decision models of finance; emphasis on working capital management, budgeting, and structure; the dividend decision. "

title: Financial Data Analytics
description: This course provides an introduction and understanding of how financial data analytics are used in business, specifically through the use of R or Python. Students develop data analysis skills that enable them to implement financial modeling across a variety of topics while utilizing a variety of common financial and economic data sources.

title: Investment Management and Analysis
description: This course offers an analysis of financial assets with emphasis on the securities market, the valuation of individual securities, and portfolio management.

title: ADVANCED INVESTMENT MANAGEMENT
description: IN-DEPTH STUDY OF PORFOLIO MANAGEMENT; OPTIONS; COMODITIES;  BONDS;AND CONTEMPORARY THEORIES OF CAPITAL MARKETS. KNOWLEDGE                 OF INTRODUCTORY INVESTMENT MANAGEMENT.

title: Portfolio Management and Wealth Planning
description: This course provides an advanced exploration of portfolio management techniques, wealth planning strategies, and their practical application.  Students will develop a deep understanding of how to construct, evaluate, and manage portfolios for both private clients and institutional investors. The use of tools like FactSet will ensure students gain hands-on experience with industry-standard software.

title: Financial Derivatives and Risk Management
description: This course covers issues related to the pricing and application of futures, swaps, and options. Emphasis is placed on different methods of valuing derivative securities and of hedging macroeconomic and firm-specific risks. The course examines real-world examples of derivative uses and how they impact firm value.

title: Fixed Income Securities
description: This course describes important fixed income securities and markets, and develops tools for valuing fixed income securities and managing interest rate risk. The course covers traditional bonds and term structure concepts as well as fixed income derivatives and interest rate modeling.

title: SEMINAR IN MACRO FINANCE
description: DETAILED ANALYSIS OF THEORY AND PRACTICE OF FINANCIAL INSTITUTIONS WITHIN THE CONSTRUCT OF DIFFERING IMPACTS OF CONDITIONS IN THE NATIONAL ECONOMY.

title: Financial Modeling for Investment Banking & Corporate Finance
description: In this course, students develop a strong command of corporate finance principles and actively apply them by building financial models commonly used by investment banking and commercial banking analysts. Students will work through the full transaction process for each topic, constructing pitch decks and complete models from the ground up. Lectures will emphasize the critical role financial analysts play in each stage of the process.

title: VALUATION THEORY
description: EXAMINE THEORETICAL METHODS APPLICABLE TO FINANCIAL VALUATIONAND TO FINANCIAL DECISIONS.  Ã¿ÂPURGED SUMMER 1997Ã¿Â¨

title: Applied Econometrics in Finance
description: This course covers statistical techniques commonly employed in financial studies. Students examine actual applications within a variety of financial studies in order to learn how to conduct statistical tests and interpret their results, and also familiarize themselves with WRDS and a variety of databases in order to learn how to conduct research.

title: Special Studies in Management
description: May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours. May be repeated within the same term.

title: Supervised Research
description: This course is for master's candidates only. A maximum of three hours may apply towards the master's degree. May be repeated to a maximum of five credit hours. May be repeated within the same term.

title: Seminar on Current Topics in Finance
description: This seminar is an in-depth study of current topics in finance. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve credit hours; may be repeated within the same term.

title: Supervised Teaching
description: A maximum of three semester hours may apply to the master's degree.

title: Thesis
description: A minimum of six semester hours is required.

title: Seminar in Finance
description: This seminar focuses on the corporate finance literature with topics including the theory of financial management, cash and working capital management, capital budgeting and rationing; and financing decisions of the firm.

title: Seminar in Finance - Investments
description: This seminar studies the development of investment theories and empirical research. Topics include asset pricing, utility analysis, risk measurement, the structure and efficiency of security markets, as well as other current issues in investments.

title: Foundations of financial theory
description: This course places emphasis on the foundations of financial theories and provides an in-depth examination of the major theoretical developments in finance, including the study of related empirical tests.

title: Foundations of Financial Theory
description: Emphasis on the foundations of financial theories with a thorough examination of the major theoretical developments of finance including the study of related empirical tests.

title: Research Methods in Finance
description: This course offers a critical examination of empirical research in finance and its related issues including design, methodology, analysis, and critique. Students utilize financial databases with appropriate quantitative techniques to design and conduct empirical research.

title: Dissertation
description: A minimum of twenty-four credit hours is required; may be repeated within the same term.

title: Doctoral Preliminary Examination
description: May be taken up to two times; may be repeated within the same term.

title: Master's Comprehensive Examination
description: Course Description not on file

title: Master's Thesis Defense
description: Course Description not on file

title: Dissertation Defense Examination
description: May be repeated within the same term.
