description: AFTER ALL COURSE WORK

title: DISSERTATION DEFENSE EXAMINATION
description: AFTER ALL COURSE WORK

title: Introduction to Teaching Foreign/Second Languages
description: This course is designed to meet the needs of those teaching second languages abroad and pre-service teachers in K-12 foreign/second language education by developing an understanding of current theories of second language learning through exploration of relevant research. Opportunities are provided for students to use the theoretical base in the design of classroom lessons.

title: INTRODUCTION TO BILINGUAL-BICULTURAL EDUCATION
description: TERMS ARE DEFINED;HISTORICAL OVERVIEW/DEMOGRAPHIC DATA GIVEN;DISUCSSION FOR/ORGANIZATION OF THE BILINGUAL SCHOOL;                          IMPLICATIONS FOR EDUCATION AND SOCIETY.                     0

title: Second Language Testing and Evaluation
description: This course is designed to acquaint students with principles of second language assessment and standardized testing, to inform them of general principles of second language test construction and administration, including traditional and nontraditional assessments, and to provide practical experiences in preparing valid items and analyzing tests.

title: Applied Linguistics for Second Language Learning
description: Course is designed for preservice teachers in the Elementary, Early childhood, and English Education programs who will teach limited English proficient and other linguistic minority students preK - 12.

title: Practicum in Multilingual/Multicultural Education
description: Practical techniques for classroom instruction of basic foreign language skills; teaching intermediate and advanced levels; use and construction of foreign language tests; techniques of planning, classroom management, ethics and school law.

title: Associate Teaching in a Foreign Language
description: Course Description not on file

title: BILINGUAL INSTRUCTION IN THE CONTENT AREAS
description: TEACHING OF CONTENT SUBJECT AREAS IN A BILINGUAL SETTING.

title: Seminar in Bilingual/Bicultural Education
description: "Introduction, definitions, psychological, social, and cultural factors, program design, the languages of instruction, pedagogy, evaluation, research, and legislation. "

title: Development of Curriculum and Materials in Foreign Languages
description: Students begin with a review of L2 learning stages and of contemporary curricular designs that pertain to teaching second/foreign languages. They learn to analyze existing curricula, materials and technology, and participate in the process of developing original units and materials.

title: Reading in Foreign Language Instruction
description: Against a backdrop of current theories, issues, and research in first and second language reading, students select from a range of reading approaches to deveoop reading units and activities for specific kinds of learners, including those with low literacy and L2 proficiency.

title: Seminar: Teaching Foreign Languages
description: Approaches and methodologies for teaching foreign languages in the traditional classroom setting (K-12).

title: Teaching of Culture: Multicultural/Multilingual Perspectives
description: This course examines culture and its scope, identification of goals and development of course objectives. Students locate and analyze sources, materials and activities. They learn how to teach and measure cultural achievement.

title: Testing and Evaluation in Foreign Languages
description: This course focuses on language assessment with objectives related to classroom assessment, proficiency testing and standardized testing. Students also develop expertise in basic statistical processes and program assessment.

title: Seminar: Research in Second Language Learning and Teaching
description: This course is a comprehensive overview of second language learning and learners. Additionally, students examine the major theories and concepts associated with second language acquisition in naturalistic, classroom, and laboratory settings.

title: Applied Linguistics in Foreign Language Teaching
description: Students in this course address the major areas of linguistics including phonology, morphology, syntax, semantics, acquisition, language socialization and variation. Students are expected to relate these to cross-linguistic issues in classrooms and provide ways to assist L2 learners in reading and language arts.

title: Seminar: Special Topics in Applied Linguistics
description: This course addresses any topic relevant to the broader field of multilingual/multicultural education and may be repeated to a maximum of twelve (12) semester hours.

title: Teaching Classics
description: This course prepares graduate students in classics for their role as teachers of undergraduates in lower-level courses in etymology, classical civilization, myth and Latin.

title: SUPERVISED TEACHING        CHG TO FRE5940

title: Food Science and Technology
description: "Introduces basic food science vocabulary, processing techniques, principles and quality control parameters, as well as professional opportunities. An elective course for non-majors."

title: Introduction to Food and Nutrition Sciences
description: This course informs students on the scope of health-related research and professions, such as health professional graduate programs and the academic, professional, and personal preparation needed to pursue a career in healthcare or research in food and nutrition sciences.

description: Course Description not on file

title: FOODS LABORATORY
description: COST CONTROL IN FOOD PURCHASING; GOVERNMENT CONTROL OF THE   FOOD SUPPLY; PRODUCTION/PROCESSING OF MAJOR FOOD CLASSES

title: Foods
description: This course is an introduction to the physiochemical properties of food and the relationship of these properties to preparation techniques and food quality. Management and service of food.

title: Foods Laboratory
description: This course is an introduction to the physiochemical properties of food and the relationship of these properties to preparation techniques and food quality. Management and service of food.

title: Food and the Consumer
description: This course explores food composition, nutritional quality, and safety. Influence of food processing on food quality and safety. Consumer aspects of food including food selection, food management, and safety.

title: Food Science
description: This course discusses the chemistry of foods and their behavior during processing. Assessment of food quality.

title: Food Safety and Quality
description: In this course, topics include food spoilage and food poisoning, food-borne pathogens, food laws and regulations, HACCP and safe food handler practices, with an emphasis on current issues related to the quality and safety of food.

title: FOOD SCIENCE
description: LABORATORY AND LECTURE

title: Tutorial in Food Science
description: Small group discussions or project work. Topics selected in contemporary issues or current research. Maximum enrollment of ten (10) students per tutorial. Repeatable when topics change.

title: Food Preservation
description: This course focuses on fundamental considerations in the preservation of foods by freezing, canning, dehydration, ionizing radiations, etc.

title: Seminar in Food and Nutrition Sciences
description: This course consists of student and faculty presentations on research and developments in food science and nutrition.

title: Selected Topics in Food Science and Technology
description: This course is an investigation of current research related to selected topics in food science and technology.

title: Physical and Chemical Techniques in Food and Nutrition
description: This course provides an experimental approach to food and nutrition research, and it may involve the study of foods, humans, or animal models and a variety of specialized instruments.

title: Experiments in Digital World Literatures
description: This course focuses on digital rhetorical forms and the evolution of print into screenic, multi-media settings with an emphasis on digital rhetorical forms and digital tools of literary analysis. Students explore definitions of digital literacy through readings of world literature with digital tools.

title: Franco-American Culture Wars
description: This course examines a major shift in the cultural balance of power between the United States and France. description: This course is for master and doctoral students in the Department of Modern Languages needing to fulfill credit hours that are not part of the regular course requirements, or of DIS and/or Research hours offered in their respective programs. To be used for instance for MA and PhD Prelim exam preparation.

title: Elementary French I
description: This course stresses oral comprehension, speaking, reading, and writing. May not be taken by native speakers. May not be taken concurrently with FRE 1121 and/or FRE 2220.

title: Elementary French II
description: This course places further emphasis on oral comprehension, speaking, reading and writing. May not be taken by native speakers. May not be taken concurrently with FRE 1120 and/or FRE 2220.

title: Intermediate French Readings
description: This course offers a rapid review of basic French structures and introduces some of the finer points of French grammar. May not be taken concurrently with FRE 1120, FRE 1121, and FRE 2220 or by native speakers.

title: Intermediate French
description: This intermediate course offers a rapid overview of basic French grammar and expands students' oral and written French expression through structured activities and compositions. May not be taken concurrently with FRE1120, FRE 1121, or by native speakers.

title: FRENCH FOR SINGERS I
description: THE COURSE IS DESIGNED TO TEACH MUSIC STUDENTS TO READ ;SPEAKAND COMPREHEND FRENCH SYNTAX IN ALL TENSES; MODES AND VOICES                  AS WELL AS GIVE THEM AN ACTIVE VOCABULARY. FOR VOICE MAJORS.

title: FRENCH FOR SINGERS II
description: THE COURSE IS DESIGNED TO TEACH MUSIC STUDENTS TO READ SPEAK AND COMPREHEND FRENCH SYNTAX IN ALL TENSES; MODES AND VOICES                  AS WELL AS GIVE THEM AN ACTIVE VOCABULARY. FOR VOICE MAJORS.

title: Intermediate French Conversation
description: Through readings and films about contemporary issues facing French society, this course aims at developing oral communication skills in a broad cultural context.

title: French Grammar and Composition I
description: This course is an in-depth study of French grammar emphasizing subtleties of written expression.

title: French Grammar and Composition II
description: This course is a further study of the subtleties of written expression in the French language.

title: Commercial French
description: This course develops language and correspondence skills appropriate to     business transactions in such areas as sales, finance, transportation, management, etc.

title: Contemporary France
description: This course, taught entirely in French, provides the student with an under standing of French culture and society from WWII through the present day. Topics include high vs. popular culture, political life, Franco-American relations, economics, media, France and the European Union, social interactions of the French, family life, education systems, religion, cuisine, and immigration and multiculturalism. Activities and assignments emphasize French writing and speaking skills.

title: Chanson
description: Does not count toward requirement for the French major but may count toward a minor in French. A survey of the popular French song from early 20th century to modern rock. In English.

title: French Phonetics
description: This course targets pronunciation practice using the phonetic alphabet with the objective of improving production of standard French pronunciation. French majors only.

title: Advanced Conversation
description: This course is about oral expression, listening skills, and vocabulary acquisition in French with a variety of domains, using contemporary materials.

title: Advanced Grammar and Composition
description: This course aims at developing writing and speaking ability at an advanced level through a review of French grammar, an introduction to Comparative Stylistics of French and English, the reading of sophisticated French prose, and the writing of two research papers.

title: French Culture and Civilization
description: This course provides the student with an understanding of French culture and society from WWII until the present day. Topics include high vs. popular culture, political life, Franco-American relations, economics, media, France and the European Union, social interaction of the French, family life, education systems, religion, immigration and multiculturalism, food, etc. Activities and assignments also practice writing and speaking skills in French.

title: Phonetics: Theoretical and Applied
description: Study of the International Phonetic Alphabet and its application to French with practice in reproducing accurately French sounds and intonation patterns.

title: Directed Individual Study
description: In this course, students arrange with individual faculty members to undertake specialized study in areas outside of or in addition to the regular curriculum. May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Special Topics
description: This course allows students to study literary topics of a special kind, depending on student interest and faculty expertise. May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours.

title: Honors in the Major Research
description: In this course, students accepted into the Honors in the Major program complete an original research or creative project in their major area of study. This course must be repeated at least twice to complete a minimum of six (6) credit hours total, but may be repeated up to a maximum of twelve (12) credit hours in total.

title: Internship in Applied French
description: This course provides academic credit for students working in governmental agencies or private business where students employ the foreign language. Departmental permission required. May be repeated to a maximum of six (6) credit hours; repeatable within the same term.

title: Graduate Reading Knowledge in French
description: This course is designed to present structures of the French language and vocabulary to prepare graduate students majoring in other disciplines to read learned journals, books, and monographs written in French useful for the student's research in humanities, natural or social sciences.

title: Reading Knowledge Examination
description: This course is a translation examination to ascertain the student's ability to read research materials written in French. Use of translation software is prohibited.

title: Stylistics
description: This course is a systematic study of the stylistics and idiomatic differences between French and English, designed to improve writing skills.

title: French and Francophone Cultures
description: This course covers developments in France and in the wider Francophone (French-speaking) world since the Second World War. The course explores the institutions of the Fifth Republic, the evolution of ideas since May 1968, and the emergence of new artistic movements in France. The course also examines the rise of Francophone cultures in the former colonies in Africa, the Caribbean, and elsewhere. May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Post-Colonial Cultures in France
description: This course examines the new cultural practices being forged in France by writers, filmmakers and musicians mixing elements from African, Caribbean, French, American and other sources. It is taught in French.

title: Introduction to Global French Studies
description: This course introduces graduate students to graduate work in French studies and traces the disciplinary shifts in this field. This course focuses on Global French studies as a way of understanding the literary and cultural production of the French-speaking world through its spatial and temporal relations.

title: Old French
description: In this course, the primary objectives are to acquire a reading knowledge of the language and to learn basic concepts concerning its structure and development.

title: Readings in Old French Language
description: This course is a diachronic study of short works written in Old French. The goal is to introduce students to major genres and authors and to increase their reading knowledge of the language.

title: Structure of Modern French (Graduate)
description: "Systematic study of the phonology, morphology, and syntax of modern French. "

title: Studies in French Language and Literature
description: This course varies in content as student's needs are addressed. May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours.

title: Teaching Practicum
description: A maximum of three semester hours may apply to the master's degree. May be repeated to a maximum of five semester hours.

title: Tutorial in Professional Issues
description: This tutorial is an advanced professional preparation course to acquaint students with issues in their academic discipline. A maximum of three semester hours may count towards the degree. May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours.

title: INTRODUCTION TO FRENCH LINGUISTICS
description: In this course, students investigate the language beginning with the smallest linguistic structure up to the largest: sounds (phonetics and phonology), word structure (morphology), sentence structure (syntax), meaning (semantics) and how these elements fit together at the discursive or pragmatic level. The course also focuses on how native speakers navigate casual and formal speech depending upon social situations including dialectical and individual variations.

title: Masterworks of French Literature in Translation
description: This course is a survey of selected masterpieces of French literature, ranging from the Middle Ages to the present. The readings and instruction are in English.

title: Paris World Capital
description: This course examines Paris and its monuments in a range of texts and media to promote deeper understanding of the city's sense of place and iconic status, as well as its long-standing attraction as a site of world heritage and global cultural encounter.

title: Cultures of the Caribbean
description: This course provides an overview of Caribbean cultures, geography, and history through an analysis of texts, films, music, and performances. It focuses on how aesthetic practices shape and are shaped by transnational cultural exchanges and by colonialism in its past and present forms.

title: French and Francophone Cinema
description: This Francophone cinema course is offered in two versions: one focusing on the relationship between cinema and Francophone cultures and societies, and another taking a chronological and thematic approach to the movements and directors of metropolitan French cinema. This course is taught in English and, with instructor permission, three hours may be used for major or minor credit. May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours.

title: French Women Writers
description: This course addresses issues of race, gender, and class in a selection of works written by prominent French/Francophone writers. Taught in English. Can be used for minor credit with permission of the coordinator.

title: Immigration and National Identity in France
description: This interdisciplinary course examines the ways in which immigration and ethnicity have been reshaping the contours of contemporary French society and culture. It is taught in English.

title: MASTERPIECES OF FRENCH LITERATURE IN TRANSLATION
description: Course Description not on file

title: Survey of French Literature I: Early-Modern France
description: This course is an introduction to the study of early-modern French literature by reading and discussing works representative of the various schools and movements in their cultural contexts.

title: Survey of French Literature II: Modern France
description: This course is an introduction to the study modern French Literature by reading and discussing works representative of the various schools and movements. (Spring semester only.)

title: French Cinema
description: Study of the movements and directors of French Cinema with emphasis on the postwar New Wave. Three hours may be used for major or minor credit with permission of the instructor.

title: Medieval and Renaissance Literature
description: This course is an introduction to the poetry and prose of the medieval and early-modern periods. Emphasis is on the themes of love and friendship.

title: 17th- and 18th-Century Literature
description: This course surveys major works in the areas of theatre, philosophy, and prose fiction. Special attention is given to the possible meanings of concepts such as Classicism and Enlightenment.

title: 19th-Century Literature
description: This course focuses on major themes and issues in 19th-century literature and culture.

title: 20th-Century Literature

title: French Literature of Quebec

May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Francophone Caribbean/African Cultures

title: Classical Theatre of the 17th Century

title: Critical History of the French Theatre: Postclassicism to the Theatre of the Absurd

title: Critical History of French Poetry: Origins to Baudelaire

Course readings focus on the field's fundamental arguments as well as new scholarship. Students will develop greater understanding of this topic through their own consistent analysis and written work.

May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours.

description: This course is designed for PhD students who have completed all of their required course work and are preparing to sit for their preliminary examinations in the current semester. May be repeated to a maximum of twenty-four semester hours.

title: Doctoral Issues in Professional Development
description: This seminar focuses on a wide range of issues pertaining to careers as business scholars, and provides a versatile vehicle to impart knowledge and build skill on issues in the field that typically are not covered in traditional Ph.D. content and methods seminars.  Through reading assignments, discussion, simulations, and webcasts, students gain an appreciation for the many issues that will challenge them as they seek to build and manage a successful career in the business academics.

title: COOPERATIVE EDUCATION WORK EXPERIENCE
description: MAY BE REPEATED THREE TIMES

title: Environmental Science
description: This course explores the causes of local and global environmental problems and their impacts, including resource use, pollution, ecosystems, and population growth.

title: Human Geography
description: This course is an introductory survey of geographic theories, issues and applications from the human perspective. In particular, how people interact with each other politically, economically, culturally and socially across distances, scales and within various physical environments. In addition, global contrasts are examined using urban versus rural habitation, local versus transnational trade, and uneven economic development.

title: Colloquium in Social Science and Public Affairs
description: This course provides a venue for discussing key issues and controversies that students are likely to encounter as they pursue a major in one of the social sciences. Students learn critical skills in debating public policy issues while also gaining an appreciation for topics that shape everyday life. Those topics are impacted by social forces, economic dynamics, and governmental decisions around the world. May be repeated to a maximum of two hours.

title: Physical Geography
description: This course is an overview of earth-sun relations, weather, climate, landforms, water systems, soils, and vegetation.

title: Map Analysis
description: "An introduction to the acquisition, processing, and presentation of cartographic data."

title: GEOGRAPHIC INFORMATION SYSTEMS
description: ACQUIRING STORING RETRIEVING MAPPING AND ANALYZING GEOGRAPHICINFORMATION.

title: Sports Geography
description: This course focuses on the geographical basis of sports at different spatial scales, including locational strategies of franchises, recruiting patterns, and the urban political economy of professional sports arenas.

title: Economic Geography
description: This course examines the geography of economic activity at local, national, and global scales: historical development of capitalism, regional development, spatial structure of agriculture, manufacturing and services, the global economy, third world poverty, and population growth.

title: Cooperative Education Work Experience
description: Course Description not on file

title: Survey of Geographic Thought
description: Changing philosophies of space.

title: CARTOGRAPHY AND GRAPHICS
description: THIS IS A TRADITIONAL STUDIO OFFERING IN WHICH THE STUDENT ISFIRST REQURIED TO ACQUIRE SUCH FUNDAMENTAL SKILLS AS DRAFTING                 AND MECHANICAL LETTERING IN INK.                            0

title: Environmental Field Methods
description: This course focuses on the design and implementation of a field-based project employing field sampling, GIS, GPS, and exploratory statistical methods.

title: GEOGRAPHIC INFORMATION PROCESSING AND SYSTEMS
description: ADVANCED SEMINAR ON GIS TOPICS,INCLUDING LOCATIONAL CONTROL, SPATIAL DATA STRUCTURES,SPAT./CART. STATS,MODELING & ANALYSIS& FUTURE TRENDS IN DECISION SUPPORT, SENSORS, & GEOGRAPHIC METHODS.

title: Geographic Information Processing and Systems
description: "Survey of GIS topics, including locational control, spatial data structures, modeling and analysis, and future trends in decision support, sensors, and geographic methods."

title: Spatial Data Analysis
description: This course is an introduction to the quantitative analysis of geographic data that explores clustering, spatial patterns, and intrinsic relationships between geographic variables.

title: Computer Cartography
description: Examination of computer mapping systems, theory, methodology, and applications.

title: ADVANCED PHYSICAL GEOGRAPHY
description: SYSTEMS AND GLOBAL PATTERN OF SOILS AND LANDFORMS.

title: Landforms and Landscapes
description: This course is on the spatial distribution of geomorphic landforms across landscapes: how they form, how they change over time, how they are designated, and their nomenclature. Emphasis is given to how humans interact with these landscapes and how these landscapes can impact human habitation.

title: Geography of Climate Change and Storms
description: This course explores the critical debate on global climatic fluctuations and extreme weather frequency in relation to human impact and interference. Particular focus is given to geographic variations and temporal validity.

title: Soils and Landforms
description: "Hydraulic and geomorphological processes, and soils types and management."

title: Geography of Water Resources
description: This course provides students with a comprehensive overview of the natural processes associated with water occurrence and resources. Focus is given to water's unique properties, how it occurs and moves through Earth's environment; how it impacts human habitation, and its future as a critical and valuable natural resource. Development of socio-economic concepts of management, supply, use, reclamation, and sustainability are also emphasized.

title: Biogeography
description: This course examines the spatial distributions of flora and fauna, ecosystem change, and human interventions such as logging, invasive species, and wilderness preservation.

title: ENVIRONMENTAL PERCEPTION
description: IMPLICATIONS FOR GEOGRAPHIC ANALYSIS;THEORETICAL PROBLEMS;   CASE STUDIES;SCALING; POTENTIAL RESEARCH APPLICATIONS.                                                                                    0

title: Living in a Hazardous Environment
description: This course explores types of environmental hazards (natural and human-made) and their effects, techniques for the analysis of risks, and strategies for recovering losses.

title: Environmental Disasters & Apocalypse
description: This course covers theoretical debates on climate change and the Anthropocene, linking them to cinematic and cultural tropes of disaster and apocalypse. Students explore how anthropogenic climate change is being shaped by the actions of humans who have solely contributed to the massive build-up of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere since industrialization; the increased amounts of radioactivity in the layers of earth and ice; and repercussions from over-population.

title: Geography: Food and Environment
description: This course explores food production, distribution and consumption by intensive global agro-food and local organic farm operations, and their impacts on environmental sustainability.

title: Environmental Conflict and Economic Development
description: This course examines controversies over the use, transformation, and destruction of nature, including political ecology.

title: NATURAL RESOURCES AND ENVIRONMENTAL POLICY
description: HISTORICAL DEVELOPMENT OF POLICIES CONCERNING NATURAL RESOURCES FROM COLONIAL PERIOD TO PRESENT; CURRENT ISSUES IN CONSERVATION AND ENVIRONMENT.                                                       0

title: Natural Resource Assessment and Analysis
description: This course examines the assessment and analysis of policies concerning natural resources and environmental management in the U.S. and internationally.

title: Landscape Ecology
description: This course offers a review of methods on analyzing geographic patterns of natural phenomena, including ecological conservation, natural resource management, landscape and urban planning, as well as human-environmental interactions and implications. Familiarity with software packages such as ArcGIS is assumed.

title: Geography of Marine Conservation
description: This course outlines the major conservation issues in coastal and marine systems worldwide, including the science, management and policy dimensions of marine conservation. The course explores critical conservation problems facing marine ecosystems; and at the same time explore their causes and threats from climate change, over-fishing, and other types of natural resource extraction and management failures. Students explore solutions, both science-based, and social science-based (particularly economics, management and policy implementation).

title: Global Change, Local Places
description: This course examines four aspects of global change--economic, environmental, cultural, and political--with a focus on how globalization is impacting individual countries and how places are responding to globalization's challenges.

title: Black Geographies
description: This course addresses the historical, political, and spatial contexts in which geographies of black populations emerge throughout the United States and beyond. The course seeks to investigate ways in which black communities throughout the African Diaspora are spatially marginalized, and the ways in which Black communities produce space. The course discusses race, racism, alongside conversations of spatial marginalization (e.g. segregation).

title: Environment and Gender
description: In this course, students look at how physical space (be it national boundaries or public parks) and the terrain of the symbolic realm are sometimes at odds. Included in this investigation is the examination of how ideas of gender, place and space affect individuals’ experiences and how said experiences are created and limited by ideas of space at various geographical scales and contrasts between more and less economically developed nations.

title: Cultural Geography
description: The study of the processes by which various cultural features have diffused throughout the world. Emphasis is on the contemporary cultural landscape.

title: Medical Geography
description: This course applies geographical concepts and techniques to health-related problems, including the ecology of health, disease diffusion, medical cartography, and health care access.

title: Historical Geography
description: Concepts, approaches, and research methods for analysis of spatial pattern s of the past and changes through time.

title: Political Geography
description: This course focuses on the spatial dimensions of political processes from the local to the global level, including elections and geopolitics of the world system.

title: Military Geography
description: "The geography of warfare; tactics and terrain, strategy and the theater of war, insurgency, war in cities, geopolitics and grand strategy."

title: Globalization
description: This course explores the concepts and processes that define a world system of commodity production, labor costs, and cultural exchange.

title: Fossil Fuels and Environmental Conflicts
description: This course addresses the politics of oil, not simply in terms of the policies that governments adopt, but also in terms of how petroleum has fundamentally shaped conceptions of what is politically possible, necessary, and good. Following oil from production, along pipelines, through refineries, and to consumers, this course engages with themes related to the political, economic, environmental, developmental, cultural, and urban geography.

title: ADVANCED ECONOMIC GEOGRAPHY
description: THE LOCATIONS OF VARIOUS TYPES OF FARMING EXTRACTIVE MANUFACTURING AND DISTRIBUTIVE ACTIVITIES AND THE FLOWS OF GOODS BETW                 EEN PARTS OF THE ECONOMY ARE DESCRIBED AND ANALYZED.

title: Urban Geography
description: This course explores the historical growth of cities; spatial structure of commercial, industrial, and public facilities within cities; residential segregation; urban poverty and fiscal distress, and urbanization in the third world.

title: Transport Geography
description: This course offers a review of the literature and techniques for the spatial impacts of transportation systems, including functionality, and their role on society, the economy, energy, the environment, and sustainability.

title: Communications Geography
description: This course examines the geopolitics and space-shrinking effects of telecommunications as well as economic and social impacts of several technologies, including the Internet and cyberspace.

title: Geography of Wine
description: This course discusses the interplay of geographic factors that result in the type and quality of wine. Climate, soil, terrain, latitude, surrounding plants, and the tradition/culture of wine-making techniques determine the unique terroir of a wine region. Wine therefore is tied to place, identified by place, made unique by place, and even made valuable by place. In doing so, the production of wine (viniculture) has made social, economic, political, and cultural impacts around the world.

title: Directed Individual Study
description: May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours.

title: Special Topics in Geography
description: May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours.

title: Honors in the Major Research
description: In this course, students accepted into the Honors in the Major program complete an original research or creative project in their major area of study. This course must be repeated at least twice to complete a minimum of six (6) credit hours total, but may be repeated up to a maximum of twelve credit hours in total.

title: Internship
description: This course provides students with an opportunity to apply skills in supervised situations off-campus. May be repeated to a maximum of six (6) credit hours; repeatable within the same term.

title: Social Theory and Spatial Structures
description: Course examines interrelations of contemporary social theory and political economy with geographic relations.

title: Geographic Visualization
description: This course examines the design and implementation of effective visualization of geographic data, phenomena, patterns, and processes. The theoretical basis is formed by cartography, visual perception and communication models. Emphasis is placed on the creation, analysis, and display of statistical surfaces. Students explore trends in cartographic visualization methods, including interactive and animated mapping techniques. Practical training with one or more commercial software packages is included as well as graphical design exercises.

title: Introduction to Geographic Research
description: This course is a survey of research design and methods, strengths and weaknesses of alternative strategies, reliability and validity measures, and methods of writing.

title: Advanced Remote Sensing
description: This course focuses on quantitative approaches to the analysis of remotely sensed data. Digital multitemporal, multispectral, multisensor remote sensing images acquired by a range of sensors, and the application of digital remote sensing for urban and environmental analysis will be discussed. Quantitative methods in digital remote sensing image enhancement, radiometric normalization, rectification, georeferencing, clasification, thematic information extraction, integration of remote sensing with GIS will also be addressed.

title: GEOGRAPHIC INFORMATION PROCESSING & SYSTEMS
description: ADVANCED SEMINAR ON GIS TOPICS,INCLUDING LOCATIONAL CONTROL, SPATIAL DATA STRUCTURES,SPAT./CART. STATS,MODELING & ANALYSIS& FUTURE TRENDS IN DECISION SUPPORT, SENSORS, & GEOGRAPHIC METHODS.

title: Advanced Geographic Information Systems
description: "Students apply GIS to a problem from their own research or one supplied by a local government agency. Topics include environmental modeling, GIS spatial analysis and visualization."

title: Advanced Geographic Information Science
description: This course examines advanced topics and cutting-edge techniques in Geographic Information Science. Subjects covered include any combination of the following: spatial cognition; geographic representation; spatial pattern analysis; linear modeling; spatial autocorrelation; spatial modeling and simulation; spatial interpolation; digital terrain modeling and visualization; spatial data mining and reasoning; data quality and uncertainty; mobile GIS; Internet GIS; design and implementation; and social dimension of geographic information. The course consists of lectures, lab assignments, readings, and independent research projects.

title: Quantitative Geography
description: This course introduces the use of probability theory and descriptive and inferential statistics in geographic research, including chi-square tests, logit models, correlation techniques, geo-statistics, analysis of variance, simple and multiple regression, and factorial analysis.

title: Geographic Information Systems for Environmental Analysis and Modeling
description: Technical topics covered include space-time variability in environmental data, environmental data acquisition and integration, interpolating environmental data, error and uncertainty, environmental decision support systems, environmental modeling techniques, and the integration of geospatial technologies with environmental modeling systems. Applications include hydrological modeling and watershed analysis, terrain modeling and landform analysis, landscape metrics and landscape pattern analysis, cartographic modeling and land suitability analysis, non-point pollutant modeling, soil erosion modeling, wildfire modeling, and landscape dynamic modeling.

title: SPATIAL DATA ANALYSIS
description: INTRO. TO QUANTITATIVE ANALYSIS OF SPATIAL DATA. INCLUDING   MEA. OF CENTRAL TENDENCY AND DISPERSION, PROB., SAMPLING, STAT. TESTING, CORREL. SPATIAL, POINT PATTERN, TRANSURFACE ANALYSIS,ETC.

title: Soils and Land Forms
description: "Land forms, soils development, and hydraulic processes, and their spatial variations with particular emphasis on the coterminous United States. "

title: Water Resource Analysis
description: "Focuses on water as natural and social resource, models of hydraulic flow, groundwater, water pollution, public planning measures; particular reference is made to water utilization in Florida."

title: Disaster Preparedness and Hazards Mitigation
description: This course deals with natural hazards such as hurricanes and earthquakes and human-made hazards such as nuclear power and air pollution. Students acquire perspectives, tools, and information to choose rationally among publicpolicy alternatives regarding responses to environmental hazards.

title: Human Dimensions of Global Environmental Change
description: Course surveys the multiple ways in which humans have initiated or accelerated changes in the earths' biophysical environment, including population growth, resource depletion, pollution and species destruction. It relates these topics to contemporary geographical theory.

title: Geospatial Data and Analysis
description: This course addresses topics in geographic theory, beginning with the history and social context of the field, including the debates over regions, urbanization, economy, population, development, and the role of markets and nation states. Each theoretical position is tied to method, both in terms of standard practices and critical challenges.

title: Race and Place
description: This course integrates various concepts and topics concerned with the spatial construction and effects of race and ethnicity, including identity, segregation, political and cultural landscapes, and environmental justice.

title: Global Health
description: This course explores and evaluates public health problems and examines global health inequality.

title: Advanced Economic Geography
description: This course is an in-depth examination of several themes in the analysis ofeconomic landscapes, including input-output analysis, historical materialism, post-Fordism, services and telecommunications, and the global economy.

title: World Systems Theory
description: This course focuses on the systematic interrogation of the birth and historical trajectory of the contemporary capitalist world economy, including dependency and modernization theory, and current topics in ethnic conlict and the global economy.

title: LAND USE ANALYSIS AND GROWTH MANAGEMENT
description: THIS COURSE PROVIDES AN INTRODUCTION TO THE SPATIAL ANALYSIS OF LAND USE AND GROWTH MANAGEMENT PROBLEMS.

title: Supervised Research
description: A maximum of three semester hours may apply to the master's degree.

title: Seminar in Current Topics
description: In this course, a variety of subjects are offered on an occasional basis under the heading of "Special Topics." Recent offerings include the Geography of Hunger, Advanced GIS, and Globalization. May be repeated to a maximum of eighteen semester hours.

title: Supervised Teaching
description: A maximum of three hours may apply to the master's degree.

title: Thesis
description: A minimum of six semester hours is required.

title: Professional Development Geography
description: This course reviews procedures for students to assume academic and non-academic employment arising from the attainment of a PhD in Geography.

title: Qualitative Geography
description: This course uses a mix of theoretical and practical approaches to examine the reciprocal relationship between social theory and qualitative research methodology. The course investigates the political and institutional contexts in which geographers conduct their work, and how power relations, researcher positionality, and research ethics inform qualitative research practice.

title: Dissertation
description: Course Description not on file

title: Preliminary Doctoral Examination
description: This course encompasses written and oral portions of the preliminary doctoral exam, for admission to candidacy to the doctoral degree.

title: Master's Comprehensive Examination
description: Course Description not on file

title: Master's Thesis Defense
description: Course Description not on file

title: Dissertation Defense
description: Course Description not on file

title: Elementary Conversational German
description: This course is an introduction to German with emphasis on speaking (conversational method). Additional hours arranged for conversational practice. Students with more than two years of high school German or the equivalent should consult the department for placement. May not be taken by native speakers. May not be taken concurrently with GER 1111, 1120, 1121 and/or 2220.

title: Elementary German I
description: This course is an introduction to German. May not be taken by native speakers. Students with more than two years of high school German or the equivalent should consult the department for placement. May not be taken concurrently with GER 1110, 1111, 1121, or 2220.

title: Elementary German II
description: This course may not be taken by native speakers. Students with three or four years of high school German or the equivalent should consult the department for placement. May not be taken concurrently with GER 1110, 1111, 1120 and/or 2220.

title: Intermediate German
description: This course expands skills in reading, writing, and conversation. This course completes the baccalaureate degree requirement and serves as the transition to upper-level study. May not be taken by native speakers. May not be taken concurrently with GER 1110, 1111, 1120, and/or 1121.

title: INTERMEDIATE GRAMMAR AND COMPOSITION
description: MAY NOT BE TAKEN BY NATIVE SPEAKERS. SECOND YEAR GRAMMAR AND COMPOSITION SKILLS.                                                                                                                       0

title: GERMAN FOR SINGERS I
description: THE COURSE IS DESIGNED TO TEACH MUSIC STUDENTS TO READ;SPEAK AND COMPREHEND GERMAN SYNTAX IN ALL TENSES;MODES AND VOICES                   AS WELL AS GIVE THEN AN ACTIVE VOCABULARY. FOR VOICE MAJORS.

title: GERMAN FOR SINGERS II
description: THE COURSE IS DESIGNED TO TEACH MUSIC STUDENTS TO READ;SPEAK AND COMPREHEND GERMAN SYNTAX IN ALL TENSES;MODES AND VOICES                   AS WELL AS GIVE THEM AN ACTIVE VOCABULARY. FOR VOICE MAJORS.

title: German Grammar
description: This course focuses on the rules of German grammar and syntax and employing them correctly in speaking and writing.

title: German Grammar
description: This course focuses on the rules of German grammar and syntax and employing them correctly in speaking and writing.

title: Composition and Conversation
description: In this course, the objective is the ability to write and converse on general cultural topics at a level which demonstrates near mastery of German grammar and the beginning of a personal style in the language. The course is conducted in German.

title: German Business Language and Practice
description: This course is an introduction to business languages and practices in German-speaking countries.

title: German Studies
description: This course, taught primarily in German, serves as an introduction to German studies. The course provides the student with an understanding of the major cultural aspects (literature, visual arts, history, politics, etc.) of German-speaking countries from the twentieth century to the present; the emphasis is placed on Germany in the second half of the twentieth century.

title: Topics in German Studies
description: This course, taught primarily in German, presents a survey of one topic in the area of German studies. Topics may include themes from art, music, or literature placed in a cultural and historical perspective. Topics change frequently. May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours with permission of instructor.

title: Phonetics
description: In this course, the objectives are the acquisition of correct German sound formation by comparison with English phonetics and the improvement of the students conversational German through pronunciation exercises. The course is conducted in German.

title: Special Topics
description: This course allows students to study non-literary topics of a special kind, depending on student interest and faculty expertise. May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours when content changes.

title: Directed Individual Tutorial Practice
description: Students review grammar and improve their speaking skills by leading groups of students in the GER 1110/1111 classes.

title: Advanced Composition
description: In this course, students gain the ability to write in a professional style, in standard, intermediate, and advanced German, on a variety of topics and in different genres. The course is taught in German.

title: Modern German of the News Media
description: This course is an advanced-level skills course. Discussion of current events and mass media in German-speaking countries and work with authentic texts (newspapers and audio-visual material).

title: Directed Individual Study
description: In this course, students arrange with individual faculty members to undertake specialized study in areas outside of or in addition to the regular curriculum. May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Honors in the Major Research
description: In this course, students accepted into the Honors in the Major program complete an original research or creative project in their major area of study. This course must be repeated at least twice to complete a minimum of six (6) credit hours total, but may be repeated up to a maximum of twelve (12) credit hours in total.

title: Internship in Applied German
description: This course provides academic credit for students working in governmental agencies or private business where students employ the foreign language. Departmental permission required. May be repeated to a maximum of six (6) credit hours; repeatable within the same term.

title: Graduate Reading Knowledge in German
description: This course is designed to present structures of the German language and vocabulary to prepare graduate students majoring in other disciplines to read learned journals, books, and monographs written in German useful to the student's research in humanities, natural or social sciences.

title: Reading Knowledge Examination
description: This translation examination is to ascertain the student's ability to read research materials written in German. Use of translation software is prohibited.

title: Essay Workshop
description: For this course, the objective is the ability to write in German at a level that approximates native use of the language for advanced cultural discourse in general and literary commentary in particular. The workshop setting is designed for collaborative learning through discussions of various styles in existing texts, for the purposes both of recognizing stylistic properties of different types of texts and of selecting styles for the student's own uses, and through collective critiques of the fellow student's writings. The course is conducted in German.

title: CURRENT AFFAIRS
description: GERMAN POLITICAL; ECONOMIC; SOCIAL DEVELOPMENTS ARE STUDIED  THROUGH VARIOUS MEDIA.

title: Studies in German Language and Literature
description: In this course, the topic is determined by the student and the faculty member directing the project. May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours.

title: Teaching Practicum
description: A maximum of three hours may apply to the master's degree. May be repeated to a maximum of five semester hours.

title: Tutorial in Professional Issues
description: This course offers advanced professional preparation to acquaint students with issues of concern in their academic discipline. A maximum of three hours may count towards the degree. May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours.

title: Masterpieces of German Literature in Translation: 19th and 20th Centuries
description: This course offers an introduction to masterpieces of German literature from the 19th century to the present. It uses works by authors of various ethnic, minority, and gender backgrounds that bring forth German representations of gendered or cultural others and transcultural issues. May be counted for major or minor credit. Taught in English.

title: German Cinema
description: This course covers the contextual and stylistic features of German cinema from its classical period in the 1920s, to the New German Cinema, through the present. The course focuses on methods of film analysis and on film criticism. Taught in English. May be repeated to a maximum of six  credit hours. May be repeated within the same term.

title: Translation German-English/English-German
description: This course is an advanced-level skills course. Translating a variety of texts that illustrate important distinctions between German and English grammar, syntax, vocabulary, etc.

title: German Literature in Translation
description: Course Description not on file

title: STUDIES IN A GENRE
description: REPEATABLE - MAXIMUM 6 SEMESTER HOURS. STUDY OF WORKS OF     GERMAN LITERATURE WHICH ARE GROUYPED GENERICALLY AND WHICH   ARE TYPICALLY NOT CONFINED TO ONE PERIOD.

title: Studies in a Theme
description: This course offers students the opportunity to study a recurring theme in German literature and culture (e.g., the Faust theme). The course may be structured around a specific interest of the teacher on topical issues and concerns. May be taken by students not majoring in German who read assigned materials in translation. May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Drama
description: This course focuses on contemporary German drama in a socio-historical context. Addresses the difficulties authors confront when dramatizing current social trends, as well as the problems of interpreting and staging a play.

title: German Short Fiction
description: This course introduces students to the principles of literary study through reading and discussion of short pieces of fiction, primarily from the twentieth century.

title: Modern Life and Letters I: Before 1945
description: Course objective: an understanding of the major cultural aspects (literature, visual arts, history, politics, etc.) of German-speaking countries from about 1900 to 1945, introduced by highlights of the past. The course is conducted in German as much as possible, but the students may use English if necessary. Verbal and written participation is required.

title: Modern Life and Letters II: After 1945
description: The objective is an understanding of the major cultural aspects (literature, visual arts, history, politics, etc.) of German-speaking countries from 1945 to the present. The course is conducted in German as much as possible, but the students may use English if necessary. Verbal and written participation is required.

title: Studies in an Author or Theme
description: This course offers the opportunity to study either a single author in-depth or to follow a specific theme that may extend over a brief period or over centuries. Course material may include non-literary textual and audio-visual material. May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours.

title: Studies in a Period or Movement
description: This course concentrates on a specific literary movement such as Romanticism, Realism, Expressionism, or on a period such as the Baroque, the Enlightenment, or the Weimar period. May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours.

title: Directed Individual Readings in Literature
description: Students arrange w                                                        ith individual faculty members to study literature outside the regular curriculum.

title: Studies in a Genre
description: This course is a study of German literature through genric approaches.

title: Studies in an Author or Movement
description: In this course, either the works of an individual author or a number of authors composing a specific movement are read. Course materials are frequently supplemented with films, videos, and recordings. May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Studies in a Period: Special Topics
description: In this course, an understanding of a certain period or movement investigated is determined by the student's needs and by faculty expertise. May be repeated provided the course materials are different from previous materials presented under the course title. Examples of period literatures are 17th century and Post-World War II literature in a comprehensive approach. Examples of movement literatures are Romanticism and Expressionism, literatures that are concurrent with other types of literature at a given time period. The course is conducted in German. Verbal participation (class discussion and/or reports) and written participation (examination and/or term paper) are required. May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Supervised Research
description: A maximum of three semester hours may apply to the master's degree. May be repeated to a maximum of five semester hours.

title: Topics in Fire Dynamics - Research Seminar
description: This seminar course exposes students to fire dynamics research through a variety of methods. Specific research topics of fire dynamics will be addressed by group discussion of ongoing student research, faculty research, and outside speakers. May be repeated to a maximum of four (4) credit hours.

title: Fire Dynamics Field School
description: This course provides a multidisciplinary, hands-on apprenticeship to the field methods most commonly used in prescribed fire. Students gain a greater appreciation of the complexity of weather and the atmospheric boundary layer and fire as a fluid system, and its dynamics through the active participation in prescribed fire field research and operations.

title: Advanced Topics in Fire Dynamics - Research Seminar
description: This seminar course exposes students to fire dynamics research through a variety of methods. Specific research topics of fire dynamics will be addressed by group discussion of ongoing student research, faculty research, and outside speakers. May be repeated to a maximum of four credit hours.
