description: This course is an introduction to the use of mathematical models in biology. Linear and nonlinear difference and ordinary differential equations, linear stability analysis, phase plane analysis. Applications may include population biology, infectious diseases, chemical kinetics, and physiology.

title: Topics in Applied Mathematics
description: Special topics course. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve semester hours. May be repeated within the same semester.

title: Methods of Applied Mathematics I
description: This course covers continuous and discrete models from physics, chemistry, biology, and engineering are analyzed using perturbation methods, analytical and geometrical tools, and dynamical systems theory.

title: Advanced Actuarial Models, Credibility, and Simulation
description: This course examines claim frequency models, individual loss models, aggregate loss models, multiple-life and multiple-decrement survival models, multiple-state transition models, credibility theory, and simulation.

title: Mathematics for Data Science
description: This course covers the core mathematics of data science: linear algebra, vector calculus and optimization, probability and graph theory. Applications of these topics will be presented and used as motivation.

title: Methods Of Optimal Control
description: This course equips students with foundations of deterministic and stochastic optimal control and teaches them some theoretical and numerical methods of solving optimal control problems. The methods discussed in this course vary from classical methods based on partial differential equations to more novel methods based on deep learning such as policy gradient methods. The students also are prepared to implement the methods during this course.

title: DIFFERENTIAL EQUATIONS I
description: EXISTENCE AND UNIQUENESS OF SOLUTIONS OF SYSTMS OF 1ST ORDER EQUATNS; CAUCHY-KOVALEWSKY THEOREM; 1ST ORD PARTIAL DIFFERNTLEQUAT;CAUCHY PRBL FOR HYPER/PARABOLIC EQTN;DIRICHLET-NEUMANN PRBL ELLPT EQUATN

title: DIFFERENTIAL EQUATIONS II
description: EXISTENCE/UNIQUENESS OF FIRST ORDER EQUATION SOLUTIONS;CAUCHYKOVALEWSKY THEOREMS; THEORY OF 1ST ORDER EQUATNS;CAUCHY PRBLMFOR HYPER/PARABOLIC EQUATNS;DIRICHLET-NEUMANN PRBLMS OF ELLIPTC/EIGENVALUE PRB

title: Finite Element Methods
description: This course covers the methods of weighted residuals, finite element analysis of one and two-dimensional problems, isoparametric elements, time dependent problems, algorithms for parabolic and hyperbolic problems, applications, advanced Galerkin techniques.

title: Finite Element Methods
description: This course covers the methods of weighted residuals, finite element analysis of one and two-dimensional problems, isoparametric elements, time dependent problems, algorithms for parabolic and hyperbolic problems, applications, advanced Galerkin techniques.

title: Complex Variables, Asymptotic Expansions, and Integral Transforms
description: This course covers ordinary differential equations in the complex plane; special functions. Asymptotic methods: Laplaces method, steepest descent, stationary phase, WKB. Integral transforms: Fourier, Laplace, Hankel.

title: Introduction to Fluid Dynamics
description: This course covers the physical properties of viscous fluids, hydrostatics, kinematics of flow fields, governing equations. Dynamics of viscous incompressible fluids: vorticity, boundary layer flow, potential flow.

title: Introduction to Fluid Dynamics
description: This course covers the physical properties of viscous fluids, hydrostatics, kinematics of flow fields, governing equations. Dynamics of viscous incompressible fluids: vorticity, boundary layer flow, potential flow.

title: MATHEMATICS FOR METEOROLOGISTS
description: REVIEW OF VECTOR CALCULUS WITH METEOROLOGICAL APPLICATIONS;  SOLUTIONS OF SIMPLE ORDINARY DIFFERENTIAL EQUATIONS; MATHEMA-TICAL PHYSICS; WAVE EQUATION; POISSON EQUATIONS; DIFFUSION EQUATION.

title: Perturbation Theory
description: This course covers regular and singular perturbation problems; methods of averaging, matched asymptotic expansions, multiple scales, strained coordinates, and WKBJ; applications to ordinary and partial differential equations and fluid dynamics.

title: Mathematical Bioeconomics
description: Biological and economic dynamics of renewable resource management. Optimal control theory. Theory of resource regulation. Growth and aging. Multispecies models. Stochastic models.

title: Introduction to Mathematical Biophysics
description: This course covers the mathematical tools: symbolic and numerical mathematical software packages, matrix computations, rotation matrices, Euclidean motions, lattices, continuous and discrete curves in space, torsion angles, gram and distance matrices, graphs, string matching algorithms, Fourier series, conformal mapping. Applications such as: protein secondary structure; structure determination by crystallography and NMR; writhing, twisting and knotting of DNA; nucleotide and amino acid sequence alignment; brain        mapping.

title: Computational Methods in Biology
description: This course introduces biological topics where mathematical and computational methods are applicable, including discrete and continuous models of biological systems, numerical methods for differential equations, nonlinear differential equations, and stochastic models.

title: Hydrodynamic Stability
description: "Stability of nearly parallel flows; propagation characteristics of instability waves; effects of rotation, thermal, and density stratification on hydrodynamic stability; computation methods. "

title: Wave Propagation Theory
description: This course covers phase and group velocities, dispersion, reflection, characteristics, shock formation, momentum and energy transport, and nonlinear effects. Applications such as acoustics, water waves, internal waves, Rossby waves, and seismic waves. The Korteweg-DeVries equation and solutions.

title: Introduction to Financial Mathematics
description: This course covers partial differential equations, discussion of Brownian motion, Black-Scholes analysis, introduction to measure and probability: financial applications.

title: Introduction to Computational Finance
description: This course covers the computational methods for solving mathematical problems in finance; basic numerical methods, numerical solution of parabolic partial differential equations, including convergence and stability, solution of the Black-Scholes equation, boundary conditions for American options and binomial and random walk methods.

title: Monte Carlo Methods in Financial Mathematics
description: This course examines how the theory of Monte Carlo Methods is developed in the context of topics selected from computational finance, such as pricing exotic derivatives, American option pricing, and estimating sensitivities. The theory includes pseudorandom numbers, generation of random variables, variance reduction techniques, low-discrepancy sequences, and randomized    quasi-Monte Carlo methods.

title: Topics in Applied Mathematics
description: Course Description not on file

title: Advanced Topics in Differential Equations
description: Course Description not on file

title: Advanced Partial Differential Equations I
description: This course introduces the general classical theory of ordinary differential equations, hyperbolic, parabolic, and elliptic partial differential equations. With each case, some fundamental analytical tools are utilized to probe the nature of the corresponding solutions. Main themes of the course include: the existence and uniqueness of solutions for various initial and boundary value problems, and the properties and solutions of the wave equation, the heat equation, and the Laplace and Poisson equations.

title: Advanced Partial Differential Equations II
description: The course in an introduction to functional analytic methods and modern theory in PDEs. Main themes are weak derivatives and Sobolev spaces, some embedding theorems and fundamental inequalities used in the analysis of most PDEs, second order linear elliptic problems (existence of weak solutions, regularity, and eigenfunction expansion), Fourier series and the Galerkin method, applications to linear parabolic equations of second order, distributions, Fourier transform and its applications.

title: Advanced Topics in Hydrodynamics
description: This course covers selected topics such as stability problems, linear and nonlinear theories; regular and singular perturbation techniques. Also offered in the departments of Mathematics, Computer Science, and Meteorology. May be repeated to a maximum of eighteen semester hours.

title: Advanced Topics in Hydrodynamics
description: This course covers selected topics such as stability problems, linear and nonlinear theories; regular and singular perturbation techniques. Also offered in the departments of Mathematics, Computer Science, and Meteorology. May be repeated to a maximum of eighteen semester hours.

title: Advanced Topics in Applied Mathematics
description: Course Description not on file

title: Financial Engineering I
description: This course offers a quantitative treatment of core problems in the investment industry.  Topics include techniques and analysis of active portfolio management including risk factor models and mean-variance optimization, the Martingale approach to derivative pricing for both discrete and continuous models, applied stochastic calculus, and stochastic interest rate models.

title: Advanced Seminar in Applied Mathematics
description: Course Description not on file

title: President's Seminar in Marketing
description: Course Description not on file

title: Basic Marketing Concepts
description: This course is a required prerequisite for all marketing courses. Gives the student an understanding of the decision areas and the ability to utilize marketing concepts to make business decisions.

title: PRICE AND PRODUCT MANAGEMENT
description: A STUDY OF THE POLICIES; PRINCIPLES; AND PRACTICES RELATING  TO PRICING AND PRODUCT MANAGEMENT

title: Retailing Management
description: This course introduces students to the multifaceted world of retail.  Students are exposed to the managerial side of retail principles including pricing strategies, multichannel retailing, consumer behavior, merchandising and customer service.

title: PROMOTIONAL MANAGEMENT
description: Course Description not on file

title: Promotional Management
description: This course focuses on issues related to management of promotional tools including advertising, personal selling, sales promotion, public relations, and publicity.

title: Professional Selling
description: This course addresses the application of behavioral and persuasive communication theories, as well as the techniques necessary to develop effective personal selling skills within organizations.

title: Principles of Purchasing
description: This course is an introduction to the concepts, principles, and techniques of purchasing physical resources for all types of organizations.

title: Consumer Behavior
description: This course provides students the opportunity to acquire knowledge and understanding of consumer behavior. Students learn about the consumer decision making process, what influences purchasing behavior, and the role of products and services in consumers’ lives and society.

title: Sports, Recreation and Entertainment Marketing
description: This course provides students a framework for understanding how marketing strategies and tactics can be successfully applied within sports, and within recreational and entertainment organizations.

title: Innovation and Marketing of Small Business Enterprises
description: This course investigates ways small businesses innovate and market to consumers by focusing on the entrepreneurial creation process and marketing tactics used by small business owners in the role of entrepreneurs. Students learn to understand demands, resource challenges, risks, rewards, as well as how to construct a business plan to guide the operations of a small business.

title: Marketing for Nonprofit Organizations
description: "Marketing techniques as applied to nonprofit organizations, including but not limited to government programs, public agencies, and other not-for-profit organizations."

title: RETAILING MANAGEMENT
description: SENIORS ONLY:A STUDY OF RETAILING INSTITUTIONS; POLICIES; PROCEDURES; LOCATION PRINCIPLES; LAYOUT ORGANIZATION; BUYING;                   PROMOTING; BUDGETING; PRICING; AND RELATED TOPICS.           0

title: Multinational Marketing
description: This course introduces the student to marketing management decision making in international environments through using cases and/or business games.

title: Logistics and Supply Chain Management
description: This course introduces the student to the management of logistics activities involved in the flow of goods, information, and funds throughout the supply chain.

title: Advanced Retailing Management
description: "Formulation of comprehensive, integrated marketing strategies for retail firms in contemporary environments."

title: Social Media Marketing
description: This course introduces the field of social media marketing with a detailed study of the marketing concepts, customer engagement practices, platforms, analytics, and other technologies associated with marketing to customers using social media. Students learn to develop and present social media marketing strategies, including marketing plans and analytics. Students in this course come to understand the relevance and application of social media marketing principles, strategies, analytics, and practices in various business environments.

title: Advanced Strategic Retail Management
description: This course introduces the complex nature of the retail industry, shows how to recognize and manage the many challenges when students begin their career, and examines the many characteristics of leadership and the necessary critical thinking and strategic decision-making skills they need to effectively build and lead teams.

title: MULTINATIONAL MARKETING
description: THE STUDY OF MARKETING POLICIES; PRINCIPLES; STRATEGIES AND  PRACTICES AS FUNCTIONS IN MULTINATIONAL BUSINESS ACTIVITIES

title: ADVANCED PROMOTIONAL STRATEGY
description: NEEDED DEPTH IN PROMOTIONAL MANAGEMENT AND PROMOTIONAL STRAT-EGY; INCLUDES EXPENDITURE ALLOCATION/ DEVELOPMENTAL SELECTION                 OF PROMO STRATEGIES/ MEDIA STRATEGY/                        0

title: Sales Management
description: This course exposes the student to concepts, activities, and analysis pertaining to sales and the management of the sales force.

title: Advanced Sales Techniques
description: This course builds upon and enhances student skills and knowledge developed in the basic professional selling course. Focus is on using a strategic and consultative sales model to develop, manage, and deliver realistic sales presentations.

title: Industrial Marketing
description: This course focuses on those marketing activities involved in the marketing of products and services to organizations that use products and services in the production of consumer or industrial goods and services.

title: Seminar in Purchasing/Materials Management
description: This course covers advanced concepts in purchasing and materials management. Emphasis is on recent research and current practice to achieve strategic sourcing objectives.

title: Marketing Strategy
description: This course teaches students to draw upon and utilize the knowledge and skills developed in marketing and business courses, and integrate the frameworks and analytical tools of marketing strategy that will enable students to develop a cohesive strategy that an organization can execute.

title: Consumer Demand Analytics with Big Data
description: This course is an advanced undergraduate class for mainly business students. However, students from economics, engineering, and other disciplines may find it useful.

title: Marketing Research
description: This course examines marketing research as an information-providing activity for the purpose of management decision making.

title: Advanced Marketing Research
description: This course provides experience in designing and conducting actual marketing research studies.

title: Strategic Sports Marketing
description: This course enables students to conduct strategic analyses relating to the marketing of sports. Topics include a wide range of issues within the realm of professional sports, in an interactive seminar and a workshop-like environment.

title: Electronic Marketing
description: This course examines Internet communication, direct sales through electronic commerce, as well as Internet-based promotional communications.

title: Price and Product Management
description: Familiarizes students with the scope and complexity of the marketing executives product and pricing management problem.

title: Product Innovation Management
description: This course is a structured way of thinking about product development. Students are provided with an up-to-date toolbox for developing and managing new products. The course focuses on hands-on individual assignments, creating aura to stimulate consumer awareness, and a group project to simulate the development process of a new and original product or service.

title: Services Marketing
description: Examination of marketing in the service industries with particular emphasi s on the unique aspect of services marketing, the service marketing mix, and the implementation of service strategies.

title: Customer Relationship Management
description: This course introduces students to the basic theories and terminology of customer relationship management. Special emphasis is placed on customer retention and technological tools for enhancing customer relationships.

title: Directed Individual Study
description: May be repeated to a maximum of twelve semester hours.

title: Marketing Seminar
description: This course covers various topics taught by different instructors each semester. May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Marketing Internship
description: This course is a marketing and sales internship course designed for marketing and sales majors who want to gain real world experience in the marketing and/or sales field through on-the-job practice. Students work under the direction of an approved industry professional, a faculty adviser, and the internship director.

title: Retail Practicum
description: This course focuses on the many career opportunities, job descriptions, and career paths students can follow to achieve success in the retail management field. At the end of this course, students have a clear vision of where they can begin their careers and how to make that happen.

title: Professional Sales Practicum
description: This course is designed for Professional Sales majors who want to gain real-world experience through one of three options: (1) on the job practice, i.e., a sales internship, (2) strategic account management, i.e., a faculty-directed study of sales research techniques, or (3) a professional sales competition, with the permission of the sales program director.

title: Honors in the Major Research
description: In this course, students accepted into the Honors in the Major program complete an original research or creative project in their major area of study. This course must be repeated at least twice to complete a minimum of six (6) credit hours total, but may be repeated up to a maximum of twelve hours in total.

title: Fundamentals of Marketing
description: This course introduces the student to the basics of marketing as a business discipline. It covers a wide variety of topics relevant to the task of managing resources to achieve marketing goals. Successful completion requires learning the vocabulary and concepts which characterize the marketing field and applying them to the development of a marketing strategy. Cannot be applied for credit for any graduate business degree.

title: MARKETING MANAGEMENT
description: COMPREHENSIVE OVERVIEW OF THE MARKETING SYSTEM AND MAJOR ELEMENTS OF MARKETING SYSTEM AND MAJOR ELEMENTS OF MARKETING STRATEGY. EMPHASIS ON THE FACTORS INFLUENCING MANAGERIAL DECISIONS.

title: Business Ethics and Social Responsibility
description: This course focuses on the ethical responsibilities of companies toward all stakeholders in the marketing environment, including owners, employees, customers, and society. Includes a study of ethical decision making and how it overlaps with strategic and tactical decisions in both general business and marketing.

title: Marketing Strategy in the Global Environment
description: This course examines the business-level marketing strategy in the context of global markets and uses the marketing-planning process as a framework for understanding how global environments, markets, and institutions affect the strategic marketing operations of the global business enterprise.

title: PURCHASING AND MATERIALS MANAGEMENT
description: ANALYSIS OF THE FUNCTIONS INVOLVED AND THE VARIABLES NEEDED  TO EFFECT AND CONTROL THE FLOW OF MATERIALS;WITH EMPHASIS ON THE ECONOMIC ENVIRONMENT FOR MATERIALS ACQUISITION AND ALLOCATION FUNCTION

title: Strategic Corporate Communication
description: This course takes an integrated marketing communication approach to the structure and function of corporate communication and its role in managing a corporation's overall reputation. Specifically, this course examines strategic communication planning and how the corporation communicates with its various publics, including consumers, employees, investors, the media, government, and society at large. The course also addresses crisis avoidance and crisis communication planning.

title: Sales Leadership
description: This course focuses on practical and theoretical issues associated with an array of sales leadership activities, including hiring salespeople, desig  ning and implementing training programs, in-field coaching and development , motivating and compensating salespeople, and team building.

title: Business-to-Business Sales and Marketing
description: This course focuses on building and managing relationships with business customers. It covers business-to-business management issues with an emphasis on topics at the mid-to-upper management level. Specific strategic marketing issues include problems and opportunities that leverage an understanding of the entire supply chain. Sales deals primarily with complex, large/key account management and customer relations. Sales management issues concentrate on managing a sales force focused on complex accounts.

title: Strategic Sales Force Management
description: This course focuses on quanitative methods for data analysis and strategic decision making related to sales territory design, sales force organizatioin, compensation plans, forecasting, and key account resource allocations.

title: Purchasing and Supply Chain Management
description: This course provides an understanding of the purchasing/supply management process by analyzing the sourcing strategies, negotiation tools and contract management techniques required to provide organizations with the best value for their purchase expenditures.

title: ADVANCED MARKETING RESEARCH
description: RELATES THE RESEARCH PROCESS TO THE SYSTEMATIC QUALITATIVE /   QUANTITATIVE ANALYSES OF MARKETING DATA DEALING WITH PRODUCTS; SERVICES; PROGRAMS; AND PROPOSALS.                                       0

title: Marketing Research and Analytics
description: This course focuses on the tools, techniques, and procedures involved in the marketing research process, as well as the critical thinking skills necessary to interpret marketing research findings. In addition, the course covers major analytical techniques that are used in a variety of research settings in both marketing and general business.

title: Big Data for Marketing Decision Making
description: This course serves as an introduction to the use of data in making marketing decisions, application of analytics, and the processes required for implementation. In this course, students develop “skills” that are marketable, helps them understand and interpret marketing research reports, and analyze real data from real cases to apply insights to strategic marketing decision-making.

title: Marketing Analytics
description: This course surveys the Marketing Analytics field, reducing the startup cost to using a wide variety of techniques needed by the practicing marketing scientist, and shows how marketing analytic techniques feed into the strategic marketing process and business decision-making in general.

title: MARKET ANALYSIS AND CONTROL
description: SEMINAR STRESSING RELEVANT DEMAND COMPONENTS IN THE COMPREHENSIVE ANALYSIS OF MARKETS.  ACTUAL FIELD EXPERIENCE RESULTS IN PRACTICAL APPLICATION OF CONCEPTS.                                          0

title: MARKETING STRATEGY
description: APPLIED STRATEGIC PLANNING; ANALYSIS AND CONTROL; INDIVIDUAL SITUATION ANALYSIS OF CONSUMER NEEDS/ MARKET POSITION/ MARKET                 ANALYSIS; COMPETETION AND PUBLIC POLICY

title: SEMINAR IN MARKET POLICY
description: AN INTEGRATED AND INTENSIVE EXPLORATION INTO SELECTED/ TIMELY MARKETING TOPICS; PROBLEMS; SOLUTIONS; AND SUBSEQUENT POLICY FORMULATION DERIVED FROM MARKETING KNOWLEDGE.                               0

title: Supply Chain and Marketing in the Digital Age
description: This course focuses on how technology affects the entire supply chain from marketing to suppliers. Students review databases, newer technologies such as 3D printing, blockchain, robotics, drones, automated vehicles etc. along with supporting systems such as procure to pay and other supply chain information systems.

title: Electronic Business in Supply Chain Marketing
description: This course focuses on information technology and how it affects marketing within the supply chain. These include logistical issues and the flow of goods, services and funds within the supply chain to the final consumer. Specific elements covered include: electronic commerce, Internet, intranets, extranets, marketing information systems and logistics information systems.

title: Marketing Management
description: Comprehensive overview of marketing system and major marketing management decision areas. Emphasis on factors influencing managerial decisions.

title: Corporate Affairs Management
description: This course focuses on corporate affairs activities and the strategic use of these activities to market the organization, its issues, and its ideals to potential stakeholders (consumers, general public, shareholders, media, government, etc.) Includes class presentations by corporate executives and extensive class discussion.

title: Service Marketing Management
description: In this course, students are exposed to a new way of thinking about marketing. No longer are manufacturing processes, defects per one-thousand, or logistics paramount. Services are different. The course discusses how they are different, why they are different, and what strategies result from these distinctions. The course covers why people are essential to service success, why expectations are important to service consumers, how the physical environment influences service delivery, and how services firms should recover from failure. Students also learn how quality is evaluated in service firms, why value is an essential deliverable, and what role loyalty has on the bottom line.

title: Special Studies in Management
description: May be taken up to three times, or to a maximum of nine credit hours, whichever is met first; may be repeated within the same term.

title: Supervised Research
description: This course is for masters candidates only. A maximum of three hours may apply towards the master's degree.

title: Special Topics in Marketing
description: This course is an in-depth study of current topics in marketing. May be repeated to a maximum of nine (9) credit hours; repeatable within the same term.

title: Supervised Teaching
description: This course, at a maximum of three semester hours, may apply towards the master's degree.

title: Global Business Seminar
description: This course consists of on-campus class meetings and an international trip to an overseas destination. On-campus meetings help students understand the related international business theories as well as the inhibiting and opportunity-offering roles of local cultures in international business. The international trip is to gain access to the best business practices of world-class multinational firms in the destination city. May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Thesis
description: A minimum of six semester hours credit is required.

title: Seminar in Consumer Behavior Methods
description: This course is an advanced doctoral seminar focused on learning procedures for designing and conducting experimental research.

title: Quantitative Methods I: Measurement, Scaling, and Choice
description: Covers such topics as psychographics, scaling, conjoint measurement, multidimensional scaling, brand switching models, and logit and probit regression. Students will develop an understanding of these measurement techniques and apply these models using empirical data.

title: QUANTITATIVE METHODS II:PSYCHOMETRIC AND ECONOMETRIC APPROACHES TO MARKETING
description: THIS COURSE COVERS SUCH TOPICS AS CONFIRMATORY FACTOR        ANALYSIS; STRTUCTURAL EQUATION MODELS; AND TIME-SERIES MODELS AND THEIR APPLICATION TO MARKETING THEORY AND PRACTICE.

title: Seminar in Marketing: Selected Topics in Consumer Behavior
description: This course is an in-depth analysis of current selected topics in consumer information processing, attitudes, decision making, and social and cultural influences on consumer behavior.

title: SEMINAR IN MARKETING: RESEARCH METHODOLOGY                           CCN CHANGE TO MAR 6979 SUMMER88
description: STUDY OF PHILOSOPHY AND METHODOLOGY OF RESEARCH IN MARKETING. SPECIFIC EMPHASISI IS GIVEN TO EXPERIMENTAL DESIGN; THEORY DEVELOPMENT; MEASUREMENT AND SCALING APPROACHES.

title: Quantitative Methods II: Psychometric and Econometric Approaches to Marketing
description: This course covers the study of confirmatory factor analysis, structural equation models, time-series models, and related topics and their application to marketing theory and practice.

title: Seminar in Marketing Models
description: This course examines the applicability of modeling approaches within marketing contexts. Reviews of the modeling based literature forms the cornerstone of the class, with extensive discussion and analysis. Doctoral standing and consent of instructor are required for admission.

title: ELEMENTS AND INTEGRATION OF MARKETING STRATEGY:SEMNR
description: ANALYSIS OF CONSTRAINTS AND OPTIONS WHEN MANAGING THE MAJOR  ELEMENTS OF MARKETING STRATEGY; OPTIMIZING OPPORTUNITIES;                     GOALS; AND EFFICIENCY                                       0

title: SEMINAR:ENVIRONMENTAL INFLUENCES AND MARKETING      POLICY
description: CRITICAL EXAMINATION OF ENVIRONMENTAL INFLUENCES, MARKETING  POLICY, INCLUDING RESOURCE ALLOCATION, PRODUCTIVITY, PUBLIC  POLICY, AND MULTINATIONAL BUSINESS.

title: Seminar in Marketing Management
description: This course is an exploration of the conceptual foundations and research traditions of marketing research. Emphasis is placed upon reviewing the totality of research contexts and subject matters examined within the marketing discipline. The class format revolves around the critical review of appropriate journal articles. Doctoral standing and consent of instructor are required for admission.

title: Seminar in Marketing: Elements and Integration of Marketing Strategy
description: This course is an analysis of constraints and options when managing the major elements of marketing strategy, as well as optimizing opportunities, goals, and efficiency.

title: Seminar in Marketing: Research Methodology
description: This course focuses on the strategies, theories, and concepts of the supply chain activities in both the business and the international markets.

description: For this course, a minimum of twenty-four semester hours of credit is      required.

title: Applied Linear Algebra I
description: This course covers Gaussian elimination, vector spaces, least squares problems, determinants, eigenvalues and eigenvectors, linear transformations, applications.

description: This course covers groups, permutations and symmetries, rings, integral domains, properties of the integers, fields and rational numbers. Mathematics majors other than FSU-Teach must take MAS 4302 instead.

title: Applied Linear Algebra II
description: This course covers positive definite matrices, matrix computation, linear programming and game theory. Applications.

description: This course covers the Euclidean algorithm; congruencies, quadratic residues, the law of quadratic reciprocity, and an elementary discussion of arithmetic functions and distribution of primes.

description: This course covers groups, permutation groups, subgroups, group homomorphisms, structure of groups, rings, ideals, ring homomorphisms, rings of quotients, polynomials, factorization, fields, field extensions.

description: This course is a continuation of MAS5307.

title: Abstract Algebra I
description: This course covers groups, group mappings; direct products, linear algebras; rings and ring mappings; extensions of rings and fields; factorization theory; groups with operators; Galois theory; structure of fields;           valuations.

description: This course is an intensive study of the structure of one or more of the following algebraic systems: groups, rings, fields.

description: This course includes group and individual activities designed to strengthen knowledge of, and connections among, topics in secondary and college mathematics. description: This course covers the generalities of programs for symbolic computation; programming mathematics; elementary computer algebra: manipulating polynomials, Groebner bases; elementary computer analysis; integration techniques.

title: Microbiology for Health Services
description: This course covers microbiology for students planning careers in the health services, with emphasis on infectious disease, food microbiology, and public  health.

title: Microbiology for the Health Services Laboratory
description: This course covers microbiological techniques including the isolation, typing, and identification of bacteria, properties of pathogenic bacteria, and  food microbiology.

title: PATHOGENIC MICROORGANISMS
description: LECTURE AND LABORATORY STUDIES OF DISEASE PRODUCING MICRO-   ORGANISMS; WITH PARTICULAR EMPHASIS ON BACTERIA.

title: Prokaryotic Biology
description: This course covers structural and functional characteristics of microorganisms, with emphasis on prokaryotes (bacteria and archaea) and viruses. Topics include: prokaryotic cell structure and function, physiology and genetics of prokaryotes and viruses, physiological and molecular aspects of microorganisms and human disease, and biotechnological applications of microbial physiology (environmental, food, and industrial microbiology).

title: Prokaryotic Biology Laboratory
description: This course covers laboratory methods for growth, handling, and study of prokaryotes and other types of microorganisms. Topics include: aseptic technique and isolation of pure cultures; microscopic methods; effects of environment on growth; viruses; physiological characterization methods; and methods related to medical, environmental, and food microbiology.

title: Virology
description: This course covers general virology including virus structure and replication cycles. Students review major families of the bacterial (bacteriophage) plant and animal viruses, with emphasis placed on human viruses and infectious diseases. Students also discuss subviral particles, prions and viroids.

title: Environmental Microbiology
description: The applied aspects of environmental microbiology. Topics include the basic principles of microbial ecology and biotechnological aspects of microbes and the environment. Biomediation of pollutants, biotreatments of liquid and solid wastes, and microbial control of pests.

title: MICROALGAE           PURGE REQUEST 2/19/90
description: A MICROBIOLOGICAL APPROACH TO THE DIVERSITY; STRUCTURE;      EVOLUTION AND ECOLOGY OF MARINE; FRESHWATER AND TERRESTRIAL  MICROALGAE.

title: Selected Topics in Microbiology
description: May be repeated to a maximum of sixteen semester hours.

title: Elementary Medical Spanish I
description: This course introduces medical students with little or no experience with the Spanish language to vocabulary and grammar related to human health and common and diseases. Students begin to develop knowledge and skills important for effective and culturally appropriate communication with Hispanic populations in health care settings. May be repeated to a maximum of three semester hours.

title: Medical Spanish II
description: This course has a special emphasis placed on in-class activities such as   interviewing, history taking, and role-playing of brief patient encounters in medical settings. Students have multiple opportunities to practice theirSpanish with native speakers.

title: Elementary Medical Spanish II
description: Elementary Medical Spanish II builds on language knowledge and skills developed in Elementary Medical Spanish I by extending vocabulary related to human health and common diseases and by increasing proficiency in skills for communication, interviewing, and documentation when working with Spanish-speaking populations.

title: History of Medicine and the Pharmaceutical Industry
description: This course provides an overview of the history of the pharmaceutical industry in the United States. Among other topics, the course examines the historical origins of drug patenting and high drug prices, the origin and rise of clinical drug trials, industry efforts to medicalize ordinary forms of human behavior, and the relationship between industry promotional efforts and medical decision making.

title: Mind-Body Health
description: This course is a ten-week, non-credit elective course designed to improve first- and second-year medical students' health and well-being, by combining yoga and mindfulness techniques with an educational component focusing on scientific research mind-body medicine and neuroscience.

title: Personal Finance for the New Physician
description: This course prepares medical students for the financial aspects of the health care delivery profession, with consideration for resource distribution, revenue generation, contracting, and multiple additional relevant issues. Students benefit from acquiring familiarity with these topics and become better prepared to manage their personal financial life with a sound foundation, post-graduation.

title: Public Health Epidemiology
description: Course Description not on file

title: Health Plan and Medical Management
description: Course Description not on file

title: Quality Improvement and Patient Safety in Internal Medicine
description: The student will examine the role that internists play in measuring, promoting and improving the quality of care. At the completion of the course, the student will be able to examine the relationship between patient safety and good patient care, and discuss the impact of quality improvement initiatives for the practicing physician.

title: Patient Safety in the Physician's Practice
description: Course Description not on file

title: Occupational Medicine
description: This elective exposes students to the practice of occupational medicine in order to emphasize the importance of occupation as a determinant of health status and the opportunity to prevent or alleviate health problems arising from the work environment. May be repeated within the same term to a maximum of eight semester hours. MD grade only.

title: Medical Spanish
description: The goal of the Medical Spanish elective is to expand the student's knowledge of Medical Spanish through a specific variety of topics relating to health matters. It will be necessary for the students to meet with the instructor beforehand to discuss their Spanish background and develop a course of study specifically designed to fit the student's needs. Medical Spanish will be applied to Family and International Medicine, Pediatrics, OB/GYN Medicine..

title: Narrative Medicine: Reflective and Creative Writing
description: In this course, students learn the tenets of narrative medicine and explore the role of narrative in improving clinician understanding of patients and the patient experience. To build narrative skill, students analyze and articulate personal experiences in clinical medicine through the discussion of select stories, poems, and non-fiction medical narratives, and through creative and reflective writing. The primary focus of this course is on writing, story-telling, and communication skills.

title: Implications of Health Policy to the Healthcare System
description: Course Description not on file

title: Literature and Medicine
description: This elective course is designed to be taken via distance learning. Severalfaculty members are collaborating on teaching so as to bring different per spectives. Extensive annotated reading lists are available for each theme. Students may select a theme or propose a new theme for faculty approval.   Students may select readings from the reading list.

title: Creativity and Medicine
description: Physicians blend art and science in the practice of medicine, so it’s no surprise that many doctors have blazed new trails through creative endeavors like literature, art, music, and film. Rather than simply studying the creative work of other physician-artists and authors, this course offers students the opportunity to explore the artistry inherent in the practice of medicine, while fostering their own creative potential in areas ranging from storytelling and poetry to animation, web design, digital imagery or painting, or even game design. May be repeated to a maximum of eight (8) credit hours.

title: Clinical Ethics in Practice
description: Course Description not on file

title: Bioethics on Film
description: Course Description not on file

title: Global Health Elective
description: This 2 or 4 week experience is designed to provide a deeper understanding  and appreciation of the broad field of global (or international) health.   It is composed of a series of readings, online discussion groups and       written assignments.

title: Medical Informatics Elective
description: This clinical informatics elective is offered to provide medical students with some training and exposure to clinical informatics in a large healthcare enterprise with concentration in the areas of the student's interest. The student(s) are mentored by the CMIO (Chief Medical Informatics Officer) of the system.

title: Clinical Teaching: Introduction to Medical Education
description: This course involves the development of a clinical presentation case. Students conduct in-depth research in one particular area of medicine, from the molecular level to the whole individual, exploring the demographics, as well as cultural and socioeconomical impacts of illness, thus strengthening the respective area of knowledge. May be repeated within the same term to a maximum of eight semester hours. MD grade only.

title: Psychosocial Issues in Underserved Populations
description: Course Description not on file

title: Achieving Health Equity - Health Disparities Local and Global
description: This course takes a broad approach to share concepts, current issues, and applications in this field embedded in a life course, person and population-centered perspective. Students will share and grow from each other’s diverse backgrounds and learning skills in an effort to critically appraise literature focused on identifying and addressing health disparities and tangible measures to achieve health equity.

title: Reproductive Endocrinology/Infertility
description: Course Description not on file

title: Primary Care Sports Medicine
description: This experience is designed to provide the student with an intense exposure to the practice of family medicine primary care setting where the physician integrates sports medicine into the practice. This rotation is particularly designed for the student who is seriously investigating a career in primary care with a sports medicine focus. Emphasis will be on the breadth and scope of sports medicine problems seen and managed by the family physician, and the services typically provided by the family physician that practices sports medicine.

title: Urgent Care
description: This course provides students investigating a career in an intense exposure to an urgent care setting by emphasizing the breadth and scope of patient problems seen and managed by family physicians, as well as the medical services typically provided to this population. Students are encouraged to exercise increasing independence in their patient interactions and management decision-making skills. May be repeated to a maximum of eight (8) credit hours.

title: Student Health Elective
description: In this course, fourth year medical students spend five days a week at the FSU Health and Wellness Center seeing patients under the supervision of Student Health Center staff to evaluate, diagnose, and treat common illnesses/injuries and provide health care maintenance for patients who present to the Student Health Center. During this rotation, the medical student sees college students of all ages on a continuity and walk-in basis. This rotation is mostly urgent care medicine for the college student population, including but not limited to: sexual health, mental health, infectious diseases, preventative care and initial care of injuries. May be repeated to a maximum of eight semester hours.

title: Labor and Delivery Sub-Internship
description: This course enables students to work as an integral part of the OB/GYN residency team. Students take one call per rotation week and round on at least 1-2 labor patients and any routine patients that they deliver. Students write notes every two hours while on labor and delivery rounds, and give a fifteen to thirty minute case presentation on the last day of rotation.

title: GYN Surgery Sub-Internship
description: This course enables students to work as an integral part of the OB/GYN residency team. Students take one call per rotation week and round on all GYN patients that they operated on. Students assist on operative cases and may be assigned private attending cases, if desired. Students give a 15-30 minute case presentation on the last day of rotation.

title: Inpatient Medicine
description: This 4-week course allows the student additional learning experience caring for ill adults in an inpatient setting. The student acts as part of the inpatient team and is given progressive autonomy under the direct supervision of the attending faculty. The student gathers data, takes full histories, performs thorough examinations and formulates differential diagnoses, treatment plans and participates in daily rounds, writes progress notes and participates in discharge planning.
