description: This course deals with the theories, methods, and practices of career development; utilizing occupational information and labor market trends; and understanding of requirements and characteristics of a variety of occupations, job analysis, and job modification and restructuring.

title: PSYCHOSOCIAL ASPECTS OF DIVERSITY
description: PSYCHOLOGICAL AND SOCIAL FACTORS RELATED TO ADJUSTMENT AND DIVERE POPULATIONS INCLUDING MINORITIES/WOMEN/PERSON WITH DISABILITIES/OTHER ADULTS. MUST COMPLETE 45 HRS OF COMMUNITY AT APPROVED SITES.

title: Internship in Rehabilitation Services
description: This course is a full or half semester internship in a rehabilitation services setting.

title: Field Experience in Rehabilitation
description: This course is a supervised clinical practice in a rehabilitation agency or community service organization. Duties include such activities as interviewing, assessment, case management, service coordination, and life skills training.

title: Special Topics in Rehabilitation Counseling
description: This course studies special topics in rehabilitation counseling in depth.

title: Medical Aspects of Disability
description: This course offers an introduction to the U.S. medicine structure; a survey of medical specialties and terminology; as well as a survey of body systems, common  malfunctions, therapeutic services, restorative techniques, and disability evaluations.

title: Medical Aspects of Disability
description: This course offers an introduction to the U.S. medicine structure; a survey of medical specialties and terminology; as well as a survey of body systems, common  malfunctions, therapeutic services, restorative techniques, and disability evaluations.

title: Psychosocial and Multicultural Aspects of Disability
description: This course explores the major theoretical perspectives and concepts of adjustment and adaptation to a disability and chronic illness. Social, cultural, and psychological factors of disability are viewed from a life-span perspective.

title: Assessment in Counseling
description: This course offers an understanding of assessment approaches used with counseling clients.

title: Placement Methods and Techniques
description: This course offers an overview of major job placement approaches, including selective, consultative, and job seeking skills models. While application to the employment of disabled persons is emphasized, these methods have implications for other hard-to-employ persons.

title: Principles and Practices in Rehabilitation Counseling
description: This course provides an overview of the history, philosophy, theoretical concepts, intervention strategies, process, and legal ethical aspects of rehabilitation counseling.

title: Administration and Supervision in Rehabilitation
description: This course offers an overview of rehabilitation administration and supervision both in public and private rehabilitation agencies.

title: Leadership Practicum in Rehabilitation
description: This course consists of individualized practicum experience in administration, teaching, or research. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve semester hours.

title: Special Topics in Rehabilitation
description: This course explores emerging issues in rehabilitation counseling, including developments in legislation, research, and policy and professional practice. May be repeated within the same term to a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Advanced Psychological and Social Aspects of Disability
description: This seminar examines the major psychological and social theories related to adjustment and adaptation to a disability or chronic illness with emphasis placed on research, the applicability, and the efficacy of these theories.

title: Advanced Assessment in Rehabilitation Counseling
description: This course explores theories and research related to assessment and evaluation procedures used in various rehabilitation settings. Emphasis is placed on psychological, medical, vocational, and ecological assessment procedures.

title: Advanced Theories and Principles of Rehabilitation
description: This course provides an in depth and comprehensive examination of major approaches to counseling and psychotherapy in relationship to rehabilitation settings and individuals with disabilities.

title: Professional Issues in Rehabilitation Counseling
description: This course facilitates students being knowledgeable and articulate discussants of current issues and trends in the field of rehabilitation. Emphasizes leadership development related to major policy trends and practices in the rehabilitation of persons with disabilities, as well as trends in rehabilitation education and professionalism. May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours.

title: College Prep Reading
description: Course Description not on file

title: COLLEGE PREPARATORY READING
description: TAUGHT BY TCC.  MAY NOT APPLY TOWARD A DEGREE AT FSU         REPEATABLE FOR A MAXIMUM OF 4 HOURS

title: Improving College-Level Reading
description: This course is an individualized program of instruction in critical and comprehensive reading skills. Open to students from all levels and major areas. May be repeated to a maximum of three semester hours.

title: Using Assessments to Inform and Differentiate Reading Instruction
description: This course prepares pre-service teachers to differentiate reading instruction for learners with a range of reading profiles including typically developing learners, English language learners (ELLs), and learners with and at risk for reading disabilities (e.g., dyslexia).

title: Foundations of Reading
description: This course prepares pre-service teachers to teach beginning reading skills to a wide range of learners and to include the foundations of quality reading instruction addressing Florida Reading Endorsement Competencies 1 and 2.

title: READING AND LANGUAGE ARTS INSTRUCTION FOR DIVERSE                                       CULTURES
description: METHODS;MATERIALS/PREPARATION FOR TEACHING READING/LANGUAGE  SKILLS TO CULTURALLY DIFFERENT. EMPHASIS ON READING;SPELLING;                 SKILLS AND ORAL LANGUAGE.                                   0

title: Literacy Across the Content Areas
description: This course introduces pre-service teachers to the role of literacy in the content areas. Students develop the knowledge, skills, and attitudes needed to meet the literacy needs of students.

title: Teaching Reading in Middle/Secondary Schools
description: This course is designed to introduce prospective teachers to developmental and corrective reading practices.

title: Reading for Understanding
description: This course develops in depth knowledge of the underlying processes involved in reading for understanding and addresses several indicators required for the Florida Reading Endorsement (Competencies 1 & 2). This course focuses broadly on developing comprehension, oral language, and fluency skills and builds capacity to implement effective research-based reading instruction to meet the needs of a wide range of learners, including those of varying abilities and English Language Learners.

title: Literacy Assessment
description: This course provides a foundation in assessment with an emphasis on literacy/reading and is required for the Florida Reading Endorsement (Competencies 1-3). Throughout the course, students are taught to select and administer appropriate formal and informal assessments to inform reading instruction that meets the needs of all learners. Grounded in the principles of research-based reading instruction and the Reading Endorsement Guiding Principle that teaching reading for understanding is an ongoing systematic, problem solving process, students will implement and analyze assessments, and select appropriate instruction/interventions based on the collected data.

title: Reading and ESOL Reading Practicum
description: This course is a culminating practicum and addresses several indicators required for the Florida Reading Endorsement (Competencies 4 & 5). In this culminating practicum, students apply their broad knowledge of reading to address the needs of learners with differing reading profiles to develop a comprehensive, scientifically based reading plan for a classroom. The plan includes a method to engage in systematic assessment and problem solving to effectively differentiate instruction. Students apply scientifically-based instructional practices to support all learners. This course emphasizes the principles of research-based reading instruction and the Reading Endorsement Guiding Principle that teaching reading for understanding is an ongoing systematic, problem solving process. Students implement and analyze assessments, select and implement appropriate instruction/intervention based on the collected data, and monitor student progress.

title: The Development and Assessment of Emergent Reading and Writing
description: This course reviews the beginning stages of literacy and ways adults can foster a child's development.

title: Foundations of Developmental Reading
description: This course helps classroom teachers, reading specialists, and other educators seek answers to some of the problems related to reading needs of children of varying abilities.

title: ADVANCED THEORY; CONCEPTS; AND PRINCIPLES IN                                DEVELOPMENTAL READING
description: SPECIAL ATTENTION TO NATURE OF READING PROCESS;ACQUISITION;  CONCEPT-VOCABULARY DEVELOPMENT;  COMPREHENSION; AND LITERARY                  ACQUISITION.

title: Literacy Across the Content Areas
description: This course applies the reading process to the secondary school curriculum. Diagnostic procedures and instructional strategies useful in developing school reading programs. This course introduces students to the role of literacy in the content areas. Educators develop the knowledge, skills, and attitudes needed to meet the literacy needs of students.

title: Teaching Reading to Adult Illiterates
description: This course applies the reading process to ABE curriculum. Provides practitioners, administrators, and researchers with theoretical knowledge related to whole language and literacy education. Practicum included.

title: MULTI-LEVEL MATERIALS FOR READING INSTRUCTION
description: AN INTENSIVE ANALYSIS OF VARIOUS READING MATERIALS; RATIONALE BEHIND MATERIALS; AND REVIEW OF MATERIALS IN USE.

title: Diagnosis of Reading Disabilities
description: This course reviews various types of reading problems and techniques for diagnosing these problems. This course also studies a variety of model diagnostic cases.

title: Correction of Reading Disabilities
description: This course provides teachers, reading specialists, and other educators with theoretical knowledge and expertise related to current procedures and instructional strategies for correcting reading disabilities.

title: Trends and Issues in Reading
description: This course is an exploration of current issues and recent trends in the teaching of reading with emphasis on developmental aspects, present practices, and implications of research in reading.

title: Policy and Leadership in Reading
description: This course examines the role of reading research in state and federal policy initiatives and discusses the challenges and potential solutions to implementing these policy initiatives in schools.

title: Using Literacy Research to Inform Practice
description: This course explores the most current research on what comprises effective literacy instruction, what it means for how we teach, and how to use emerging research to ensure that classroom instruction is as effective as it can be, so that all children have the opportunity to become proficient readers and experience academic success.

title: DESIGNING;IMPLEMENTING;AND EVALUATING READING                                      PROGRAMS
description: COURSE DESIGNED AS A DEPTH STUDY/ANALYSIS OF SUPERVISION AND EVALUATION OF READING PROGRAMS AT VARIOUS ADMINISTRATIVE                      LEVELS.

title: RESEARCH IN READING
description: DESIGNED TO FAMILIARIZE STUDENT WITH MAJOR RESEARCH STUDIES  IN READING;INTRODUCE STUDENT TO RESEARCH TECHNIQUES;TO                        INTERPRET RESEARCH FINDINGS.                                0

title: ASSESSMENT AND TREATMENT OF SEVERE READING                                          DISABILITIES
description: CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS OF READING DIFFICULTIES THAT INVOLVE AN   INTENSIVE/COMPREHENSIVE EXAMINATION/EVALUATION OF FACTORS                     THAT IMPACT UPON THE DISABLED READER.REPEATABLE 3 TIMES MAX 0

title: Leadership Practicum in Reading and Language Arts
description: This practicum is designed to provide individualized practicum experiences in educational agencies for advanced graduate students in reading and language arts.

title: SEMINAR IN READING/LANGUAGE ARTS
description: DISCUSS/REVIEW LITERATURE RELATING TO VARIETY OF TOPICS IN   READING/LANGUAGE ARTS. TOPICS WILL VARY FROM TERM TO TERM.                    REPEATABLE 6 HOURS MAXIMUM.                                 0

title: Seminar and Practicum in Reading and Language Arts
description: This course is designed to provide field-based experience in public setting in conjunction with an on-campus seminar. Core readings are discussed.

title: Theory and Research in Reading
description: This course helps students develop broad knowledge of the research in reading and the ability to critically analyze and interpret studies in the field of reading.

title: Reading Research Related to Adult Literacy
description: This course is designed to provide administrators, researchers, and practitioners with knowledge and expertise relating to the field of adult literacy. Current literature and data sources, research models, agencies and organizations, and literacy programs will all be investigated.

title: Doctoral Seminar in Reading and Language Arts
description: This course provides doctoral students with knowledge on current trends and issues in the field of reading education. Specifically, the seminar includes consideration of theoretical and empirical perspectives; integration of research, practice, and policy; and multi-disciplinary perspectives. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve semester hours.

title: Real Estate
description: This course is a survey introduction to real estate, real estate evaluation, and real estate investment decision making. The course, in addition to REE 4433, meets the FREC educational requirement for real estate sales licensing.

title: Real Estate Valuation
description: This course acquaints students with the valuation process and the basics of valuation terminology. It also demonstrates the application of a variety of valuation techniques to both residential and income properties.

title: Real Estate Market Analysis
description: This course includes topics such as techniques of real estate market analysis, survey research, and applications of computers to real estate problems.

title: Real Estate Finance
description: This course is an intermediate treatment of real estate finance, investment, and tax analysis. Coverage includes mortgage markets, financing devices, and quantitative evaluation of real estate projects.

title: Real Estate Investment
description: This course introduces students to the analytical tools and procedures used to evaluate real estate investments.  The course focuses on the topic of real estate investment analysis, primarily from the private investors' perspective.

title: Legal Environment of Real Estate
description: This course is an intermediate treatment of the legal environment of real estate and real estate decision making. The course emphasizes common law rules and legal considerations inherent in contemporary real property decisions. The course, in addition to REE 3043, meets the FREC educational requirements for real estate sales licensing.

title: Real Estate Internship
description: This internship is designed for College of Business students who desire to gain real-world experience in the real estate field through on-the-job practice. Students work under the direction of an approved industry professional, a faculty advisor, and the internship director. S/U grade only.

title: Honors in the Major Research
description: In this course, students accepted into the Honors in the Major program complete an original research or creative project in their major area of study. This course must be repeated at least twice to complete a minimum of six (6) credit hours total but may be repeated up to a maximum of twelve credit hours in total.

title: The Real Estate Process
description: Introduction to general principles of real estate as both a product and a process; relationship of real estate system to basic functional areas of business.

title: Topics in Real Estate Finance
description: This course provides an advanced treatment of topics fundamental to real estate finance. The course topics are presented in units that range from pricing mortgages to the lending process to the secondary mortgage market. Topics include a discussion of primary and secondary mortgage markets, mortgage market operations, mortgage instruments and mortgage-related securities.  Emerging topics of special interest are also discussed.

title: Advanced Real Estate Finance and Investment
description: This course addresses advanced topics in real estate finance and investment. Attention is given to the role of institutional investors, advisors, and real estate investment trusts (REITs). The course provides an in-depth treatment of financing income-producing properties, including coverage of the commercial mortgage underwriting process. Students are introduced to real estate investment analysis at the property portfolio level. The topics covered are of importance to real estate analysts, lenders, asset managers, appraisers, and brokers involved with income-producing real estate.

title: REAL ESTATE PROJECT FEASIBILITY ANALYSIS                                                        CHG
description: INTRODUCTION TO REAL ESTATE DECISION-MAKING PROCESS FOR      DETERMINATION OF REAL ESTATE SITE USE OR INVESTMENT BEING    USED DEALT WITH OR PURSUED.

title: Real Estate Project Feasibility Analysis
description: This course introduces students to real estate decision-making process for determination of real estate site use or investment being used, dealt with , or pursued.

title: Real Estate and Its Legal Environment
description: This course presents an overview of the real estate markets and the laws affecting land use. This course provides an advanced treatment of the legal environment of real estate, including those issues related to property ownership and its transfer, and the contracts applied in the acquisition, operation, and disposition of property.

title: Special Topics in Real Estate
description: This course is an in-depth study of current topics in real estate.

title: Cinematic Courage
description: This course is an examination of virtue through the films and selected readings with special attention to how courage is developed and displayed in response to various situations from its traditional context in the military, to areas of resistance against unjust racial and gender arrangements. Specific manifestations of military courage, religious courage as exemplified in martyrdom, political courage and professional courage are examined.

title: Introduction to World Religions
description: This course is a survey of the major living religious traditions from all regions of the world, with attention to their origins in the ancient world and their classic beliefs and practices. Students will be introduced to foundational texts from the major world religions including selections from the Western canon.

title: President's Seminar
description: Course Description not on file

title: Religion in the United States
description: This course examines the scope and nature of religious movements, trends, and figures in American religious history, with an emphasis on the role that religious groups and institutions have played in conceptions of America and formations of American identity.

title: Culture Wars
description: The class focuses on studying the historical development of the “culture wars” from the emergence of the concept in the early 1990s to its contemporary relevance in American political, social, and cultural discourse.

title: Religion and Society
description: This course is a survey of how major social and political theorists in the Western canon have interpreted religious phenomena.

title: Introduction to the Old Testament
description: The history, religious though, and social institutions of ancient Israel as reflected primarily in its literature. Includes selections from the Western canon.

title: The Lost Books of the Bible
description: In this course, students will engage and confront the “lost books of the Bible”—a corpus of texts that are in the Old Testament in the Catholic and Eastern Orthodox traditions but today are generally not printed in Protestant Bibles. Many American Christians are not familiar with these texts, even though they were in all Christian forms of the Bible for well over a thousand years.

title: Introduction to the New Testament
description: This course is an introduction to the writings of the New Testament in the context of the historical development of early Christianity.

title: Apocalypse Now and Then
description: In this course, students analyze and engage the apocalyptic tradition, as expressed in Judaism, Christianity and Islam, in order to better understand apocalyptic worldviews and how they developed in response to historical and cultural factors in antiquity. The course focuses on apocalyptic traditions in non-Western cultures and the contemporary world.

title: Religions of South Asia
description: This course studies the history and culture of the religious traditions of South Asia. A study of the manifestations of Hinduism, Buddhism, Jainism, Islam, Sikhism, and Christianity in India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Sri Lanka.

title: Religions of East Asia
description: This course is an introduction to the history, thought, and practice of religion in China, Korea, and Japan. Confucianism, Taoism, Buddhism, and popular religious traditions from ancient through modern times are covered.

title: Demons, the Antichrist, and Satan
description: This course examines traditions regarding demons, the Antichrist and Satan in the Bible, Judaism and Christianity. Biblical and ancient non-biblical texts that describe these figures are examined in their historical contexts. Traditions regarding Satan and other evil personages are traced historically so that students have a sense of how an understanding of these figures changed over time.

title: RELIGION AND THE ARTS
description: PHENOMENOLOGICAL ANALYSIS OF HISTORICAL;SOCIOLOGICAL;        PSYCHOLOGICAL RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN RELIGION AND THE ARTS;               SIMILARITIES AS INVOLVED IN CREATION;EMBODIMENT;DISCLOSURE;DIALOGUE

title: Religion and 20th Century Fantasy Literature
description: This course offers an overview of theological and anti-theological elements in twentieth and twenty-first century fantasy literature from authors Lewis, Tolkien, Rowling, and Pullman.

title: RELIGION AND BIOGRAPHY
description: EXAMINATION OF WAYS IN WHICH BIOGRAPHY SERVES AS A PRIMARY   MODE OF RELIGIOUS AND ETHICAL EXPRESSION.  INTRODUCE STUDENT                  TO DISCIPLINE OF JOURNAL KEEPING AS EXPRESSIONS OF PIETY.

title: Topics in Religion in the Americas
description: May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours. May be repeated within the same term.

title: Religious Intolerance in America
description: This course examines the long and ongoing history of religious intolerance in America with respect to a wide range of religious groups and agnostic/atheistic persons, and with an eye to the ways in which religious intolerance intersects with race, ethnicity, class, and gender.

title: Gender and Religion
description: This course considers the impact of gender on religion. Includes cross-cultural studies, theoretical works, and gender issues within religious traditions.

title: Gender and the Bible
description: "This course will examine female figures and images of the Bible, while surveying the various approaches to feminist analysis. Special attention will be given to the production of knowledge about gender in biblical history."

title: Religion, Race and Ethnicity
description: This course examines the relation between race, ethnicity and religious beliefs in a cross-cultural context.

title: Psychology in American Religious History
description: This course explores the psychological aspects of religious life in five different religious traditions in the United States. It examines the cultural experiences and social structures that have shaped psychological approaches to religion in the U.S. in the 20th and 21st centuries.

title: Religion and Science
description: This course provides an historical and philsophical analysis of major questions in the relationship between religion and science.

title: RELIGION AND SCIENCE
description: STUDY OF HISTORY;METHODS;THEORIES OF SCIENCE AND RELIGION    NOTING POINTS OF CONFLICT;MUTUAL LIMITATIONS;STRENGTHS;                  METHODOLOGICAL SIMILARITIES;DISSIMILARITIES;COMMON INVOLVEMENTS.

title: Religion and Science
description: This course provides an historical and philsophical analysis of major questions in the relationship between religion and science.

title: Religious Ethics and Moral Problems
description: This course discusses contemporary moral problems such as deception, sexual activities and relations, and capital punishment from the standpoints of major religious traditions.

title: Topics in Ethics
description: This course considers themes and problems in modern ethics. May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours. May be repeated within the same semester.

title: FOUNDATION OF RELIGIOUS ETHICS
description: INTRODUCTION TO ETHICAL DECISION MAKING WITHIN A RELIGIOUS   CONTEXT; SPECIAL EMPHASIS ON KEY CONCEPTUAL/METHODOLOGICAL             TOOLS OF ETHICAL ANALYSIS;EXAMINATIONS OF TYPES OF RELIGIOUS ETHICS.

title: RELIGION AND PUBLIC POLICY
description: EXPLORATION OF APPLICATION OF RELIGIOUS MORAL NORMS TO       ETHICAL ANALYSIS OF PUBLIC POLICY ISSUES.

title: Religion and Law
description: This course is a survey of how American jurisprudence about the First Amendment has understood religion, attending to the specific legal and ethical arguments made in the US Supreme Court opinions and how they intersect with philosophical accounts of the meaning of freedom.

title: Religion and Bioethics
description: The course offers an introduction to theoretical and practical issues in bioethics from the perspective of a variety of religious and secular positions.

title: DEATH AND DYING
description: PRACTICAL AND THEORETICAL PERSPECTIVES ON CRITICAL DIMENSIONSOF DEATH IN MODERN SOCIETY;DEFINITION OF DEATH;DETERMINATION                  OF DEATH;PROBLEMS/PERSONS FACING DEATH;EUTHANASIA;SUICIDE.  0

title: THE HOLOCAUST
description: ORIGINS;PROCESS;CONSEQUENCES OF DESTRUCTION OF EUROPEAN JEWS DURING WW II.EMPHASIS ON LONG RANGE RELIGIOUS/ SOCIAL/ THE                    POLITICAL AND HISTORICAL FACTORS THAT LED TO THE HOLOCAUST

title: The Holocaust
description: This course examines the origins, the process, and the consequences of the destruction of the European Jews during World War II.

title: The Dead Sea Scrolls
description: This course examines key manuscripts of the Qumran corpus and focuses on issues such as the history, beliefs, and praxis of the Jewish sectarian movement that is associated with the scrolls; the archaeology of the Qumran site; and the significance of the scrolls for understanding Second Temple Judaism.

title: The Hebrew Prophets
description: This course analyzes the prophetic books of the Hebrew Bible/Old Testament: Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekel, and the twelve minor prophets. The course examines the role of prophecy elsewhere in the Hebrew Bible (as in the Elijah stories) and situates the biblical prophets within the broader context of prophecy, as a religious and social phenomenon in the ancient Near East.

title: THE BIBLE AND CONTEMPORARY SOCIAL ISSUES
description: EXAMINATION OF VARIOUS CONTEMPORARY SOCIAL ISSUES IN THE     LIGHT OF AN EVALUATION OF GERMANE BIBLICAL TRADITIONS.

title: Biblical Archaeology
description: Course introduces archaeological methods and excavation results in the region of Syro-Palestine. Archaeological data is viewed alongside narrative reports in biblical literature.

title: Topics in Biblical Studies
description: This course focuses on selected topics dealing with biblical writings in their ancient historical contexts and/or their interpretation in later period. May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours.

title: Religions of the Greek and Roman World
description: This course introduces students to the religions of the ancient Mediterranean world from the rise of classical Athens to the late Roman empire. Specific emphasis is placed on the gods, rituals, and institutions of Greek and Roman city-states, new religious movements, and ancient mythic and philosophical elaboration on the nature of religion.

title: RELIGIONS OF WESTERN ANTIQUITY
description: RELIGIONS OF MESOPOTAMIA;EGYPT;GRAECO-ROMAN WORLD FROM       ANCIENT SUMER TO LATE ROMAN EMPIRE. ATTENTION GIVEN TO                TRADITIONS INTERACTED /ANCIENT ISRAELITE/EARLY CHRISTIAN COMMUNITIES.

title: RELIGIOUS TRADITIONS OF EAST ASIA
description: SURVEY OF THE RELIGIOUS TRADITIONS OF EAST ASIA; INCLUDING   TAOISM; CONFUCIANISM; FAMILY AND CIVIL RELIGION; SHINTO; WITH SOME ATTENTION TO THE VARIETIES OF EAST ASIAN BUDDHISM. OFFERED ALTERNATE YRS

title: THE BUDDHIST TRADITION
description: A SURVEY OF THE BUDDHIST TRADITION FROM ITS BEGINNINGS       THROUGH THE MODERN PERIOD.  SOME ATTENTION TO ITS CON-                        TEMPORARY FORMS.  OFFERED IN ALTERNATE YEARS.

title: Ramayana in Indian Culture and Beyond
description: This course is an introduction to the Hindu tradition through the Ramayana, one of its most popular and celebrated sacred texts.

title: RELIGIOUS TRADITIONS OF INDIA
description: INTRODUCTION TO HINDU AND MUSLIM RELIGIOUS TRADITIONS,       HISTORY AND PRACTICE.

title: Goddesses, Women, and Power in Hinduism
description: This course studies female power in Hindu cosmology, mythology, and society. A study of Hindu goddesses, women, and female symbolism and the multifaceted relationship among them.

title: The Buddhist Tradition
description: This course surveys the Buddhist tradition from its beginnings through the modern period. Some attention to its contemporary forms.

title: Chinese Buddhism
description: "A study of the history, thought, and practice of Chinese Buddhism, through readings in Tiantai, Huayan, Chan, and Pure Land materials."

title: HISTORY OF RELIGIONS:INDIA AND ISLAM
description: INTRODUCTION TO HINDU AND MUSLIM RELIGIOUS TRADITIONS;       HISTORY AND PRACTICE.

title: Chan/Zen Buddhism
description: This course focuses on Chan, a school of Chinese Buddhism popularly known in Japanese as "Zen". The course surveys Zen both historically and thematically, from its beginnings through the modern period. Topics include Chan's origins, history, doctrine, ethical beliefs, meditation, ritual, and monastic institutions.

title: Buddhist Ethics
description: This course examines the nature of Buddhist ethics within historical and evolving Buddhist traditions. The first part of the course explores how Buddhist scriptures articulate prescriptive, doctrinal ideas (e.g., cosmology, the self, soteriology, etc.). The second part of the course examines ethical issues thematically (e.g., compassion, compassionate killing, suicides, gender, environment, food, etc.).

title: Buddhism and the Mythology of Evil
description: The central question this course addresses is: "Does Buddhism have a concept of evil?" Students examine traditional Buddhist interpretations of delusion, sin, and misfortune, and they explore the multiple and shifting representations of the "demonic" in textual, historical, and social contexts.

title: Buddhist Meditation
description: This course examines the theories and practices of Buddhist meditation in different Buddhist traditions. The course is both historical and experiential. Students explore how Buddhists articulate prescriptive, doctrinal ideas in relation to descriptive meditation experiences. The course includes class discussions, experiential practices, and readings. No previous background necessary.

title: Japanese Religions
description: This course investigates the influence of Japanese religious traditions on Japanese life, culture, and history; as well as the influence of history and politics on modem Japanese religiosity.

title: Tibetan and Himalayan Religions
description: This course is an historical and thematic survey of the religions of Tibet and the Himalayas, including Nepal, Bhutan, and Sikkim. The course emphasizes significant facets of this region's rich cultural heritage, including religion, literature, art, and politics.

title: Islamic Traditions
description: This course provides a historical and topical survey of Islam as a religion and civilization, focusing on the formative and classical periods of its history. The course is primarily concerned with the life and career of Muhammad, the Prophet of Islam; the scriptural sources of Islam (i.e., the Qur'an and the Sunna); and the development of the Muslim community and its principal institutions (schools of thought, law, theology, cultural life, and mystical traditions).

title: Islamic Traditions II: Islam up to the Modern World
description: This course examines Islam and its adherents from 1300 CE to the present,  concentrating on the last two centuries of Islamic history: the period of reform, renewal, and revolution in the wake of Western political and cultural domination. This course investigates a basic question: What happened to different Muslim communities and intellectuals (specifically those in the Arab world, Iran, Turkey, and Africa) as they responded to the challenges posted by "Westernization" and "modernization?" Moreover, it explores the relatively new phenomenon of Islam in America.

title: Religion in Africa
description: This course examines the variety and complexity of religious practices and beliefs on the African continent, and in particular how African discourses of religion challenge our most fundamental understandings of the term religion.

title: Issues and Thinkers in Western Religious Thought
description: This course is an introduction to the Western tradition of religious thought as illustrated by the writings of some of its greatest representatives. Readings in such primary sources as Augustine, Dante, Erasmus, Luther, Pascal, Hegel, and Kierkegaard.

title: Critics of Religion
description: This course is an introduction to the major thinkers and texts in the critique of religion as it developed in the 19th and 20th centuries in the west. Beginning with Schleiermacher, the course moves on to consider the so-called "masters of suspicion"--Feuerbach, Marx, Nietzsche, and Freud. By means of a close examination of central texts, students explore the meaning of a critique of religion, the structure of religious consciousness, the place of religion with respect to other forms of culture, the problem of religion and alienation, and the possibility of a critical faith.

title: New Religious Movements
description: This course investigates the role of new religious movements (NRMs) in American culture and history.

title: Religion, Prisons & Abolition
description: This course makes two claims: 1) Without religious ideas and practices, prisons as we know them would be unthinkable; and 2) Religious ideas and practices are essential for building a world without prisons. Using historical, geographic, ethnographic, and narrative methods to uncover histories of religion, prisons, and abolition in the U.S., students will imagine more just ways of doing justice.

title: Religion and Global Human Rights
description: In this course, students explore human rights across various cultural and historical contexts, examining sources for human flourishing beyond traditional declarations. The course also discusses the promises and limitations of these precedents and the bodies that enforce human rights at national and global levels.

title: Religion and Ecology
description: This course explores the ways in which participants in different religious traditions and in different ecological zones understand and interact with the natural environment. In particular, it investigates the symbolic and lived expressions of these interactions in diverse religious texts, ethics, and practices.

title: The Christian Tradition
description: This course studies the major beliefs, practices, and institutional forms of Christianity in historical perspective.

title: Christians Through Roman Eyes
description: This course examines ancient Greek and Roman perspectives on the early Christian movement. Students read and interpret a range of ancient sources and assess the historical and methodological issues these sources raise for understanding the origins of Christianity under the Roman Empire.

title: American Protestant Thought in Historical Context
description: This course traces the historical development of American protestant thought by examining the writings of influential American protestant thinkers from different time periods, and by considering the social and intellectual forces that influenced their differing conceptions of Christan life.

title: The Jewish Tradition
description: "A survey of the varieties of institutional structures, beliefs, and religious practices of post-biblical Judaism in their historical contexts."

title: Jewish Ethics
description: This course is an introduction to how the Jewish tradition has approached, and can approach, ethical issues. Course focus includes both the moral judgments that Jewish ethicists make about various issues and the methods by which they come to make those judgments.

title: Topics in Buddhism
description: This course focuses on selected topics and themes in the academic study of Buddhism. The course may explore key subjects and theories in Buddhist studies, including philosophy, history, sociology, anthropology, literature, and art history intended to introduce students to the diversity of Buddhist traditions throughout Asia, Europe, and North America and to help them develop critical skills necessary for evaluating a variety of scholarly approaches to the subject. May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours. May be repeated within the same semester.

title: Special Topics in Religion
description: Topic, theme, and historical period in the study of religion vary by instructor. May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours.

title: What Is Religion? What Is Religious Studies?
description: This course is a survey of how theorists in the modern era have answered questions about the origin, essence, and function of religion, as well as an examination of the methods by which religion is studied in a scholarly environment.

title: UNDERGRADUATE SEMINAR:RELIGION AND CULTURE
description: PROBLEMS AND ISSUES IN RELIGION AND CULTURE. TOPICS VARY.    ADVANCED UNDERGRADUATE STUDENTS.PERMISSION OF THE INSTRUCTOR                  REQUIRED.REPEATABLE FOR MAXIMUM OF 9 HOURS.

title: Undergraduate Religion and Culture Seminar
description: This course focuses on problems and issues in religion and culture. Topics vary. Intended for advanced undergraduate students. Permission of the instructor required. May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours. May be repeated within the same term.

title: Readings in Classical Hebrew Texts
description: This course consists of intensive work on specific religious texts in classical Hebrew (ancient or medieval). Choice of texts vary. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve semester hours.

title: The Book of Genesis: Literary and Historical Approaches
description: This course offers a close and critical reading of the Book of Genesis in terms of its composition, history of its interpretations, its Near Eastern context, its narrative artistry, as well as its relevance for ethics and theology.

title: Judaism in the Graeco-Roman World
description: This course studies the history of the Jews and the development of Jewish religious ideas, literature, institutions, and practices from the Maccabean Revolt to the redaction of the Babylonian Talmud.

title: Undergraduate Biblical Studies Seminar
description: This course consists of advanced work in biblical studies for undergraduates. Topics vary. May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours. May be repeated within the same semester.

title: Undergraduate History of Religions Seminar
description: This course studies problems and issues in the history of religions. Topics vary. Intended for advanced undergraduate students. May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours.

title: Tutorial in Greek Religious Texts
description: "Selected readings in Greek of Jewish, Christian and other religious texts from the ancient world. A basic knowledge of Greek grammar is presumed.

title: Religions of the Graeco-Roman World
description: This course studies the religions of the Graeco-Roman world with special emphasis on traditional religious forms, mystery religions, and developments in philosophy. Some attention is given to Judaism, Christianity, and Gnosticism in their broader social, cultural, and historical contexts.

title: Religions of the Ancient Near East
description: "The religious traditions of the ancient Near East, including Egypt, Mesopotamia, and Syria/Palestine, from earliest historical times to the onset of the Hellenistic age."

title: RELIGIONS OF WESTERN ANTIGUITY
description: RELIGIONS OF MESOPOTAMIA EGYPT AND THE GRAECO-ROMAN WORLD FROM ANCIENT SUMNER TO THE LATE ROMAN EMPIRE. ATTENT GIVEN TO TRADITIONS INTERACTED W/ANCIENT ISREALITES & EARLY CHRIST. REPEAT 6 SEM HRS.

title: Modern Hinduism
description: This course studies selected topics on the Hindu tradition in 19th and 20th century India. Includes modern Hindu thinkers, reform movements, popular religion, Hindu nationalism, and pluralism. Attention also to Hindu-inspired religious movements outside India and to other topics of student interest.

title: Seminar in Indian Buddhist Philosophy
description: "A seminar exploring Buddhist philosophy from the time of Buddha through the medieval Buddhist logicians. Selections from the Nikayas, Nagarjuna, Yogacara, and Buddhist epistemologists will be studied."

title: Seminar in Taoism
description: "A seminar investigating the primary texts, central ideas and history of the Lao-Chuang Taoist tradition."

title: Classical Tibetan
description: This course is a systematic and comprehensive study of basic literary Tibetan grammar, common locution, and translation devices. Emphasis will be on exposure to a variety of styles and genres in Tibetan religious literature including Buddhist texts on philosophy, ritual and history. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve semester hours.

title: Special Topics in Asian Religions
description: This course focuses on selected topics and themes in the academic study of Asian religions with special emphasis on issues of methodology.  Topics may include key theories in Asian studies, religion, philosophy, history, sociology, and anthropology intended to help students develop critical skills.

title: Seminar on Shi'ite Islam
description: This seminar examines the historical development of the Shi'a, including but not limited to Ithna-'Ashari ("12") Shi'ism, Fatimid Isma'ilism, and contemporary Shi'ite issues.

title: Islam in North America
description: This course surveys in seminar format the manifestations of Islam in the United States, as well as American perceptions of Islam and Muslims.

title: TWENTIETH CENTURY WESTERN RELIGIOUS THOUGHT
description: INTRODUCE MAJOR PERSONS;ISSUES AND PROBLEMS OF WESTERN       RELIGIOUS THOUGHT IN TWENTIETH CENTURY.EMPHASIS ON EUROPE AND                 NORTH AMERICA.

title: Death and the Afterlife in Buddhist Cultures
description: This seminar explores death, dying, and the afterlife as Asian Buddhists do. Although Buddhist teachings stress mortality and impermanence, rites for the dead preoccupy Buddhist ritualists, theologians, and lay people alike. Local religious cultures also shape such Buddhist ideas and practices. The seminar format stresses active and in-depth discussion of readings and intensive writing projects.

title: Buddhism and Violence
description: Buddhism appears as a pacifist religion of compassion, mindfulness, and nonviolence. Yet, violence, conflict, and the use and abuse of power have played prominent roles in Buddhist doctrines, practices, and social history in Asia. This course explores Buddhism and violence both thematically and regionally. Its seminar format involves active discussion of readings and intensive written work.

title: Undergraduate Religious Thought Seminar
description: Topics vary. Intended for advanced undergraduate students. May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours.

title: Christianity after the New Testament
description: This course covers major developments in the history and theology of Christianity in the first three centuries of the Common Era.

title: Christianity in Late Antiquity
description: This course studies Christian thought, institutions, lifestyles, and literature in their social, cultural, and historical contexts from the time of Jesus to the early Middle Ages.

title: Modern Protestantism
description: The development of the Protestant traditions in the modern era. The course will emphasize the innovative responses made in Protestant thought and practice to the key developments in culture and society.

title: Modern Roman Catholicism
description: This course studies the Catholic Church from the Council of Trent to the present day; special consideration given to Vatican II, current problems, and leading thinkers.

title: Modern Judaism
description: This course studies the development of Judaism as a religious and cultural phenomenon in Europe, North America, and the Middle East from the Europeanh Enlightenment to the birth of the State of Israel.

title: Gender and Judaism
description: Examines the roles of men and women in various Jewish communities and the responses of contemporary Jews to feminist initiatives and critiques.

title: Directed Individual Study
description: This course consists of supervised reading and research on selected topics.

title: Tutorial in Pali
description: This course studies the grammar, vocabulary and style of the Pali canon to better understand both Buddhist philosophical concepts and the culture of Bancient Buddhist India.

title: Tutorial in Sanskrit Texts
description: This course consists of readings in Sanskrit of selected religious texts. Topics vary. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve semester hours.

title: Tutorial in Classical Chinese Religious Texts
description: Readings in Chinese of selected religious texts. Topics will vary.

title: Tutorial in Latin Religious Texts
description: This course consists of readings in Latin of selected religious texts. Topics vary. A basic knowledge of Latin grammar is presumed. May be repeated to a maximum of twelve semester hours.

title: Honors in the Major Research
description: In this course, students accepted into the Honors in the Major program complete an original research or creative project in their major area of study. This course must be repeated at least twice to complete a minimum of six (6) credit hours total, but may be repeated up to a maximum of twelve credit hours in total.

title: SEMINAR:INTRODUCTION TO THE STUDY OF RELIGION
description: GRADUATE INTRODUCTION TO HISTORY;PRESENT STATUS;PRINCIPLE    ISSUES;METHODOLOGIES IN ACADEMIC STUDY OF RELIGION.

title: SEMINAR: RELIGION AND CULTURE
description: TOPICS VARY.                                                 REPEATABLE MAXIMUM NINE SEMESTER HOURS.

title: Tutorial in Near Eastern Languages and Literature
description: Readings of selected religious texts in Semitic languages such as Akkadian, Ugaritic and Aramaic. The languages studied and course content will vary by semester. Previous work in a Semitic language is presumed.

title: WESTERN RELIGIOUS THOUGHT: THE TWENTIETH CENTURY
description: MAJOR MEN AND MOVEMENTS IN WESTERN RELIGIOUS THOUGHT FROM    1900-1960.

title: Christianity in Early Modern Europe
description: "This course examines the thought, movements, lifestyles, and literature of European Christians from the 14th century to the 17th."

title: Religion in the Classroom
description: This course trains graduate students in the Department of Religion to be effective teaching assistants and instructors. It addresses three areas that are relevant to the teaching of religion: the logistics, design, and execution of courses. As such, it introduces students to a range of pedagogical issues surrounding the teaching of religion at academic institutions.

title: Seminar: Introduction to the Study of Religion
description: This course is a graduate introduction to the history, present status, principal isues, and methodologies in the academic study of religion.

title: Seminar: Religion and Culture
description: Course Description not on file

title: Seminar: Biblical Studies
description: Course Description not on file

title: Seminar: History of Religions
description: Course Description not on file

title: Tantric Buddhism
description: This seminar examines the historical development of Tantra and Esoteric Buddhism (Mantranaya, Vajrayana) in early medieval India, with some attention to the spread and practice of Tantric/Esoteric traditions in Nepal, Tibet, China, and Japan.

title: Seminar: Chinese Buddhism
description: This course looks at Chinese Buddhism by way of social and cultural practice; examining the institutional, ritual, and doctrinal components for the construction of Buddhist values, roles and identities within the larger field of Chinese religious life. Special consideration is given to the symbolics of religious alterity, especially as they apply to the negotiation between Buddhist and non-Buddhist traditions.

title: Readings in Tibetan Religious Texts
description: This course is a seminar that covers selected primary-source readings in Tibetan language about the religious history of Tibet. Readings are drawn from a variety of historical periods and genres, including history, biography, Buddhist canonical texts, philosophical treatises, ritual manuals, poetry, and epic narrative. The course also introduces students to various tools and methods for the study of classical and modern Tibetan literature.

title: American Conservatism and the Religious Right
description: This intensive seminar investigates the history of American conservatism and its relationship to the development of the "New Religious Right."

title: Religious Thought in America
description: This course studies the classic theological traditions in American religion from Puritanism to contemporary theology. Emphasis will be on Protestant thought, but attention is given to representative Roman Catholic and Jewish thinkers.

title: Seminar: Religious Thought
description: Course Description not on file

title: Supervised Research
description: A maximum of three hours may apply to the master's degree.

title: Departmental Colloquium
description: A departmental colloquium for the presentation and discussion of student, faculty, and visitor work-in-progress.

title: Supervised Teaching
description: A maximum of three hours may apply to the master's degree.

title: Thesis
description: A minimum of six semester hours is required.

title: Seminar: Ethics and Politics
description: This course is a seminar in ethics and politics that encourages research into the relationships between religion, morality, and the social-political life of persons and groups.

title: Seminar: Scriptures and Interpretation
description: This course is a seminar in scriptures and interpretation that encourages research in selected aspects of the interpretation of sacred texts in a particular tradition or traditions.

title: Seminar: Religious Movements and Institutions
description: This course is a seminar in religious movements and institutions that encourages research in selected religious movements and institutions in a religious tradition.

title: Readings for Examination
description: This course is designed for graduate students who have completed all of their required coursework and are preparing for their examinations.

title: Dissertation
description: Course Description not on file

title: Preliminary Doctoral Examination
description: May be repeated in the same semester.

title: Master's Comprehensive Examination
description: Course Description not on file

title: Master's Thesis Defense
description: Course Description not on file

title: Dissertation Defense
description: May be repeated in the same semester.

title: Practice of Risk Management
description: This course reflects the most current developments in risk management.

title: Principles of Risk Management
description: This course is a survey of the general principles of risk management and their role in business.

title: Personal Insurance Planning
description: This course is an introduction to personal risk exposures. The course integrates life, health, property, liability, private, and governmental programs.

title: Personal Lines Insurance
description: This course provides a thorough review of personal lines insurance principles and exposures.

title: Benefits
description: This course reflects the most current developments in insurance benefits.

title: Personal and Business Property Insurance
description: This course provides an overview of property risks and coverages. Insurer operations are discussed in detail. Social problems associated with the risks are discussed as well as the impact of inland marine, transportation, and multi-peril coverages.

title: Commercial Property Insurance
description: This course provides a thorough review of commercial property principles and exposures.

title: Commercial Casualty Insurance
description: This course provides a thorough review of commercial liability principles and exposures.

title: Analysis of Risk Management
description: This course covers the concepts relating to the analysis of risk.

title: Risk in Business and Society
description: This course is designed to enhance student understanding of risk and its implications for individuals, business, and society. The course focuses on the impact of uncertainty on decisions and the risk-reward tradeoff.  Students analyze the implications of risk in a variety of settings.

title: Risk Financing
description: This course reflects the most current developments in risk financing.

title: Risk Control
description: This course reflects the most current developments in Risk Control.

title: Introduction to Risk Management and Insurance
description: This course is an introduction to the principles, practices, and economics of insurance. The focus of the course is the relationship of fire, life and casualty contracts to business and contingency risks.

title: Agency Management
description: This course reflects the most current techniques and theories for agency management.

title: Risk Management/Insurance
description: This course is an introduction to the principles of risk management and insurance and their application to personal and business pure risk problems.

title: RISK MANAGEMENT AND INSURANCE
description: THEORETICAL AND CONCEPTUAL FRAMEWORK TO IDENTIFY/ MEASURE; AND TREAT PURE  RISK SITUATIONS.  ALSO; ANALYSIS OF THE RISK DECISION PROCESS AND USES OF  INSURANCE AND OTHER TOOLS FOR RISK TREATMENT.

title: Lifecycle Risk Management
description: This course focuses on the fundamental objectives of personal risk management. Students conduct an in-depth analysis of the risk transfer mechanisms used to protect against economic losses caused by health risks, death, disability, long term healthcare needs, and excessive longevity

title: EMPLOYEE BENEFIT PLANS
description: STUDY OF BASIC CONCEPTS AND MANAGERIAL CONCERNS UNDERLYING     GROUP INSURANCE MECHANISM/  THE CHARACTERISTICS OF VARIOUS   RETIREMENT PLANNING VEHICLES (BOTH QUALIFIED AND NON-QUALIFIED).           0

title: Employee Benefit Plans
description: This course studies basic concepts and managerial concerns underlying the group insurance mechanism and the characteristics of various qualified retirement planning vehicles.

title: LIFE AND HEALTH INSURER OPERATIONS
description: A STUDY OF THE ACTUARIAL AND OTHER FUNCTIONAL ASPECTS OF LIFE INSURER OPERATIONS--RATE MAKING; RESERVES; UNDERWRITING; AND FINANCIAL STATEMENT ANALYSIS.                                               0

title: PROPERTY AND CASUALTY INSURER OPERATIONS
description: A DISCUSSION OF THE COMPOSITION; FINANCIAL STRUCTURE AND OPERATIONS OF THE INSURANCE INDUSTRY. SPECIAL CONSIDERATION IS                   PROBLEMS AND SOLUTIONS OF CONSUMERS

title: Property and Casualty Insurance Products
description: This course analyzes more common basic insurance contracts-their use and coverage afforded as a fundamental basis for understanding legal, underwriting, marketing, financial, and other insurance functions.

title: PROPERTY AND CASUALTY  INSURANCE PRODUCTS
description: ANALYSIS OF MORE COMMON BASIC INSURANCE CONTRACTS-THEIR USE /   COVERAGE AFFORDED AS A FUNDAMENTAL BASIS FOR UNDERSTANDING LEGAL; UNDERWRITING; MARKETING; FINANCIAL; AND OTHER INSURANCE FUNCTIONS.

title: Insurance Data Analytics
description: This course focuses on the use of data and analytics tools in the insurance industry. Students develop a set of tools for presenting and analyzing data, explore sources of data, and consider the range of applications for the data that is collected throughout the industry.

title: Property and Casualty Insurer Operations
description: This course discusses the composition, financial structure, and operations of the insurance industry. Special consideration is given to consumer problems and solutions.

title: Advanced Property and Casualty Insurance
description: This course studies business insurance problem evaluation and planning with proposed solutions utilizing comprehensive coverage package program.

title: Applied Learning in Risk Management and Insurance
description: This course provides an in depth exploration into specific risk management and insurance topics of current interest. Within these topics, students are encouraged to explore some aspect of human experience with a focus on at least one source of diversity. Topics vary over time.

title: SEMINAR IN RISK AND ITS CONTROL
description: PREREQUISITE:  CONSENT OF INSTRUCTOR.                        REPEATABLE UP TO 6 HOURS                                                                                                                  0

title: Seminar in Risk and Its Control
description: Topics vary. May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Commercial Risk Management
description: This course studies the application of the risk management process. Includes risk control, risk financing, and business risk management problems.

title: Legal and Political Aspects of Insurance
description: This course studies insurance contracts and marketing-judicial doctrines of contract construction, claims processes, insurance institutions, governmental regulation, and sponsorship of insurance.

title: ESTATE PLANNING
description: ANALYSIS OF THE CONCEPTS AND TOOLS FOR ESTATE/ TAX PLANNING: EMPHASIZING THE NATURE/ VALUATION/ DISPOSITION/ADMINISTRATION                 AND TAXATION OF PROPERTY                                    0

title: Risk Management and Insurance Internship
description: This course is designed for Risk Management Insurance majors to gain real world experience in the Risk Management Insurance field through on-the-job practice. Students work under the direction of an approved industry professional, a faculty advisor and the internship director.

title: Insurance Fundamentals
description: The course covers the fundamental concepts of using insurance to manage risks, understand exposures, and to understand insurance contracts.

title: INSURANCE: FOUNDATIONS; PRACTICES; AND PRODUCTS
description: A STUDY OF THE CONTRIBUTIONS OF OTHER DISCIPLINES TO THE     INSURANCE INDUSTRY AND ITS PRODUCTS WITH EMPHASIS ON THE     RISK SHARING AND RISK TRANSFER FUNCTIONS.

title: Fundamentals of Risk and Insurance
description: This course develops concepts such as time value of money, statistical analysis, information technology, and management of risk exposure. Topics include risk fundamentals, risk management, insurer operations, and insurance regulation.

title: Alternative Risk Financing
description: This course evaluates how corporations and insurance companies finance risk. The course covers the basic financial tools that are used in risk financing, the traditional and alternative risk financing techniques corporations use, and the unique risk financing techniques used by insurance companies.

title: International Risk Management
description: The course provides an overview of risk management in a global economy. Students discuss the complexities and opportunities of risk in a global environment. The course builds on prior knowledge of risk assessment, control and financing tools, and applies them to multinational and international settings, as well as exploring trends and issues in global risk management.

title: Property/Liability Insurance Contract Analysis
description: This course analyzes basic commercial property and liability insurance contracts, including commercial property, commercial general liability, crime, inland marine, boiler and machinery, commercial auto and farm policies.

title: Data Analytics in Risk Management and Insurance
description: This course focuses on the use of data and analytical tools in the insurance industry. Students develop tools for analyzing the types of data used by insurers across various functions including loss estimation, loss reserving, underwriting, and claims.

title: Risk Management in the Business Enterprise
description: This course studies the application of the risk management process, including risk control and risk financing techniques, to business risk management problems.

title: REGULATION AND MANAGEMENT OF THE INSURANCE ENTERPRISE
description: EFFECTS OF THE REGULATORY ENVIRONMENT ON THE OPERATIOS OF    INSURANCE COMPANIES WITH EMPHASIS ON CURRENT PROBLEMS AND                     RELATED POLICY DECISIONS.

title: Insurance Company Operations
description: This course covers the fundamentals of risk, the management of pure risk, insurance mechanisms, insurer operations and the evolution of risk management.

title: Insurance Accounting and Finance
description: This course is a survey of accounting and finance, financial statement analysis, and statutory requirements for insurance companies.

title: Healthcare Financing
description: This introduces the financing of health insurance and benefits plans. The course will cover forms of employer-sponsored coverage, including self-funded and fully-insured managed-care plans, and understanding the implications of private versus public forms of insurance coverage.

title: Personal Financial Planning
description: This course analyzes loss exposures facing individuals and families, basic personal-lines property-liability insurance (auto and homeowners), individual life, health and disability insurance, and individual/family financial planning.

title: Supervised Research
description: For master's candidates only. A maximum of three hours may apply towards the master's degree.

title: Special Topics in Risk Management and Insurance
description: This course evaluates how corporations and insurance companies finance risk. May be repeated to a maximum of nine (9) credit hours; repeatable within the same term.

title: Supervised Teaching
description: A maximum of three hours may apply to the master's degree. May be repeated to a maximum of five credit hours; may be repeated within the same term.

title: Thesis
description: A minimum of six semester hours is required.

title: Doctoral Seminar in Insurance: Life/Health Insurance Topics
description: This course reviews current literature and theory in life/health insurance, including product development, management and regulation of life insurance companies, and the place of life insurance companies in the capital markets.

title: Doctoral Seminar in Insurance: Property/Liability Insurance Topics
description: This course reviews current literature and theory in property/liability insurance, including product development, management and regulation of property/liability insurance companies, and the place of property/liability insurance companies in the capital markets.

title: Doctoral Seminar in Risk and Insurance Theory
description: This course reviews literature in the theoretical foundations of risk and insurance, including the concept of risk, contributions from other disciplines, determinants of insurance consumption and risk management decisions, and industry dynamics.

title: Dissertation
description: A minimum of twenty-four semester hours is required.

title: Doctoral Preliminary Examination
description: Course Description not on file

title: Master's Comprehensive Examination
description: Course Description not on file

title: Master's Thesis Defense
description: Course Description not on file

title: Dissertation Defense Examination
description: Course Description not on file

title: DOCTORAL PRELIMINARY EXAMINATION
description: Course Description not on file

title: DISSERTATION DEFENSE EXAMINATION
description: Course Description not on file

title: Media Techniques
description: This course introduces students to basic principles and terminology associated with the aesthetics of film making and television production.

title: Elements of Broadcasting
description: This course surveys American broadcasting from the standpoint of communicators, organizations, content, and audience with special emphasis on research.

title: WRITING FOR THE ELECTRONIC MEDIA
description: FORMS OF WRITING FOR RADIO AND TELEVISION;INCLUDING ANNOUNCE-MENTS;BROADCAST CONTINUITY;AND NARRATIVE DRAMA               .

title: Writing for the Electronic Media
description: This course teaches non-fiction writing for recently evolved electronic media and fosters an understanding of the theory and practice of writing for those media. Students create content for television, radio, social media, blogs, podcasts, vlogs, webisodes and other forms of electronic media.

title: Narrative Writing for Television and Film
description: This course consists of the development and writing of fictional scripts for television and film.

title: AUDIO PRODUCTION
description: A LECTURE-RESEARCH-LABORATORY COURSE IN THE FUNDAMENTALS;TECHNIQUES;& AESTHETICS OF SOUND RECORDING & PRODUCTION OF FOR   .                RADION; TELEVISION & FILM.

title: Single-Camera Video Production
description: "Course addresses direction and production of single-camera video projects including camera, audio, lighting and linear editing."

title: Multiple Camera Studio Production
description: Direction and production of multiple-camera studio video projects includin g camera, lighting, audio and live-to-tape switching.

title: TELEVISION INTERVIEWING AND HOSTING
description: LECTURE-LABORATORY. ON-CAMERA INTERVIEWING AND HOSTING OF NEW AND PUBLIC AFFAIRS PROGRAMS, INCLUDING RESEARCH AND WRITING COMPONENTS.

title: Directing Performance for the Camera
description: Students will direct student actors in a workshop setting. Actors and directors will gain experience with and appreciation for those on the other side of the camera. Lecture-laboratory.

title: Television Production
description: This course consists of the fundamentals of studio and field production including camera, audio, lighting, and production planning using the crew system.

title: Digital Media Production Workshop
description: This course consists of advanced editing and post production techniques applied to field and studio projects. Emphasis on digital non-linear editing systems.

title: Television Interviewing and Hosting
description: This course introduces students to on-camera interviewing and hosting of news and public affairs programs including research and writing components.

title: 3D Video Animation
description: This course covers the techniques used to prepare, create, and post-produce 3D graphics and animation with video.

title: Video Workshop
description: This course is designed for students to gain experience in the production of television programs and video projects. May be repeated to a maximum of three semester hours.

title: Television Workshop for Non-Majors
description: May not be applied toward communication major. A course designed for students to gain experience in the production of television programs and video projects.

title: Radio Workshop
description: This course consists of general staff work in any department of the campus  student-run radio station. Designed to afford both entry-level training and advanced application of skills. Students must work three hours per week  for one hour of credit. May be repeated to a maximum of three semester hours.

title: Radio Practicum
description: This course consists of radio work and day-to-day broadcast operations with an emphasis on practical application in either of two areas: management or other advanced roles at the student radio station; or special individual projects in the application, study, or research pertaining to radio broadcasting. May be repeated to a maximum of nine credit hours.

title: INTRODUCTION TO BROADCAST TECHNOLOGY
description: TECHNICAL INFORMATION OF SOUND SYSTEMS USED IN BROADCASTING  WITH LABORATORY DEMONSTRATION AND PRACTICE

title: TELEVISION PRODUCTION
description: INTRODUCTION TO PRACTICES OF PRODUCTION INCLUDING CAMERA AND LIGHTING APPROACHES AND PRODUCTION PLANNING AND EXECUTION THROUGH THE CREW SYSTEM.

title: Advocacy Video Theory and Practice
description: This course explores the theory and practice of short-form video production. In addition, the course examines the social media distribution possibilities for these videos.

title: INFORMATION AND DOCUMENTARY ON FILM AND TV
description: EXAMINATION OF THE EVOLUTION OF DOCUMENTARY (FILM AND TV)    FROM 1895 TO THE PRESENT.

title: Documentary Video Production
description: This course offers instruction in the theory and practice of production of non-fiction documentary video. Students produce a final video product after studying the documentary tradition, theory, and history.

title: Immersive Documentary Production
description: This course explores the ways that immersive media is being used to tell reality-based stories. From journalistic 360 video content developed by news organizations to virtual reality experiences addressing social issues, immersive media has become a site of documentary media production. Students develop working prototypes of immersive documentaries.

title: Television Practicum
description: This course consists of producing and directing television programs and video projects. May be repeated to a maximum of nine semester hours.

title: Electronic Media Audience Measurement
description: This course provides an understanding of past and current methods of measuring radio, TV and cable audiences; terms and formulas used by the ratings services; the local market radio (Arbitron) and TV (Nielsen) reports; how ratings and other audience estimates are utilized in radio/TV/cable programming and sales; the newest developments in audience measurement; customized and qualitative approaches to audience measurement; and computer applications widely used in the broadcasting and advertising industries.

title: Advanced Post Production
description: This course is a hands-on introduction to the craft of editing for digital video. This course covers a broad range of post-production topics, including compression and codecs, video editing, basic motion graphics, color correction, audio editing, and exporting. This course includes instruction in industry-standard software, and is also balanced with exploration of the aesthetics and various theories of editing.

title: Advanced Lighting & Cinematography
description: This course focuses on advanced lighting and cinematography theory, techniques, and aesthetics. Students gain practical experience by producing original content over the course of the semester.

title: Immersive Video Production
description: This course explores a range of new camera technology and software that allows for the post-production of immersive media, and identifies best practices for producing, shooting, editing and displaying immersive video products.

title: Advanced Narrative Production
description: This course includes original student narratives produced through writing, pre-production, and post-production stages.

title: Advanced Feature Production
description: This course is a "professional experience" course designed to give students professional production experience in an educational environment. Students act as the production crew on program features. This includes videography, editing, audio, and graphic design.

title: Advanced Feature Reporting
description: This is a professional course designed to give students scriptwriting, producing, voiceover and on-camera experience in an educational environment, while promoting FSU through creative and informative visual storytelling. Students produce feature content that may be used on FSU Departmental and CCI Websites, Seminoles.com, FSU Athletics Team Web Sites, Social Media channels, and student-produced TV programs.

title: Broadcast Operations & Management
description: This course explores the purpose, function, organization, and management of broadcast operations with an emphasis on advanced application, understanding, and skills-building.

title: Radio-Television Station Operation and Programming
description: "Purpose, function, organization, and programming of the radio and television station with an emphasis on research."

title: Los Angeles Television Experience
description: This course examines the TV and film industry in Los Angeles including the industry structure, production cycles, the studios, the networks, and the writers. The course takes place in Los Angeles and also addresses professional career paths in the Los Angeles entertainment industry.

title: Senior Thesis in Production
description: This culminating course for students in production enables individual students, focusing on areas they have mastered in intermediate courses, to produce senior thesis video projects.

title: New Communication Technology: Theory and Research
description: Survey of key concepts and theoretical approaches in research on new communication technology.

title: New Communication Technology
description: This course surveys key issues related to new communication technologies within a variety of communication contexts. Specific topics vary as new technologies emerge.

title: New Communication Technology
description: This course surveys key issues related to new communication technologies within a variety of communication contexts. Specific topics vary as new technologies emerge.

title: Communication Regulation and Policy
description: This course studies laws, regulations and policies for broadcasting, cable, telephone, and computer-communication industries.

title: MEDIA MANAGEMENT
description: MANAGEMENT OF ELECTRONIC MEDIA WITH AN EMPHASIS ON BROADCAST RESEARCH APPLICATIONS

title: Advanced Seminar in New Communication Technologies
description: This course is a doctoral-level seminar in the use of new communication technologies for information and entertainment. May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours.

title: Elementary Russian I
description: This course introduces students to basic Russian. Students with high school language experience or equivalent should consult the department for placement. May not be taken by native speakers. May not be taken concurrently with RUS 1121 and/or 2220.

title: Elementary Russian II

description: This multimedia course offers a thorough overview of grammar and basic cultural literacy. Language structures are studied through popular fiction and film genres. Students produce a short film in Russian.

title: Advanced Russian to English Translation

title: Advanced Russian Conversation and Composition
description: This course focuses on the styles and levels of oral expression on a wide range of topics. May be repeated to a maximum of six semester hours.

description: This course provides an understanding of the phonetic and phonemic structure of Russian with extensive oral practice.

title: History of the Russian Literary Language

title: Special Topics
